    Proposal runtime-types

    Stage 0 Draft / July 24, 2026

    Runtime Types

    Introduction

    This proposal adds an optional static type system to ECMAScript. Its types are values at run time and are enforced there: a typed binding rejects a value outside its type, a conversion between numeric types is written explicitly rather than performed silently, and the type of a value is available through reflection. Annotations are optional and additive, so a program that does not use them keeps its current meaning.

    The system is deliberately close to the value-type discipline of systems languages. It adds sized integers, floats, and decimals; value-type classes with a defined in-memory layout; operator overloading; generics specialized by monomorphization; and types as first-class, interned values that ordinary code can compute with and reflect on. None of this is visible to a program that does not opt in.

    This document is the normative specification. It is written against the design, which, together with the rationale, the full set of extensions, and worked examples, is maintained in the design repository. Readers new to the proposal should start there; this document formalizes what that material describes.

    Note

    The specification is under active construction. The clauses below establish the shape of the document; normative text is added phase by phase, following the specification plan in the design repository. Until a section is filled in here, the corresponding behaviour is defined only by the design documents.

    1 Runtime Types

    This clause and its subclauses define the runtime type system as a set of additions to and revisions of the ECMAScript specification. The work is organized into the phases described in the design repository's specification plan: the static type-checking framework; the grammar of type expressions; the type universe; conversions; runtime enforcement; typed declarations, operators, functions, and classes; the type-object and reflection API; and the numeric library. Extensions (SIMD, generics, decorators, primitive metadata, and the rest) are specified as independent additions layered on this core.

    Each addition is expressed as an insertion into or revision of the edition of ECMAScript that 1.1 pins, so that this document reads as a patch against that specification rather than a restatement of it. Clauses appear in the order of the underlying ECMAScript specification, cross-referenced to the sections they modify, and the extension clauses follow the core, each of them named in Table 23.

    1.1 Conventions

    This document is a diff. It is written against ECMA-262, 16th edition (ES2025), which is the pinned edition, and it should be re-pinned and re-diffed at each subsequent edition rather than left to drift. Within a grammar block, a production shown with inserted text is that edition's production with the marked additions; a production shown entirely as an insertion is new to this proposal. A cross-reference whose target is not defined here is a reference to the pinned edition and resolves against it. Where a later clause extends a production an earlier clause has already patched, the later block restates the production in full, marking only its own additions as inserted; the blocks are one production, and the latest restatement is its complete form.

    The design is the collection of documents in the ecmascript-types repository: a README specifying the core and a set of extension documents specifying SIMD, generics, decorators, primitive metadata, memory layout, type programming, and the rest. This specification is that design rendered as a patch against the pinned edition. A note that cites the design is pointing at the source of a requirement, at a case the design works through, or at a question this specification decided and the design should record; as the proposal advances, each such citation should become a link to an issue in that repository, so that a reader can follow a decision to the discussion that settled it.

    Three conventions govern the semantics. The relations of the type system, SameType, IsSubtype, IsAssignable, and the operations over Type Records, are abstract operations rather than syntax-directed operations, because they relate types to types and not Parse Nodes to values; the syntax-directed part of the type system is the Static Type of an expression (1.2.4), which each clause that adds an expression form extends. The static semantics that attach to a production are Early Errors, and this document states one with the phrase “it is a type error if”, which 1.2.6 defines as an Early Error rather than a thrown error; they are stated at the rule they belong to rather than gathered into one block, because a type error is a condition on types and reads where the types are. A thrown TypeError or RangeError is reserved for a check that could not be resolved statically.

    Note

    The editorial state of this document is worth stating plainly. Its structure, cross-references, and grammar are complete and it builds clean, and every obligation the design records is either specified here or named in Table 23 as belonging to an extension. What it has not had is the multi-editor review that turns a complete specification into an approved one, and the decisions it records, marked as such in the notes, are this specification's answers rather than the design's, offered so that a reader can disagree with a stated position instead of guessing at an unstated one.

    1.2 Type System Foundations

    This clause defines the machinery the rest of the specification builds on: the representation of a type, the structural identity that interns types into unique values, the subtyping and assignability relations, the static type of an expression, compile-time evaluation of a type expression, and the reporting of type errors. It introduces no user-facing syntax; the productions and library that use this machinery are added by later clauses.

    Note

    ECMAScript defines runtime behaviour through abstract operations and a limited set of static semantics, chiefly Early Errors, but it has no framework for assigning a type to an expression or relating two types. This clause establishes that framework. Because a type in this proposal is also a value, a single notion, the Type Object, serves both as the entity the type checker reasons about and as the value a program observes.

    1.2.1 Types and Type Objects

    A Type Record is a Record used by this specification to describe an ECMAScript type. Each Type Record has a [[Kind]] field and the additional fields listed for that kind in Table 1.

    Note 1

    The structured fields referenced above, a Property Type Record, an Index Signature Record, a Signature Record, and a Tuple Element Record, are defined by the clauses that introduce object types, function types, and arrays and tuples. This clause depends only on Type Records being comparable and canonicalizable, which the operations below provide for every kind.

    A Type Object is an ECMAScript object that denotes a type. Every Type Object has a [[TypeRecord]] internal slot whose value is a canonical Type Record (1.2.2.5). A type name or type expression in expression position evaluates to a Type Object, and reflection returns one.

    Type Objects are interned. 1.2.2.6 maps each canonical Type Record to a unique Type Object, so two Type Objects denote the same type if and only if they are the same value. Consequently comparing two Type Objects with IsStrictlyEqual returns true exactly when they denote the same type, and a Type Object may be used as a property key or a key in a Map or Set to key on a type.

    Note 2

    The observable behaviour of a Type Object, including how it exposes its own structure to reflection, is defined by 1.11. The remainder of the type system uses only its [[TypeRecord]] and its identity.

    1.2.2 Structural Identity

    Two types are the same when they have the same structure, with one exception: a nominal type is identified by its declaration, so two classes with identical members are still different types. This clause defines that notion and the canonical form that makes it decidable and internable. A pair of Type Records is carried through the recursive comparisons as a List so that comparison of a recursive type terminates; each such pair is represented as a Record with a [[First]] field and a [[Second]] field.

    1.2.2.1 SameType ( s, t )

    The abstract operation SameType takes arguments s (a Type Record) and t (a Type Record) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether s and t denote the same type. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. Return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s, t, « »).

    1.2.2.2 SameTypeWithAssumptions ( s, t, assumptions )

    The abstract operation SameTypeWithAssumptions takes arguments s (a Type Record), t (a Type Record), and assumptions (a List of Records that have [[First]] and [[Second]] fields) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether s and t denote the same type, treating each pair in assumptions as already established so that comparison of recursive types terminates. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If s and t are the same Record, return true.
    2. If assumptions contains a Record p such that p.[[First]] is s and p.[[Second]] is t, return true.
    3. If s.[[Kind]] is nominal and s.[[Declaration]] is an enum declaration, then
      1. If IsSubtype(the underlying type of s.[[Declaration]], t, assumptions) is true, return true.
    4. If s.[[Kind]] is literal, return IsSubtype(s.[[Base]], t, assumptions).
    5. If s.[[Kind]] is parameterized, then
      1. If IsSubtype(s.[[Base]], t, assumptions) is true, return true.
      2. If t.[[Kind]] is parameterized and SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Base]], t.[[Base]], assumptions) is true and the metadata subtype judgment of 1.4.13 holds of s.[[Metadata]] and t.[[Metadata]], return true.
      3. Return false.
    6. If s.[[Kind]] is not t.[[Kind]], return false.
    7. Set assumptions to the list-concatenation of assumptions and « the Record { [[First]]: s, [[Second]]: t } ».
    8. If s.[[Kind]] is any or s.[[Kind]] is void, return true.
    9. If s.[[Kind]] is primitive, then
      1. If s.[[Name]] is not t.[[Name]], return false.
      2. Return SameArgumentList(s.[[Arguments]], t.[[Arguments]], assumptions).
    10. If s.[[Kind]] is literal, then
      1. If SameValue(s.[[Value]], t.[[Value]]) is false, return false.
      2. Return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Base]], t.[[Base]], assumptions).
    11. If s.[[Kind]] is parameterized, then
      1. If s.[[Metadata]] and t.[[Metadata]] are not structurally equivalent (1.2.5.2), return false.
      2. Return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Base]], t.[[Base]], assumptions).
    12. If s.[[Kind]] is application, then
      1. If s.[[Builder]] and t.[[Builder]] are not the same function, return false.
      2. Return SameArgumentList(s.[[Arguments]], t.[[Arguments]], assumptions).
    13. If s.[[Kind]] is nominal, then
      1. If s.[[Declaration]] and t.[[Declaration]] are not the same Parse Node, return false.
      2. Return SameArgumentList(s.[[Arguments]], t.[[Arguments]], assumptions).
    14. If s.[[Kind]] is union or s.[[Kind]] is intersection, return SameTypeList(s.[[Members]], t.[[Members]], assumptions).
    15. If s.[[Kind]] is tuple, then
      1. If s.[[Elements]] and t.[[Elements]] do not have the same number of elements, return false.
      2. For each integer i such that 0 ≤ i and i < the number of elements of s.[[Elements]], do
        1. Let p be s.[[Elements]][i].
        2. Let q be t.[[Elements]][i].
        3. If p.[[Rest]] is not q.[[Rest]], return false.
        4. If p.[[Initial]] is none or q.[[Initial]] is none, then
          1. If p.[[Initial]] is not none or q.[[Initial]] is not none, return false.
        5. Else if SameValue(p.[[Initial]], q.[[Initial]]) is false, then
          1. Return false.
        6. If SameTypeWithAssumptions(p.[[Type]], q.[[Type]], assumptions) is false, return false.
      3. Return true.
    16. If s.[[Kind]] is array, then
      1. If s.[[Extent]] is not t.[[Extent]], return false.
      2. Return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Element]], t.[[Element]], assumptions).
    17. If s.[[Kind]] is reference, return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Target]], t.[[Target]], assumptions).
    18. If s.[[Kind]] is function, return SameFunctionType(s, t, assumptions).
    19. Assert: s.[[Kind]] is object.
    20. Return SameObjectType(s, t, assumptions).
     Note

    SameTypeList holds when two Lists of Type Records have the same length and are position-wise the same under assumptions. SameArgumentList is SameTypeList extended to compare non-type arguments, such as an array extent, with SameValue. SameObjectType (1.10.1.1) and SameFunctionType (1.9.2.1) compare the members of an object or function type. Because a canonical union or intersection has its members in a fixed order (1.2.2.5), position-wise comparison of members is sufficient.

    1.2.2.3 SameTypeList ( a, b, assumptions )

    The abstract operation SameTypeList takes arguments a (a List of Type Records), b (a List of Type Records), and assumptions (a List of Records that have [[First]] and [[Second]] fields) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether a and b have the same length and are position-wise the same type under assumptions. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If a and b do not have the same number of elements, return false.
    2. For each integer i such that 0 ≤ i and i < the number of elements of a, do
      1. If SameTypeWithAssumptions(a[i], b[i], assumptions) is false, return false.
    3. Return true.

    1.2.2.4 SameArgumentList ( a, b, assumptions )

    The abstract operation SameArgumentList takes arguments a (a List), b (a List), and assumptions (a List of Records that have [[First]] and [[Second]] fields) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether a and b have the same length and equal elements, comparing Type Record elements as types under assumptions, Parse Node elements by being the same Parse Node, compound compile-time constants by structural equivalence (1.2.5.2), and other elements with SameValue. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If a and b do not have the same number of elements, return false.
    2. For each integer i such that 0 ≤ i and i < the number of elements of a, do
      1. Let x be a[i].
      2. Let y be b[i].
      3. If x is a Type Record and y is a Type Record, then
        1. If SameTypeWithAssumptions(x, y, assumptions) is false, return false.
      4. Else if x is a Type Record or y is a Type Record, then
        1. Return false.
      5. Else if x is a Parse Node and y is a Parse Node, then
        1. If x and y are not the same Parse Node, return false.
      6. Else if x is a Parse Node or y is a Parse Node, then
        1. Return false.
      7. Else if x and y are compound compile-time constants, then
        1. If x and y are not structurally equivalent (1.2.5.2), return false.
      8. Else if SameValue(x, y) is false, then
        1. Return false.
    3. Return true.

    1.2.2.5 CanonicalizeType ( t )

    The abstract operation CanonicalizeType takes argument t (a Type Record) and returns a Type Record. It returns the canonical form of t, reading no [[Origin]] (17) in deciding it. Canonical Type Records that denote the same type are the same Record, which is what lets 1.2.2.6 intern them. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If t.[[Kind]] is union or t.[[Kind]] is intersection, then
      1. Let members be a new empty List.
      2. For each element m of t.[[Members]], do
        1. Let c be CanonicalizeType(m).
        2. If c.[[Kind]] is t.[[Kind]], then
          1. For each element n of c.[[Members]], append n to members.
        3. Else,
          1. Append c to members.
      3. Remove from members each element that is the same type (SameType) as an element occurring earlier in members.
      4. If t.[[Kind]] is intersection and members contains an element whose [[Kind]] is union and whose [[Members]] is empty, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: union, [[Members]]: « » } (the never type; see 1.4.11).
      5. If members has exactly one element, return that element.
      6. Order the elements of members according to the total order of 1.7.5.1.
      7. Return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: t.[[Kind]], [[Members]]: members }.
    2. If t.[[Kind]] is tuple, then
      1. Let elements be a new empty List.
      2. For each element e of t.[[Elements]], do
        1. Append the Tuple Element Record { [[Type]]: CanonicalizeType(e.[[Type]]), [[Rest]]: e.[[Rest]], [[Initial]]: e.[[Initial]] } to elements.
      3. Return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: tuple, [[Elements]]: elements }.
    3. If t.[[Kind]] is array, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: array, [[Element]]: CanonicalizeType(t.[[Element]]), [[Extent]]: t.[[Extent]] }.
    4. If t.[[Kind]] is reference, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: reference, [[Target]]: CanonicalizeType(t.[[Target]]) }.
    5. If t.[[Kind]] is literal, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: literal, [[Value]]: t.[[Value]], [[Base]]: CanonicalizeType(t.[[Base]]) }.
    6. If t.[[Kind]] is parameterized, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: parameterized, [[Base]]: CanonicalizeType(t.[[Base]]), [[Metadata]]: t.[[Metadata]] }.
    7. If t.[[Kind]] is application, then
      1. Let args be a new empty List.
      2. For each element a of t.[[Arguments]], do
        1. If a is a Type Record, append CanonicalizeType(a) to args.
        2. Else, append a to args.
      3. Return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: application, [[Builder]]: t.[[Builder]], [[Arguments]]: args }.
    8. If t.[[Kind]] is object or t.[[Kind]] is function, return the canonical form of t defined alongside that kind, with each contained Type Record canonicalized.
    9. Return t.
     Note

    Flattening merges a nested union into its parent, and likewise for intersection; de-duplication and the fixed order make member order insignificant, so A | B and B | A canonicalize to one Record and, after interning, one Type Object. A nominal, primitive, any, or void Type Record is already canonical apart from canonicalizing the Type Records in its [[Arguments]], which the recursion reaches when they occur. Recursion terminates because a cycle in a type passes through a nominal position, which is canonical without descending into its declaration. Flattening is also what removes a never member from a union: the member is an empty union, so it contributes nothing, and T | never canonicalizes to T with no rule naming the case. The intersection step above is the one algebraic fact about never that does not fall out this way (1.4.11). Canonicalization performs no subtype absorption: uint8 | number keeps both members, because folding by IsSubtype would make the canonical form depend on a relation that reads canonical forms, and the unabsorbed union costs nothing, since membership and assignability answer identically either way.

    1.2.2.6 GetTypeObject ( t )

    The abstract operation GetTypeObject takes argument t (a Type Record) and returns a Type Object. It returns the unique Type Object that denotes the type described by t. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. Let canonical be CanonicalizeType(t).
    2. If the type-object intern table contains a Type Object existing such that SameType(existing.[[TypeRecord]], canonical) is true, return existing.
    3. Let obj be a new Type Object whose [[TypeRecord]] internal slot is canonical.
    4. Add obj to the type-object intern table.
    5. Return obj.
     Note

    The intern table is a specification device; an implementation may realize it in any way that yields the same identities. It is a property of a heap: Type Objects, like other objects, are shared by the agents that share one and are not shared with an agent that does not (19).

    1.2.3 Subtyping and Assignability

    Subtyping relates a more specific type to a more general one. Assignability is the relation the type checker applies at an assignment, an argument, or a return: it is subtyping, widened at the boundary with the any type. Because this proposal performs no implicit numeric widening, a primitive type is a subtype of no other numeric type. It is a subtype of itself, of the any type, and of the same primitive bearing fewer metadata arguments, since a metadata parameterization refines the type it parameterizes rather than being a different type: Meter is a float32.<{ m: 1 }> and is a subtype of float32, which is what makes m instanceof float32 true.

    1.2.3.1 IsSubtype ( s, t, assumptions )

    The abstract operation IsSubtype takes arguments s (a Type Record), t (a Type Record), and assumptions (a List of Records that have [[First]] and [[Second]] fields) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether every value of the type s is a value of the type t, treating each pair in assumptions as already established so that comparison of recursive types terminates. The initial call passes an empty List for assumptions. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If SameTypeWithAssumptions(s, t, assumptions) is true, return true.
    2. If assumptions contains a Record p such that p.[[First]] is s and p.[[Second]] is t, return true.
    3. Set assumptions to the list-concatenation of assumptions and « the Record { [[First]]: s, [[Second]]: t } ».
    4. If t.[[Kind]] is any, return true.
    5. If s.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. For each element m of s.[[Members]], do
        1. If IsSubtype(m, t, assumptions) is false, return false.
      2. Return true.
    6. If t.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. For each element m of t.[[Members]], do
        1. If IsSubtype(s, m, assumptions) is true, return true.
      2. Return false.
    7. If t.[[Kind]] is intersection, then
      1. For each element m of t.[[Members]], do
        1. If IsSubtype(s, m, assumptions) is false, return false.
      2. Return true.
    8. If s.[[Kind]] is intersection, then
      1. For each element m of s.[[Members]], do
        1. If IsSubtype(m, t, assumptions) is true, return true.
      2. Return false.
    9. If s.[[Kind]] is not t.[[Kind]], return false.
    10. If s.[[Kind]] is nominal, then
      1. If t.[[Declaration]] is s.[[Declaration]], return SameArgumentList(s.[[Arguments]], t.[[Arguments]], assumptions).
      2. If t.[[Declaration]] is an ancestor of s.[[Declaration]] in the class and interface hierarchy, then
        1. Return the result of comparing t.[[Arguments]] against the arguments s supplies to t.[[Declaration]] through the hierarchy, with SameArgumentList.
      3. Return false.
    11. If s.[[Kind]] is array, then
      1. If t.[[Extent]] is not dynamic and t.[[Extent]] is not s.[[Extent]], return false.
      2. Return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Element]], t.[[Element]], assumptions).
    12. If s.[[Kind]] is tuple, then
      1. If the minimum length of s is less than the minimum length of t, return false, where a tuple's minimum length counts the elements before any [[Rest]] element whose [[Initial]] is none.
      2. If the maximum length of s exceeds the maximum length of t, return false, where a tuple's maximum length is unbounded when any element's [[Rest]] is true and its element count otherwise.
      3. For each integer i such that 0 ≤ i and i < the greater of the element counts of s.[[Elements]] and t.[[Elements]], do
        1. If s cannot supply position i, continue, where a tuple can supply each position below its maximum length.
        2. If IsSubtype(the type at position i of s, the type at position i of t, assumptions) is false, return false, where the type at a position is the [[Type]] of the element that receives it, a [[Rest]] element receiving its own position and every later one.
      4. Return true.
    13. If s.[[Kind]] is reference, return SameTypeWithAssumptions(s.[[Target]], t.[[Target]], assumptions).
    14. If s.[[Kind]] is function, return IsFunctionSubtype(s, t, assumptions).
    15. If s.[[Kind]] is object, return IsObjectSubtype(s, t, assumptions).
    16. Return false.

    Three of the steps above are one relation. A type refines another when it is a parameterized type whose [[Base]] is that type (1.4.13), an enum whose underlying type is that type (1.10.5), or a nominal type whose declaration extends or implements that type's declaration. A type is a subtype of every type it refines and of everything that type is a subtype of; the converse never holds without a crossing, a construction boundary for a parameterization, a validated call for an enum, and nothing at all for a class. The three steps are that rule's cases.

    Note

    A literal type is a subtype of its base and of everything its base is a subtype of, which is what makes 'on' usable where a string is required while string is not usable where 'on' is. A tuple's [[Rest]] flags must match position for position, and its defaults take part in identity but not in subtyping; tuples relate by the arity window: s is a subtype of t when every length s admits, t admits, and every position s can supply is a subtype of the position of t that receives it.

    A deferred application is a subtype only of itself and of the any type. Before specialization nothing finer than identity is known about its result, so nothing finer is assumed: two mentions of one deferred call are one type by interning, and two different calls are unrelated until they evaluate. No step above names the kind because none needs to; an application source reaches the final step for every target but itself and any.

    A generic class is invariant in its arguments, so Map.<string, uint8> is a subtype of no other instantiation of Map. This is the conservative choice and the only one that is sound for a mutable container, since a Map.<string, uint8> used as a Map.<string, number> would accept a Number into storage typed uint8. Whether a generic parameter may be declared covariant or contravariant, as it may in C# and Kotlin, is a question for the generics extension; nothing here forecloses it, and the step above is what such a declaration would relax.

    The kind-specific steps record the variance chosen by this proposal: a nominal type is a subtype along its declared inheritance; an array is invariant in its element, and a fixed-length array is a subtype of the dynamic-length array of the same element; a tuple is covariant position-wise; a reference is invariant in its target. IsObjectSubtype (1.10.1.2) applies width subtyping to properties and depth subtyping through a readonly one; IsFunctionSubtype (1.9.2.3) applies contravariant-parameter, covariant-return subtyping to signatures. An enum type is additionally a subtype of its underlying type (1.10.5). The justification for each choice is given by the clause that introduces the corresponding feature.

    1.2.3.2 IsAssignable ( s, t )

    The abstract operation IsAssignable takes arguments s (a Type Record) and t (a Type Record) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether a value of the type s may be assigned where the type t is expected. This is the relation checked at bindings, arguments, and returns. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If s.[[Kind]] is any or t.[[Kind]] is any, return true.
    2. Return IsSubtype(s, t, « »).
     Note

    When s is the any type and t is not, assignability holds but the value's membership in t is not known statically; a check is inserted at the boundary (1.6.3.1) that throws a TypeError if the value is not of the type t. When t is the any type, no check is required. This two-part treatment, static where the types are known and a run-time check at the any boundary, is the gradual character of the system: it is why an untyped program keeps its behaviour while a typed one is checked.

    1.2.4 The Static Type of an Expression

    The Static Type of an expression is the Type Record the type checker assigns to it. It is a syntax-directed operation defined by cases over the expression grammar. Each later clause that adds or modifies an expression form gives the Static Type for that form; the cases established by this clause are those that do not depend on a feature introduced later, listed in Table 2.

    Note

    The Static Type of a numeric literal given here is its type in the absence of a contextual type. Literal propagation, by which a literal takes the type its context requires, so that 0 in a uint8 position is a uint8, refines this and is defined by the conversions clause. The Static Types of the remaining expression forms, including operators, calls, array and object literals, and this, are given by the clauses that specify those forms; until a form is specified, its Static Type is the any type. An unannotated binding, let and const alike, likewise has the any Static Type whatever its initializer: this proposal infers no binding types, so the widening question an erased system answers with as const does not arise here, and Reflect.typeOf reads the value where a program wants the runtime fact.

    1.2.5 Compile-Time Evaluation

    Because a type is a value, a type annotation may be an expression that computes one (1.3.2.2); an expression whose computation can be carried out during type checking is compile-time evaluable. This clause defines when such an expression may be evaluated, when it is, what its evaluation may do, and what carries its result before it can run: the evaluability discipline, the deferred application that stands for a call whose generic arguments are not yet bound, the evaluation of a call with memoization and a fixpoint for recursion, and the budget that bounds the whole.

    Evaluation is confined to checking and reads declarations rather than run-time bindings. A top-level type-position expression evaluates during the compilation of its module, with the other static semantics and before the evaluation of any module in the graph begins. A type-position expression inside a generic declaration evaluates at each specialization, once every generic parameter it reads is bound. The callee of a call resolves through imports to a function declaration, whose body these semantics interpret, so no module's run-time evaluation is required or performed by evaluating a type.

    No operation of this specification evaluates such an expression before every generic parameter it reads is bound. The invariant this serves is stated exactly: the inference fixpoint is builder-free, and every checker-visible effect of user type code is declarative data, a forward-verified proposal, or a per-instantiation-checked contract. Those three are the whole of what user code may contribute to checking, and each is closed: the declarative data are a signature's [[ThisType]] and [[Narrows]] (8) and the hooks of a meta type (6), which the checker reads and calls at named seams; a forward-verified proposal is a trial candidate or a declared inverse (11), which binds nothing until forward evaluation and assignability confirm it; and a contract is checked at every evaluation before it is believed at any (10). The computed constraints of 1.3.3.1 are the measure of the boundary rather than an exception to it, since a constraint over T runs after T is bound and before the parameter it constrains is, which is after everything it reads.

    Note

    The blunt form of this invariant, that a builder never runs during inference at all, is what a reader expects and is not quite what holds, so it is worth being precise about which part is load-bearing. A trial evaluates a builder while a parameter is being inferred; what it does not do is let the builder into the fixpoint. Each evaluation is of a builder whose arguments are already bound, to a candidate rather than to an inference variable, and its result is checked rather than solved against. Nothing runs backwards, nothing is unified, and no builder observes a partially-solved state. That is the difference between a proposer the checker verifies and a participant the checker trusts, and this proposal admits only the first. It is stated so that later ergonomic pressure has something to push against, and the sentence to push against is the one this note follows, not the slogan.

    1.2.5.1 IsCompileTimeEvaluable ( expr )

    The abstract operation IsCompileTimeEvaluable takes argument expr (a Parse Node) and returns a Boolean. It determines whether expr can be evaluated during type checking, reading only values known at that time. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If expr is a literal, return true.
    2. If expr is a reference to a binding, then
      1. If the binding is a parameter of the function under evaluation, or a local binding of that function, return true.
      2. If the binding is immutable and its initializer is compile-time evaluable, return true.
      3. If the binding is a value generic parameter or a type generic parameter, return true.
      4. Return false.
    3. If expr is a function call, then
      1. Let callee be the expression that expr calls.
      2. Let args be the List of argument expressions of expr.
      3. If IsCompileTimeEvaluable(callee) is false, return false.
      4. If callee does not denote a compile-time-evaluable function, return false.
      5. For each element arg of args, do
        1. If IsCompileTimeEvaluable(arg) is false, return false.
      6. Return true.
    4. If every operand of expr is compile-time evaluable and the result of expr's operator is determined by its operands, return true.
    5. Return false.

    A compile-time-evaluable function is one whose body reads only its parameters, its own local bindings, immutable bindings whose initializers are compile-time evaluable, and other compile-time-evaluable functions. It may create local state and mutate it. Its body contains no await, no yield, no direct or indirect eval, no use of the Function constructor, and no construction of a Proxy; regular expressions are within the fragment. It performs no input or output and reads no ambient mutable state, none of which it can name under the reading rule above, which is why the discipline is a property of what the code can name rather than a wall around what it does.

    Note

    This is the same notion the where clauses and metadata annotations of the extensions require, and it is what lets a switch over a generic parameter be evaluated to a type during checking. A function that is not compile-time evaluable remains an ordinary function in expression position; only its use in type position is restricted. Local mutation is admitted because an accumulator array or a visited Set is how a walk over a type is written; shared module-level mutable state is not evaluable and is rejected statically, which also disposes of the hand-rolled memo cache, since the memoization of 1.2.5.3 is the supported mechanism and is observably equivalent. Fixing the fragment's grammar and semantics as a normative implementation target in its own right, with a conformance suite, is A; the discipline above is the boundary it pins.

    1.2.5.2 Deferred Applications

    A call whose arguments involve an unbound generic parameter cannot evaluate yet, and is carried as a type: the application Type Record of Table 1. The deferred application Type Record of a ComputedType has [[Builder]] the function its callee resolves to and, for each argument in order, an element of [[Arguments]] that is: the TypeParameter Parse Node where the argument is a reference to an unbound generic parameter; the deferred application Type Record where the argument is itself a ComputedType reading one; and otherwise the argument's compile-time value, a Type Object contributing its [[TypeRecord]].

    Identity is by that key. SameTypeWithAssumptions compares two application Records by builder identity and SameArgumentList, and Type Objects are interned, so two mentions of partial(T) in one generic body are one Type Object before anything is known about T. At specialization, each TypeParameter Parse Node among the [[Arguments]] is replaced by the parameter's binding, and the application evaluates by 1.2.5.3.

    Two compound compile-time constants, arrays or plain objects of compile-time constants, are structurally equivalent when they have the same shape and their leaves are pairwise SameValue, a Type Object comparing by identity and a RegExp by its source and flags. A compound constant argument contributes to an application's identity by structural equivalence rather than by reference, so pick(User, ['id', 'name']) written twice is one application although the array is a fresh object at each occurrence.

    Note

    An unbound parameter is represented by its declaring Parse Node rather than by a Type Record kind of its own, because a parameter is never a type by itself anywhere this specification currently reaches; what a bare parameter annotation denotes inside a generic body belongs to the generics work, and the hook for it is recorded in Table 23. Structural equivalence for compound constants is the value semantics the Composites proposal gives tuples and records; it is stated directly here so that this specification does not depend on that proposal, and can defer to it if it advances.

    1.2.5.3 EvaluateBuilderCall ( f, args )

    The abstract operation EvaluateBuilderCall takes arguments f (a compile-time-evaluable function) and args (a List) and returns either a normal completion containing a Type Object or a throw completion. It evaluates one type-level call, with memoization and a fixpoint for recursion, and checks the contracts of 10 against its result. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. Let key be CanonicalizeType(the Type Record { [[Kind]]: application, [[Builder]]: f, [[Arguments]]: args }).
    2. If the evaluation memo contains an entry for key, return that entry's Type Object.
    3. If the in-flight List contains key, then
      1. If no placeholder exists for key, let the placeholder for key be a fresh Type Object, created without interning and distinguished from every other value.
      2. Return the placeholder for key.
    4. Append key to the in-flight List.
    5. Let result be Completion(the evaluation of the body of f with the elements of args as its arguments under the semantics of this clause).
    6. Remove key from the in-flight List.
    7. Let value be ? result.
    8. If value is not a Type Object, throw a TypeError exception.
    9. If a placeholder exists for key, then
      1. If value is the placeholder for key, throw a TypeError exception.
      2. Let final be the Type Record obtained from value.[[TypeRecord]] by replacing each position that holds the placeholder with a reference back to the type being defined, forming a cyclic Type Record.
      3. If no replaced position is a reference position, throw a TypeError exception.
      4. Set value to GetTypeObject(final).
    10. Record value in the evaluation memo for key.
    11. Return value.

    The evaluation memo is a specification-level map from canonical application Type Records to Type Objects, and the in-flight List tracks the keys under evaluation. Each distinct key evaluates once per program; every later occurrence, in any module, yields the same interned Type Object. An implementation may realize both devices in any way that yields these results.

    Note

    The fixpoint is what makes a builder work on a recursive type with no API surface: a builder mapping over a type that references itself re-enters itself with the same key, receives the placeholder, and the placeholder resolves at return into the same kind of named-back-edge cyclic type that a recursive alias produces from syntax, validated by the same cycle rule (1.7.1). The two throws distinguish the two failures: a call whose entire result is its own placeholder learnt nothing and is unproductive recursion, and a cycle resolved at a position that embeds the layout inline would demand an infinite layout. A recursion that never repeats a key, a genuinely infinite family, is not caught here; it runs into the budget of 1.2.5.4. This mechanism is the computational twin of the coinductive assumption Lists that SameTypeWithAssumptions and IsSubtype already carry.

    1.2.5.4 The Evaluation Budget

    Evaluation under this clause is metered by two host-defined limits: a count of evaluation steps and a count of constructed Type Records, each per top-level type-position evaluation. For a given source text, two hosts either produce, position for position, the same interned types, or at least one reports a type error: the limits decide whether compilation fails, never what it produces.

    Exhaustion is not an abrupt completion the evaluated code can observe. No try statement within the evaluation handles it; the evaluation is abandoned, and EvaluateToTypeObject of the containing type-position expression is empty, with a diagnostic naming the outermost call and the deepest frames.

    Note

    Making exhaustion catchable inside evaluation would let a program's types depend on the host's limits: the same source would elaborate differently under a large budget and a small one, and the memo would cache whichever answer came first, which is nondeterminism through the error channel. Graceful degradation therefore has to be deterministic and structural: a builder takes an explicit cap as an argument and computes the identical type on every host, rather than falling back when fuel runs out. Bounding a deliberately Turing-complete type language by limits has precedent; the magnitudes are fixed here as floors a host meets or exceeds: ten million evaluation steps and one million constructed Type Records per top-level evaluation, ten thousand automaton states for the pattern decision procedure, and sixty-four specializations for a trial, calibrated against the erased-system limits the design cites. A host exceeding a floor can only turn a failure into a result, never a result into a different one, so the invariant above is undisturbed.

    1.2.5.5 EvaluateToTypeObject ( expr )

    The abstract operation EvaluateToTypeObject takes argument expr (a Parse Node) and returns either a Type Object or empty. It evaluates expr, which appears in type position, to the Type Object it denotes, or defers it, or returns empty if it does not evaluate to one. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If IsCompileTimeEvaluable(expr) is false, return empty.
    2. If expr is a ComputedType and expr reads a generic parameter that is not bound, return GetTypeObject(the deferred application Type Record of expr (1.2.5.2)).
    3. Let result be Completion(the evaluation of expr during type checking in which each call of a compile-time-evaluable function is performed by EvaluateBuilderCall).
    4. If result is an abrupt completion, return empty.
    5. Let value be result.[[Value]].
    6. If value is not a Type Object, return empty.
    7. Return value.

    It is a type error if an expression appears in type position and EvaluateToTypeObject of it is empty (1.2.6). When the empty arises from an abrupt completion whose value is an Error, the type error's diagnostic carries that Error's message, prefixed by the source text of the type-position expression; when it arises from the budget, the diagnostic is the one 1.2.5.4 describes. Diagnostic content is not observable to the evaluated program.

    Note

    Evaluation during type checking uses the ordinary evaluation semantics of the operators and calls involved, restricted to the compile-time-evaluable fragment, so no separate evaluator is defined. Because enum and sealed-class exhaustiveness is checked, a switch used to map a value to a type covers every case its argument can take and therefore yields a Type Object rather than undefined. A thrown exception becoming the diagnostic is the design's authored-error story: a builder states its own misuse message, which an erased type language cannot.

    1.2.6 Type Errors

    A type error is a violation of the type system detected by the type checker: an assignment whose source is not assignable to its target, a call whose arguments match no signature, a type annotation that is not compile-time evaluable, and the like. The clauses that follow state such a violation with the phrase “it is a type error if”.

    A type error that is determinable before a source text runs is an Early Error: a source text that contains one is rejected rather than evaluated. Detection is the work of the checking pass, one pass per Script or Module over the same Parse Nodes the parser produced, performed after the source text is parsed and before it is evaluated. The purely syntactic type rules are applied together with the other Early Errors at parse; the rules that evaluate — a computed type, a builder call, a hook of the metadata protocol (6) — are applied by the checking pass, under the discipline and budget of 1.2.5. For a Script the pass runs when evaluation of the script would begin, before the first step of its body; for a Module it runs per module, after the modules it depends on have been evaluated and before the module's own body, the point at which evaluation order reaches it. Before applying any judgment that consults them, the pass processes the source text's own type declarations — its type aliases, its interfaces, and its meta declarations — in source order, which is what makes a meta type declared in a source text govern a judgment in the same source text; a declaration the pass has processed is not processed again when evaluation reaches its position.

    A check that cannot be resolved statically, because a value reaches a typed position only as the any type, is deferred to run time. 1.6 specifies where these deferred checks are inserted and what they throw: a TypeError when a value is not of the required type, and a RangeError when a numeric value is outside the range of its type.

    Note

    The design documents sometimes describe a statically-determinable violation, such as assigning a uint32 to a uint8 binding, as a “TypeError”. This specification realizes such a violation as an Early Error, and reserves a thrown TypeError for the any boundary and other genuinely dynamic checks. The distinction is observable: an Early Error rejects the source text before any of it runs, whereas a thrown TypeError occurs during evaluation. Treating a statically-determinable type violation as a whole-source rejection matches a strict, check-before-run type system; whether some violations should instead be deferred to a thrown error at the offending operation was a decision left open when this clause was first written and is now settled, in this direction, for the reason that follows: the compile-time evaluation phase (1.2.5) already commits every implementation to the per-module checking pass this clause now names, so the cost that once made deferral attractive is sunk, and a diagnostic at the declaration is strictly better than the same diagnostic at first execution. For a module the rejection is observable after the module's dependencies have evaluated, which is when the pass runs; that two-phase timing is not new, being exactly how an error thrown by a module's own evaluation already behaves.

    1.3 Grammar

    This clause adds the syntax for writing a type. It is grammar only: it defines what may be written and where, while the meaning of each form is given by the clauses that follow. Every production here is additive, and every construct it introduces is a syntax error in the current language, so no source text changes meaning.

    Note

    The type grammar is a separate grammar from the expression grammar, reached from expression contexts only through the annotation and operator productions of 1.3.4 and 1.3.5. Keeping the two apart is what lets a type use |, &, {, and ( with meanings unrelated to their expression counterparts.

    1.3.1 Lexical Grammar

    1.3.1.1 Punctuators

    Two punctuators are added. Neither token sequence occurs in any currently valid source text, which is what allows them to be added without changing the meaning of an existing program.

    Punctuator :: OptionalChainingPunctuator TypePunctuator OtherPunctuator TypePunctuator :: one of .< := Note

    .< introduces a type argument list (1.3.2.1). Today a . must be followed by an identifier name, a private name, or a numeric part of a literal, so .< is never produced by valid source text. The leading . is what distinguishes generic application from a comparison: a.<b>(c) cannot be read as a < b > (c), which is the ambiguity that prevents the bare angle-bracket form. Because a numeric literal is lexed by longest match, 1.<2 still lexes as the literal 1. followed by <, and so continues to mean a comparison.

    := introduces a typed initializer or a conversion (1.3.5). A : immediately followed by = does not occur in valid source text.

    1.3.1.2 The InputElementType Goal Symbol

    A type argument list is delimited by < and >, and type arguments nest, so a source text may end one list immediately inside another. Under the ordinary lexical goals the two closing punctuators would be lexed as a single >> token. A goal symbol is therefore added in which > is always a single punctuator.

    InputElementType :: WhiteSpace LineTerminator Comment CommonToken RightBracePunctuator

    The InputElementType goal symbol is used in all syntactic grammar contexts where a TypeArguments or a TypeParameters is being parsed, between its opening .< or < and its closing >. When this goal symbol is used, the punctuators >>, >>>, >=, >>=, and >>>= are not recognized; a > is lexed as the single-character punctuator >.

    Note

    This is the same device the specification already uses to lex / differently where a regular expression may appear. It resolves the nesting problem lexically rather than by a parser rule, so Map.<string, [].<uint8>> closes both lists. A shift operator inside a type argument list must be parenthesized, which is only relevant to a value argument, since a shift cannot otherwise appear in a type.

    1.3.1.3 Imaginary Literals

    An imaginary literal is a numeric literal with the suffix i, denoting a value on the imaginary axis. The suffix occupies the slot a numeric literal followed immediately by an identifier start occupies today, which is a syntax error, and is the slot the n suffix of a BigInt literal already uses.

    NumericLiteral :: DecimalLiteral DecimalBigIntegerLiteral DecimalImaginaryLiteral NonDecimalIntegerLiteral[+Sep] NonDecimalIntegerLiteral[+Sep] BigIntLiteralSuffix LegacyOctalIntegerLiteral DecimalImaginaryLiteral :: DecimalLiteral ImaginaryLiteralSuffix ImaginaryLiteralSuffix :: i Note

    Unlike the BigInt suffix, which attaches only to an integer literal, the imaginary suffix attaches to any DecimalLiteral, so 4i, 2.5i, and 1e3i are all imaginary literals. The rule that the source character following a numeric literal must not be an identifier start continues to apply, so 4if remains a syntax error. The type of an imaginary literal is defined by the complex numbers extension.

    1.3.2 Type Expressions

    A Type is the syntax of a type. Union and intersection compose types, and the remaining forms are primary.

    Type[Yield, Await] : UnionType[?Yield, ?Await] FunctionType[?Yield, ?Await] UnionType[Yield, Await] : IntersectionType[?Yield, ?Await] UnionType[?Yield, ?Await] | IntersectionType[?Yield, ?Await] IntersectionType[Yield, Await] : PrimaryType[?Yield, ?Await] IntersectionType[?Yield, ?Await] & PrimaryType[?Yield, ?Await] PrimaryType[Yield, Await] : shared PrimaryType[?Yield, ?Await] PredefinedType LiteralType TypeReference[?Yield, ?Await] ComputedType[?Yield, ?Await] KeyOfType[?Yield, ?Await] ArrayOrTupleType[?Yield, ?Await] ObjectType[?Yield, ?Await] ReferenceType[?Yield, ?Await] CoverParenthesizedTypeAndFunctionTypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await] PredefinedType : one of void null undefined LiteralType : NumericLiteral - NumericLiteral StringLiteral true false ReferenceType[Yield, Await] : ref PrimaryType[?Yield, ?Await] KeyOfType[Yield, Await] : keyof PrimaryType[?Yield, ?Await] Note

    FunctionType is an alternative of Type rather than of UnionType, because a function type's return extends as far to the right as it can: (string) => uint32 | null returns uint32 | null. A function type is therefore not by itself a member of a union or an intersection, and putting one in a union requires parenthesizing it. That is true on either side, so the order of a union's members never matters.

    void and null are reserved words and so cannot be reached through TypeReference, which is why they are given their own production. undefined is not a reserved word but a global binding, an ordinary identifier; it is given its own production here as well, so that undefined in type position always names the undefined type rather than depending on the global binding being in scope, matching the treatment of null. The other built-in type names, uint8 and the rest, are ordinary identifiers and are reached through TypeReference; they are shadowable, so a program that declares its own binding named uint8 is unaffected.

    ReferenceType takes a PrimaryType operand, so ref uint8 | null is a union of ref uint8 and null. ref is a contextual keyword: it is a valid identifier today, so ref followed by a token that cannot begin a type is an identifier.

    keyof is likewise a contextual keyword, and a KeyOfType binds tighter than union and intersection, so keyof A | B is the union of keyof A and B. Its result is the union of the operand's key types, one literal type per named member. The full definition over each kind is given by 1.3.2.3, producing a union of the literal types of 1.4.12.

    1.3.2.1 Type References and Type Arguments

    A type reference names a type, optionally applying type arguments to it. Generic application always uses .<, in a type and in an expression alike.

    TypeReference[Yield, Await] : TypeName[?Yield, ?Await] TypeArguments[?Yield, ?Await]opt TypeName[Yield, Await] : IdentifierReference[?Yield, ?Await] TypeName[?Yield, ?Await] . IdentifierName TypeArguments[Yield, Await] : .< TypeArgumentList[?Yield, ?Await] ,opt > TypeArgumentList[Yield, Await] : TypeArgument[?Yield, ?Await] TypeArgumentList[?Yield, ?Await] , TypeArgument[?Yield, ?Await] TypeArgument[Yield, Await] : Type[?Yield, ?Await] Note

    Because .< is a single punctuator, a qualified name and an application are told apart lexically: in Temporal.Unit the . is followed by an identifier, while in Map.<string, uint8> the .< opens an argument list, so a TypeName cannot extend across it.

    A type argument is written as a Type even when it is a value, as the width in int.<8> and the lane count in vector.<float32, 4> are. A numeric or string literal argument is a LiteralType, and a name that resolves to a value generic parameter is a TypeReference, so the syntax need not distinguish the two; the clause on generics decides which a given parameter expects. A value argument that is a computed expression, such as an arithmetic combination of value generic parameters, is written through a ComputedType (1.3.2.2): the builder call is the spelling for a computed argument, evaluated once its parameters are bound, and the argument position takes no further computation syntax of its own. A where clause (4.3) constrains the values a variadic or value parameter admits.

    1.3.2.2 Computed Types

    A type position admits a call. The callee is written as a type reference and the arguments as ordinary expressions, so partial(User), keysOf(T), and pick(User, ['id', 'name']) are types, as is a call of a call where a function returns a function that computes one.

    ComputedType[Yield, Await] : TypeReference[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await] ComputedType[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await]

    A ComputedType is a compile-time type expression and is governed by 1.2.5: the call must be compile-time evaluable, and the type the ComputedType denotes is the Type Object that EvaluateToTypeObject returns for it. A call that is not evaluable, that completes abruptly, or that does not produce a Type Object is a type error by the rule stated there.

    Note

    The form is additive twice over. Every position in which a Type may appear is new syntax, so no existing program contains one; and within the grammar of this specification a TypeName followed by ( derived nothing before this production, so no program written against the earlier grammar changes meaning either. Disambiguation costs one token: after a type reference, .< opens type arguments, ( opens a call, and any other token ends the reference.

    Only a call is admitted, deliberately. The design's rule is that an expression evaluating to a Type Object during checking is a valid type, and the call is the one expression form the type grammar needs to reach all of it: a conditional over IsAssignable, a map over a union's members, or any richer computation is a function away, written inside the builder rather than inline in the annotation. Keeping the surface to one form also keeps the evaluability requirement attached to one production.

    The same call in expression position is an ordinary call and needs nothing from this clause: a builder is an ordinary function, and calling it at run time yields the same interned Type Object the type position denotes. This production makes the call writable where a type is required; it does not change what the call is.

    1.3.2.3 Static Semantics of keyof

    A KeyOfType denotes GetTypeObject of the Type Record that KeyTypesOf returns for the Type Record its operand denotes. It is a type error if KeyTypesOf of that Record is empty.

    1.3.2.3.1 KeyTypesOf ( t )

    The abstract operation KeyTypesOf takes argument t (a Type Record) and returns either a Type Record or empty. It returns the type of the keys of t, or empty where t has no keys. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If t.[[Kind]] is object, then
      1. Let keys be a new empty List.
      2. For each element p of t.[[Properties]], do
        1. If p.[[Key]] is a String, let base be the Type Record of the String type.
        2. Else, let base be the Type Record of the Symbol type.
        3. Append the Type Record { [[Kind]]: literal, [[Value]]: p.[[Key]], [[Base]]: base } to keys.
      3. For each element x of t.[[IndexSignatures]], append x.[[KeyType]] to keys.
      4. Return CanonicalizeType(the Type Record { [[Kind]]: union, [[Members]]: keys }).
    2. If t.[[Kind]] is intersection, then
      1. Let keys be a new empty List.
      2. For each element m of t.[[Members]], do
        1. Let k be KeyTypesOf(m).
        2. If k is empty, return empty.
        3. Append k to keys.
      3. Return CanonicalizeType(the Type Record { [[Kind]]: union, [[Members]]: keys }).
    3. If t.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. If t.[[Members]] is empty, return t.
      2. Let first be KeyTypesOf(t.[[Members]][0]).
      3. If first is empty, return empty.
      4. Let kept be a List of the union members of first: the elements of first.[[Members]] where first.[[Kind]] is union, and « first » otherwise.
      5. For each integer i such that 1 ≤ i and i < the number of elements of t.[[Members]], do
        1. Let k be KeyTypesOf(t.[[Members]][i]).
        2. If k is empty, return empty.
        3. Remove from kept each element that is not assignable (IsAssignable) to k.
      6. Return CanonicalizeType(the Type Record { [[Kind]]: union, [[Members]]: kept }).
    4. If t.[[Kind]] is parameterized, return KeyTypesOf(t.[[Base]]).
    5. Return empty.
     Note

    These are the rules of the design's keysOf, which computes the same function over a Type Object held in a variable, so the operator and the kit function cannot drift apart. A union's keys are the common keys, computed by keeping the first arm's keys that remain assignable to each further arm's, which is what lets the literal 'x' survive against an arm whose keys come from a string-keyed index signature. An intersection's keys are the union of its members' keys. A Symbol key contributes its own literal type (1.4.12), so the operation is total over the keys an object type can have. A refinement has its base's keys, so keyof sees through a brand and through the where of a dependent record type (5) to the properties underneath, which is the only reading on which keyof and the property mappers agree about a refined object. A kind with no keys is empty and a type error, matching the kit's authored throw. keyof never is distinct from that case: never is the empty union, so the union step above returns it unchanged and keyof never is never, not empty and not an error. The kit's keysOf fold must include the empty-union base case to agree, since its arm-intersection fold does not otherwise reach it; a keysOf written as a non-empty reduction over the arms should special-case the empty union to never.

    1.3.2.4 Array and Tuple Types

    An array type is a fixed or dynamic count of elements of one type, and a tuple type is a fixed count of individually typed positions. Both are written in brackets, and are told apart by whether a type argument list follows the closing bracket.

    ArrayOrTupleType[Yield, Await] : [ ] [ ] TypeArguments[?Yield, ?Await] [ ArrayExtent[?Yield, ?Await] ] TypeArguments[?Yield, ?Await] [ TupleElementList[?Yield, ?Await] ,opt ] ArrayExtent[Yield, Await] : AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] TupleElementList[Yield, Await] : TupleElement[?Yield, ?Await] TupleElementList[?Yield, ?Await] , TupleElement[?Yield, ?Await] TupleElement[Yield, Await] : Type[?Yield, ?Await] Initializer[+In, ?Yield, ?Await]opt ... Type[?Yield, ?Await] Note

    [] alone is the array of any, which is the bound of the array and tuple family, so a type parameter constrained by it is satisfied by any array or tuple. [].<T> is a dynamic array, [N].<T> a fixed one, and [T1, T2] a tuple. A tuple's trailing position may carry a default through Initializer, and a tuple may spread another tuple or an array type at either end.

    The first three alternatives and the fourth are distinguished only after the closing ], by whether a TypeArguments follows, and their bracketed contents belong to different grammars: an extent is an expression and a tuple element list is a list of types. A single-element case such as [uint8] therefore cannot be resolved by bounded lookahead. This specification resolves it with a cover grammar, as it does for parenthesized expressions and arrow parameters: the bracketed text is parsed under a cover production and refined to an extent expression or a tuple element list once the token after the closing ] is known, an extent when a TypeArguments follows and a tuple otherwise. The committee may still prefer to distinguish the two forms by their opening bracket, which would remove the cover grammar rather than change the meaning of any program it already accepts; this is a decision worth taking before the syntax is final.

    shared before a type marks a type whose values may live in memory shared between agents, as let a: shared [4].<uint8>, a fixed-length array backed by a SharedArrayBuffer. It is a type error if the operand of shared is itself shared. Which types admit shared, and every rule of construction, publication, and access that follows, belongs to the threading extension and is recorded at its hook (Table 23).

    1.3.2.5 Object Types

    An object type describes a value by its members. It is the inline form of an interface.

    ObjectType[Yield, Await] : { } { TypeMemberList[?Yield, ?Await] TypeMemberSeparatoropt } TypeMemberList[Yield, Await] : TypeMember[?Yield, ?Await] TypeMemberList[?Yield, ?Await] TypeMemberSeparator TypeMember[?Yield, ?Await] TypeMemberSeparator : one of , ; TypeMember[Yield, Await] : PropertyName[?Yield, ?Await] ?opt TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await] Initializer[+In, ?Yield, ?Await]opt PropertyName[?Yield, ?Await] ?opt MethodSignature[?Yield, ?Await] IndexSignature[?Yield, ?Await] MethodSignature[Yield, Await] : TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FunctionTypeParameterList[?Yield, ?Await]opt ,opt ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt IndexSignature[Yield, Await] : [ BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await] ] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await] Note

    Members are separated by either , or ;, and a trailing separator is permitted, so both { x: float32, y: float32 } and { x: float32; y: float32; } are written. A ? marks an optional member.

    An IndexSignature constrains the properties an object type does not declare by name: { [key: string]: uint32 } is the type whose remaining string-keyed properties, beyond those declared, are uint32. It is a type error if a TypeMember's Initializer appears without ?, since a default belongs to an optional member; where an object lacking the member is checked against the type, the default supplies its value, the design's c?: any = []. It is a type error if the key type of an IndexSignature is not string, symbol, uint32, or a union of these, and a type error if a member declared in the same ObjectType is not assignable to the signature's value type. Each signature contributes an Index Signature Record to the [[IndexSignatures]] field of the object Type Record the type constructs (1.10.1).

    1.3.2.6 Function Types

    A function type is a parameter list and a return type. Its parameters may be named or unnamed, and may carry the ref and rest modifiers a declaration's parameters carry.

    FunctionType[Yield, Await] : FunctionTypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await] => Type[?Yield, ?Await] FunctionTypeParameters[Yield, Await] : CoverParenthesizedTypeAndFunctionTypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await] CoverParenthesizedTypeAndFunctionTypeParameters[Yield, Await] : ( ) ( FunctionTypeParameterList[?Yield, ?Await] ,opt ) FunctionTypeParameterList[Yield, Await] : FunctionTypeParameter[?Yield, ?Await] FunctionTypeParameterList[?Yield, ?Await] , FunctionTypeParameter[?Yield, ?Await] FunctionTypeParameter[Yield, Await] : refopt Type[?Yield, ?Await] refopt BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] ?opt TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await] ... BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await] ... Type[?Yield, ?Await]

    When the production PrimaryType : CoverParenthesizedTypeAndFunctionTypeParameters is recognized, the interpretation of CoverParenthesizedTypeAndFunctionTypeParameters is refined using ParenthesizedType : ( Type ) ; it is a type error if the cover does not match that refinement.

    ParenthesizedType[Yield, Await] : ( Type[?Yield, ?Await] ) Note

    (uint8) is a parenthesized type and (uint8) => uint8 is a function type, and the two are told apart by the token after the closing parenthesis, exactly as a parenthesized expression is told from arrow parameters. The cover grammar is the same device the specification already uses there.

    1.3.3 Type Parameters

    A generic declaration introduces its parameters in angle brackets. Declaration uses <, while application uses .<, so the ambiguity that application avoids does not arise at a declaration, where no expression may appear.

    TypeParameters[Yield, Await] : < TypeParameterList[?Yield, ?Await] ,opt > TypeParameterList[Yield, Await] : TypeParameter[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameterList[?Yield, ?Await] , TypeParameter[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameter[Yield, Await] : BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameterConstraint[?Yield, ?Await]opt TypeParameterDefault[?Yield, ?Await]opt TypeParameterConstraint[Yield, Await] : : Type[?Yield, ?Await] extends Type[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameterDefault[Yield, Await] : = Type[?Yield, ?Await] Note

    A parameter written with : is a value generic parameter, whose argument is a value of the named type, as W and H are in class GridArray<W: uint32, H: uint32>. A parameter written with extends is a type parameter bounded by a type, as T is in T extends []. A parameter with neither is an unbounded type parameter. The where clause that further constrains a parameter is defined by the generics extension.

    1.3.3.1 Computed Constraints and Literal Inference

    The Type of a TypeParameterConstraint or a TypeParameterDefault may be a ComputedType (1.3.2.2) that reads parameters declared earlier in the same TypeParameterList. It is a type error for it to read the parameter it belongs to or one declared later in the list.

    Parameters bind left to right. At a use of the generic declaration, whether through explicit arguments or through inference from a call, the first parameter is bound, then the next parameter's constraint is evaluated by 1.2.5 over the bindings so far, then that parameter is bound and checked against its evaluated constraint, and so on down the list. Within the declaration itself, before any parameter is bound, such a constraint is carried as the deferred application of 1.2.5.2.

    Where a parameter's evaluated constraint is a literal type or a union of literal types (1.4.12), the binding inferred for that parameter from a call argument is the literal type of the argument's value, not the widened base. The binding is then checked against the constraint as any binding is.

    Note

    This is the one placement of a builder call that needs a sentence of new specification, and the one inference rule builders ask for. function pluck<T, K: keysOf(T)>(o: T, key: K): indexed(T, K) reads in order: infer T from o, evaluate keysOf(T) over that binding, and infer K from the second argument literally, so pluck(user, 'name') binds K to the literal type 'name' and the return type evaluates to that property's type. The constraint does double duty, checking the argument and cueing the literal inference, which is the cue K extends keyof T gives an erased system. It is a rule about constraints rather than a hook: no user code runs inside the inference of the parameters the constraint reads, and a mistaken argument fails the ordinary constraint check, with the evaluated union available to the diagnostic.

    A constraint that is a value type binds a value, as the note above says of W: uint32; a constraint that is a union of literal types binds a literal type, whose value is recoverable from it. The design's kit reads that value back out constantly, which suggests these are one mechanism seen from two sides, a binding to the literal type with the value as its view, and the decision is that the literal-type binding is primitive with the value as its view, executed by the generics clause.

    1.3.4 Type Annotations

    A type annotation attaches a type to a binding, a parameter, a return, or a class member. This clause adds the annotation and the positions that accept it; the meaning of an annotation in each position is given by the declarations, functions, and classes clauses.

    TypeAnnotation[Yield, Await] : : Type[?Yield, ?Await] TypedInitializer[In, Yield, Await] : := AssignmentExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await]

    A binding may carry an annotation, or a typed initializer that infers the binding's type from its initializer. The binding of a for-of or for-in head takes the same annotation, as for (const a: int32 of f()), and a BindingPattern in any of these positions annotates its elements through SingleNameBinding.

    LexicalBinding[In, Yield, Await] : BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt Initializer[?In, ?Yield, ?Await]opt BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypedInitializer[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] BindingPattern[?Yield, ?Await] Initializer[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] VariableDeclaration[In, Yield, Await] : BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt Initializer[?In, ?Yield, ?Await]opt BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypedInitializer[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] BindingPattern[?Yield, ?Await] Initializer[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] ForBinding[Yield, Await] : BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt BindingPattern[?Yield, ?Await]

    A parameter may carry an annotation, an optional marker, and a ref modifier.

    FormalParameter[Yield, Await] : refopt BindingElement[?Yield, ?Await] SingleNameBinding[Yield, Await] : BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] ?opt TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt Initializer[+In, ?Yield, ?Await]opt

    A function may carry type parameters and a return annotation.

    FunctionDeclaration[Yield, Await, Default] : function BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FormalParameters[~Yield, ~Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt { FunctionBody[~Yield, ~Await] } [+Default] function TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FormalParameters[~Yield, ~Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt { FunctionBody[~Yield, ~Await] }

    A class field may carry an annotation.

    FieldDefinition[Yield, Await] : ClassElementName[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt Initializer[+In, ?Yield, ?Await]opt Note

    The positions above are representative rather than exhaustive: the remaining function forms, including methods, arrow functions, generators, and class constructors, take the same annotations and are patched by the clauses that specify them (1.9.8). An arrow function's parameter list and return annotation interact with the arrow cover grammar and are given by 1.9.7.

    ref before a parameter is a contextual keyword. A parameter named ref remains valid, since an identifier that is not followed by a token that can begin a binding element is a parameter name; function f(ref) therefore continues to declare a parameter named ref, while function f(ref a: int32) declares a reference parameter named a.

    1.3.5 Types in Expression Position

    A type name is already an expression, since a type is a value, so uint8 and Map.<string, uint8> may be written where a value is expected. The type forms whose syntax collides with the expression grammar cannot: a function type reads as an arrow function, an object type as a block, and a union as a bitwise or. The type operator resolves the collision by parsing its operand as a type.

    UnaryExpression[Yield, Await] : UpdateExpression[?Yield, ?Await] delete UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] void UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] typeof UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] + UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] - UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] ~ UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] ! UnaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] [+Await] AwaitExpression[?Yield] TypeOperatorExpression[?Yield, ?Await] TypeOperatorExpression[Yield, Await] : type [no LineTerminator here] Type[?Yield, ?Await]

    A conversion converts the value of its left operand to a type, and is written after the value.

    RelationalExpression[In, Yield, Await] : ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] < ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] > ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] <= ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] >= ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] instanceof ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] [+In] RelationalExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] in ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] [+In] PrivateIdentifier in ShiftExpression[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] := Type[?Yield, ?Await] RelationalExpression[?In, ?Yield, ?Await] [no LineTerminator here] is Type[?Yield, ?Await] Note

    Placing a conversion at RelationalExpression gives it the precedence of instanceof, so a + b := uint8 converts the sum, and the right operand being a Type means x := A | B converts to the union rather than taking the bitwise or of a conversion.

    type is a contextual keyword, since a program may already bind the name type, and this leaves an ambiguity: type (uint8) => uint8 is a type operator applied to a function type, while type (x) is a call of a function named type, and the two agree until the token after the closing parenthesis. This specification resolves it with a cover grammar, as the grammar already does for arrow parameters and parenthesized expressions: the parenthesized text is parsed under a cover production and refined to a function-type operand or a call argument list once the token after the closing parenthesis is known. The type declaration form raises no ambiguity, since its type is followed by a binding identifier, and the computed types of 1.3.2.2 do not widen it: type f(x) was a syntax error and is now the operator applied to a computed type, so the only covered case is an operand that begins with (. The committee may still prefer to avoid the cover grammar by restricting the operand or by requiring the operator only where a type cannot otherwise be written; that would remove the covered case rather than change the meaning of any program the cover grammar already accepts, and is a decision worth taking before the syntax is final.

    The operand of type is a full Type and so extends as far as one reaches: type A | B is the union of A and B, not the bitwise or of type A and B. A bare name is a type expression, so type uint8 is a redundant spelling of uint8. The extensions that introduce type-position operators use the Type production defined here, as is (5.2) does above, sharing this precedence so that x is A | B tests the union.

    is is a contextual keyword rather than a reserved word, and the [no LineTerminator here] restriction is what that costs. a is b on one line is a Syntax Error today, so the new production takes syntax no program can be using; but a, is, and b on three lines is three statements today, by automatic semicolon insertion, and must stay three. The restriction keeps it so, at the price that a program cannot break the line before is. This is the treatment async already receives before an arrow's parameters, and the alternative, reserving the word, is not available.

    1.3.6 Type Arguments and Placement new in Expression Position

    A specialization such as f.<uint8>(x) and a placement allocation such as new(buffer, byteOffset) Type(0) are member-expression forms. The productions below restate MemberExpression and CallExpression with the added alternatives (1.1).

    MemberExpression[Yield, Await] : PrimaryExpression[?Yield, ?Await] MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] [ Expression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ] MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] . IdentifierName MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] TemplateLiteral[?Yield, ?Await, +Tagged] SuperProperty[?Yield, ?Await] MetaProperty new MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await] MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] . PrivateIdentifier MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] TypeArguments[?Yield, ?Await] new ( AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ) MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await] new ( AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] , AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ) MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await] new ( AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] , AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] , AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ) MemberExpression[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await] CallExpression[Yield, Await] : CoverCallExpressionAndAsyncArrowHead[?Yield, ?Await] SuperCall[?Yield, ?Await] ImportCall[?Yield, ?Await] CallExpression[?Yield, ?Await] Arguments[?Yield, ?Await] CallExpression[?Yield, ?Await] [ Expression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ] CallExpression[?Yield, ?Await] . IdentifierName CallExpression[?Yield, ?Await] TemplateLiteral[?Yield, ?Await, +Tagged] CallExpression[?Yield, ?Await] . PrivateIdentifier CallExpression[?Yield, ?Await] TypeArguments[?Yield, ?Await]
