    A MemberExpression or CallExpression followed by TypeArguments evaluates the expression and specializes the result by the listed types. The value must be generic: a generic function, a generic class, or a parameterized type; the selection and substitution are those of 4.2, of which this production is the explicit written form. Where the expression's Static Type shows a value that is not generic it is a type error, and otherwise a specialization of a non-generic value throws a TypeError exception. The OptionalChain productions are not extended; a specialization does not follow ?..

    The placement forms allocate into an existing buffer rather than into fresh storage. The first argument is the buffer, the second, when present, is the byte offset and otherwise 0, and the third, when present, is the byte length reserved for each element and otherwise the constructed type's byteLength. Construction stores each field of each instance at its laid-out position (Table 23, memory layout); a third argument larger than byteLength leaves padding between elements, a smaller one makes elements overlap, and bytes the layout does not assign keep the buffer's contents, since reusing storage is what the form is for and the zero-fill rule of fresh allocation does not apply. It is a RangeError exception when the extent so computed exceeds the buffer's length. Over a resizable buffer the extent is recorded: shrinking the buffer below a live allocation's extent detaches those instances, touching a detached instance throws a TypeError exception, and growing never invalidates.

    Note

    new ( begins both a placement form and an ordinary new whose constructor is parenthesized. Source that continues with a MemberExpression after the closing parenthesis is the placement form; under the base grammar such source has no parse as a single expression, so the added reading changes no existing program. The array form of the design, new(buffer, byteOffset, byteElementLength) [10].<Type>(0), needs no further production: [10].<Type> is an ArrayLiteral specialized by TypeArguments, which the alternative above already admits as the target MemberExpression.

    1.4 The Type Universe

    This clause adds the value types of this proposal to the ECMAScript language types of 6.1. For each type it fixes the set of values the type contains and the Type Record (1.2.1) that describes it. The operations over these values, the conversions between them, and the literals that produce them are specified by later clauses. This clause settles only what the values are and what the types are called.

    1.4.1 Value Types

    A value type is a type whose values are ECMAScript language values that are not Objects. A value of a value type has no identity: two values of the same value type that are the same value are indistinguishable, and nothing observable distinguishes one occurrence of such a value from another. The existing primitive types are value types in this sense, and every type this clause adds is one.

    The values of distinct value types are distinct. Where two types are described below as containing the same mathematical values, each contains its own values representing them: the uint8 representing 0 and the uint16 representing 0 are different values of different types, as the Number 1 and the BigInt 1 are today. Every value therefore belongs to exactly one type, which is what lets 1.6.1.1 report it.

    Every type added here is described by a Type Record whose [[Kind]] is primitive, whose [[Name]] is the String naming the type, and whose [[Arguments]] is the List of its type and value arguments. uint8 is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "uint", [[Arguments]]: « 8 » }, and vector.<float32, 4> by { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "vector", [[Arguments]]: « the Type Record for float32, 4 » }.

    Note

    Because Type Records are canonicalized and interned (1.2.2.6), a type written two ways is one Type Object: int8 and int.<8> name the same Type Record and therefore compare strictly equal. The shorthand names of 1.4.17 are consequently aliases rather than separate types.

    1.4.2 Integer Types

    For each positive integer N, int.<N> is a value type whose values are the integers from -2N-1 through 2N-1 - 1 inclusive, and uint.<N> is a value type whose values are the integers from 0 through 2N - 1 inclusive. Each has exactly 2N values. Neither has a negative zero, an infinity, or a NaN.

    N is a value argument, so int.<8> and int.<16> are distinct types, and the width of a type is recoverable from its Type Record.

    Note

    N is bounded: it lies in the inclusive interval from 1 to 216, and a width outside it is a type error. The bound is a specified constant like the budgets, generous enough for any hashing or cryptographic width and small enough that a computed type cannot demand an unbounded layout for one Type Record of cost. The design gives shorthands only through 128 and uses uint.<1> as the lane type of a boolean vector, but nothing above restricts N, and an unbounded N obliges every implementation to provide arithmetic at arbitrary width, which is a substantially larger commitment than the named widths imply. Fixing N to a bounded set, to the powers of two, or to a range up to 128 are all defensible; the choice belongs with the committee, and it interacts with the bit-field widths of the memory layout extension, which are the reason a width such as 3 is meaningful at all.

    1.4.3 Binary Floating-Point Types

    float16, float32, float64, and float128 are value types whose values are those of the corresponding IEEE 754-2019 binary interchange formats, with the same treatment of NaN the Number type receives: the many NaN encodings of the format are represented as a single NaN value. Each therefore has 2w - 2p + 3 values, where w is the width of the format in bits and p its precision in bits, as given in Table 3.

    Each of these types has a positive zero and a negative zero, a positive infinity and a negative infinity, and a single NaN, as the Number type does.

    Note

    float64 and the Number type have the same set of values but are distinct types, so neither is assignable to the other without a conversion. This is the rule stated for every pair of value types and is not a special case: Number is the type an untyped program computes with, and float64 is the type a program asks for when it wants that format by name.

    1.4.4 Decimal Floating-Point Types

    decimal32, decimal64, and decimal128 are value types whose values are those of the corresponding IEEE 754-2019 decimal interchange formats. A finite value of a decimal type is a triple of a sign, an integer coefficient, and an exponent, drawn from the ranges in Table 4. Each type additionally has a positive infinity, a negative infinity, and a single NaN.

    Two finite values of a decimal type that have the same numerical value but different exponents are distinct values of the type. The values written 1.0, 1.00, and 1.000 are three values of decimal128, one for each exponent, and a decimal type therefore preserves the significance of the quantity it was given. IEEE 754 calls such a set of values a cohort.

    Note

    Preserving trailing zeros is the point of the decimal types: a price given to two places prints to two places, which is what the money types of the primitive metadata extension depend on. The consequence is that the numerical order and the value identity of a decimal type come apart, and the equality operations must choose between them. The design settles two of the three: == compares numerical value, so 1.0 == 1.00 is true, and SameValueZero compares numerical value, so 1.0 and 1.00 are one key in a Map or a Set rather than two. Identity follows the representation: SameValue distinguishes the cohort members 1.0 and 1.00, so they are distinct map keys and distinct literal types, while === and == compare numerically and find them equal, the same split Number already has for +0 and -0.

    This specification settles SameValue the way the paragraph above already uses it: SameValue distinguishes cohort members, so Object.is(1.0, 1.00) is false for two decimal128 values of different exponents, while SameValueZero and == compare numerical value and find them equal. This is the split the design records for ===/== versus identity, and it is the parallel with negative zero, where SameValue distinguishes +0 from -0 and SameValueZero does not. The alternative, SameValue comparing numerical value, was rejected because it would make Object.is unable to distinguish values that print differently, which no other type permits; its cost, that SameValue and SameValueZero then differ on a case unrelated to zero, is the same cost negative zero already imposes and is judged the lesser one. This remains a decision the committee owns, recorded here because the type universe is where the cohort becomes observable.

    1.4.5 Rational Types

    For each positive integer N, rational.<N> is a value type whose values are the exact rational numbers representable as a quotient of two values of int.<N>. A value of rational.<N> is a pair of a numerator and a denominator, both values of int.<N>, such that the denominator is greater than 0 and the greatest common divisor of the numerator and the denominator is 1. Every value of the type is therefore in lowest terms, and each rational number has at most one representation in the type.

    A rational type has no negative zero, no infinity, and no NaN. An operation whose exact result is not representable, including one whose normalized numerator or denominator falls outside int.<N>, throws a RangeError rather than rounding.

    Note

    Normalizing on construction is what makes the type a value type in the ordinary sense: without it, one half would have many representations and equality would have to normalize before comparing. It is also what makes the RangeError unavoidable, since a sum of two representable rationals can have an unrepresentable normalized denominator. Refusing to round is the distinction between this type and a float; a rational that cannot be represented exactly is an error, not an approximation.

    1.4.6 Complex Types

    For a value type T that is number or one of the binary floating-point types, complex.<T> is a value type whose values are the ordered pairs of a real part and an imaginary part, each a value of T. The number of values of complex.<T> is the square of the number of values of T.

    Note

    The width-named shorthands of 1.4.17 count total bits rather than component bits, following the convention of NumPy and Go, so complex64 is a pair of float32 and not a pair of float64.

    1.4.7 Vector Types

    For a value type T that is an integer, binary floating-point, or vector type, and a positive integer N, vector.<T, N> is a value type whose values are the sequences of exactly N values of T. T is the lane type and N the lane count. The number of values of vector.<T, N> is the number of values of T raised to the power N.

    Note

    A vector's lane type may itself be a vector, which is how the boolean vectors of the design are built: boolean8 is vector.<uint.<1>, 8>, eight one-bit lanes, and boolean8x16 is vector.<boolean8, 16>. The lane counts an implementation can hold in a register are a matter for the SIMD extension; the type exists for every positive N.

    It is a type error to form vector.<T, N> where T is not a lane type or N is not a positive integer, since neither denotes a vector type. Where the lane type is itself a vector, this applies to it in turn.

    1.4.8 The any Type

    any is the type of every value. Every ECMAScript language value is a value of the any type, and a binding whose type is any accepts any value. It is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: any }, and it is the type of a binding that carries no annotation, which is what makes an untyped program a program whose bindings are all of the any type.

    1.4.9 The void Type

    void is the type with no values. It is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: void }, and it is written as the return type of a function that returns no value. No value is a value of the void type, so no binding may have it.

    Note

    A call of a function whose return type is void evaluates to undefined, as a call of a function with no return statement does today. The void type is the statement that a program must not depend on that result, not a claim that no result exists.

    1.4.10 The null and undefined Types

    null is the type whose one value is null. It is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "null", [[Arguments]]: « » }, and it is written with the PredefinedType null.

    undefined is the type whose one value is undefined. It is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "undefined", [[Arguments]]: « » }, and it is written with the PredefinedType undefined. It is distinct from the void type: void is the type with no values, written to say that a result must not be depended on, whereas undefined has the one value undefined and a binding may hold it.

    Note

    These are the types 1.6.1.1 reports for null and undefined. The reject type of a typed promise is undefined by convention when the promise never rejects, which is one place the undefined type is written directly; another is a union such as uint32 | undefined for a value that may be absent, the position an erased system reaches with an optional. null and undefined are ordinary members of the type universe and may appear in a union, an intersection, or any other type position.

    1.4.11 The never Type

    never is the type with no values: the union of no members, described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: union, [[Members]]: « » }. It is not a new kind of type; it is the empty case of a kind the type system already has, admitted as a type in its own right because computation reaches it regardless. A union filtered of its every member is this type, whatever this clause says.

    Its algebra follows from the operations already defined, with no rule specific to it except the one noted last.

    • never is a subtype of every type. In IsSubtype (1.2.3.1) a source of the union kind is a subtype when every member is, and a quantification over no members holds.
    • No type other than never is a subtype of never. A target of the union kind requires some member to accept the source, and there is none.
    • never is the identity of union. Canonicalization (1.2.2.5) flattens a member of the same kind into its parent, and flattening an empty union contributes no members, so T | never canonicalizes to T with no rule naming the case.
    • never annihilates intersection: an intersection with a never member is never. This is the one fact that does not fall out, and CanonicalizeType carries the step for it.

    No value is a value of the never type: IsOfType (1.6.2.1) over a union with no members is false for every value, and DefaultValueOf (1.7.3.1) of it is none, so a binding of the type can be neither defaulted nor, since no expression's value can satisfy it, initialized. A value of the any type reaching a position of the never type fails its boundary check (1.6.3.1) for every value. That vacuity is the point: never exists to be computed with, as the result of removing every member from a union, not to be inhabited.

    Note

    Because the empty union canonicalizes to itself and Type Objects are interned (1.2.2.6), every computation that produces the empty union produces one Type Object, so never has the same identity story as every other type. The name is admitted through 1.4.17 on the same terms as every other built-in type name, so let x: never; is writable, and the name is shadowable exactly as uint8 is. Admitting it does not extend switch exhaustiveness, which remains reserved to enums and sealed hierarchies (1.10.6). It is also distinct from the empty sentinel of the narrowing operations (1.7.5), which reports a test that cannot succeed as a mistake rather than narrowing to the type with no values.

    1.4.12 Literal Types

    A literal type has exactly one value. It is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: literal, [[Value]]: v, [[Base]]: b }, where v is a String, Number, Boolean, BigInt, or Symbol and b is the Type Record of the existing language type of which v is a value. A LiteralType (1.3.2) denotes the literal type of the value it writes: a StringLiteral with base string, a NumericLiteral with base number or bigint as the literal is written, and true or false with base boolean. A Symbol has no literal syntax, so no LiteralType writes a symbol literal type: one arises through construction (1.11.4) or through keyof over a symbol-keyed object type (1.3.2.3), and a program names it with an ordinary binding.

    The one value of a literal type is a value of its base, so the type is a subtype of its base (1.2.3.1), 'on' is usable where a string is required, and a value is of a literal type exactly when it is that value. A union of literal types is the closed set the design's discriminated unions select on, and keyof produces one (1.3.2.3). boolean and true | false have the same values and are distinct types: true | false is a subtype of boolean through the literal rule, and boolean is deliberately not a subtype of the pair, because completing the relation over value-set equality is the road to deciding language equivalence, which the pattern judgment already declines.

    A literal expression whose contextual type (1.5.2) is a literal type, or a union or intersection with a literal type among its members, takes the literal type of its own value where that literal type is assignable to the contextual type, so let a: 'on' | 'off' = 'on'; gives a the literal type 'on'. A contextual type of any other shape leaves the literal to the rules already given for untyped literals, so let s: string = 'on'; still gives s a string and let x: uint8 = 5; is unchanged.

    Note

    The base of a numeric literal is number however the literal will be used, matching the Static Type a literal takes with no contextual type; a literal type over another numeric type, the uint8 3 as a type, is expressible through construction but not through syntax, and its interaction with literal propagation is settled where it was needed: a : constraint naming a value type gives a literal argument the literal type over that base, the contextual rule above extended to the constraint's context, so W: uint32 binds the literal type of 4 over uint32 with 4 as its value view, and propagation elsewhere keeps base number. A Symbol is a literal type's value like any other, compared by SameValue, which for a Symbol is identity, so a symbol's identity is already its type's identity and the unique symbol of an erased system has nothing left to do. Any Symbol qualifies, well-known, registered, or freshly created, and no declaration form distinguishes them because none is needed. A symbol literal type is reproducible outside its Agent exactly when its Symbol is: one from Symbol.for names itself by its key, while one from Symbol is Agent-local, so a type carrying it cannot be shared, and the extension that shares types across Agents reports that rather than inventing an identity (Table 23).

    1.4.13 Parameterized Types

    A metadata parameterization refines a base type with a metadata value: the Type Record { [[Kind]]: parameterized, [[Base]]: b, [[Metadata]]: m }. Over a primitive base it is written float32.<{ m: 1 }>; over any base it is reachable through construction (1.11.4), so an object, class, or function type may carry a brand or a pattern. A width or lane argument such as the N of int.<N> is not a parameterization: structural arguments live in a primitive Record's [[Arguments]], and metadata rides this wrapper.

    A parameterized type has exactly the representation of its [[Base]]. Metadata never changes layout, alignment, or size.

    The default relation is the branding rule. A parameterized type is a subtype of its base, so the brand is shed freely on the way up; the base is not a subtype of the parameterization, so the way down is a crossing: calling the Type Object, as UserId(7), is the construction boundary, and the metadata's validation judgment runs there where one is defined. Two parameterizations of one base with structurally equivalent metadata are one type by interning; two with different metadata are related only as the metadata subtype judgment admits. A metadata value's fields are governed by the meta types that claim them, which supply that judgment and the validation judgment below, and which are required to be pure, compile-time evaluable, and deterministic; 6 defines them all.

    A value is of a parameterized type when its runtime type is that type or a subtype of it, or when it is a value of the [[Base]] and the validation judgment holds of the value and the metadata (6). A brand, whose meta type defines no validation, therefore admits no bare value of its base except through the construction boundary, which is the point of a brand.

    The StringPattern meta type carries a pattern as metadata, a source and flags rather than a RegExp object, so that one pattern written in two modules is one type; a hook that reads it is handed a RegExp built from those. Its validation judgment holds of a String exactly when the entire String matches the pattern, the whole-string discipline 1.12.5 already uses; its subtype judgment holds only of patterns whose source and flags are identical, which structural equivalence already makes one type, so the floor is reflexivity and the conservatism is intentional. No template-literal type kind exists or is planned: a finite string family is ordinary code over a union of literal types, and an infinite one is a pattern.

    Note

    Interning is what makes a brand a real newtype: brand(uint32, 'UserId') written in two modules is one type with no registry, because the metadata compares structurally and GetTypeObject does the rest. The brand is not a lie told to the checker, as an intersection with a phantom symbol is in an erased system: the parameterization is visible to reflection, participates in validation, and can carry conversion semantics when the brand is a unit rather than a tag. Builders still cannot mint a nominal type, a class, an enum, or a sealed hierarchy, and this clause is why they do not need to: the thing nominal minting is wanted for is a brand.

    The sanctioned sharpening of the pattern subtype judgment is the design's decision procedure over the regular fragment: pattern pairs free of backreferences and lookaround, within a fixed automaton size, get the exact language-inclusion answer, and pairs beyond the bound get the syntactic one. The tier is decided by syntactic size and never by remaining fuel, so the judgment is identical on every host, the same invariant 1.2.5.4 enforces; the size constant is among the design's open budget numbers. A parameterized field with no initializer takes the base's default where validation admits it or none is defined, and has none otherwise; whether a brand should instead always demand an initializer is a policy its meta type states by defining validation.

    1.4.14 The type Type

    type is the type whose values are the Type Objects (1.2.1). It is described by the Type Record { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "type", [[Arguments]]: « » }, and it is the Static Type of a type name or type expression in expression position (1.2.4). A function that computes a type annotates its return with it.

    Note

    type is not a value type: its values are Objects, and they have identity. That identity is exactly what interning fixes, so two occurrences of a type expression that denote the same type are the same Type Object rather than merely equal ones. type is nonetheless a primitive type in the sense of the Type Record kinds, since it is a named type of the language rather than a structural description of an object's members.

    Because type is itself a type, type is a value of type, and a type argument may be constrained to it, which is what a generic parameter written T: type asks for. 1.11 defines what a Type Object exposes.

    1.4.15 The Existing Language Types

    The names number, boolean, string, symbol, object, and bigint denote the existing ECMAScript language types of 6.1: the Number type, the Boolean type, the String type, the Symbol type, the Object type, and the BigInt type respectively. This proposal adds no values to any of them.

    Each is described by a Type Record whose [[Kind]] is primitive and whose [[Name]] is the name above, except object, which is described by { [[Kind]]: object, [[Properties]]: « » }, the object type with no required properties, to which every Object is assignable.

    Note

    number is a value type like the rest, so no other numeric type is assignable to it and it is assignable to no other numeric type. It is nonetheless the type an untyped program computes with, and the type a numeric literal takes in the absence of a contextual type (1.2.4), which is why an unannotated program continues to mean what it means today.

    1.4.16 Numeric Types

    The specification already treats a numeric type as a set of values together with a fixed table of abstract operations over them, and defines Number and BigInt that way in 6.1.6. Each integer, binary floating-point, decimal floating-point, rational, complex, and vector type is a numeric type in that sense, and provides the operations of that table for which it is defined.

    For a numeric type T, the operations are those named in 6.1.6: T::unaryMinus, T::bitwiseNOT, T::exponentiate, T::multiply, T::divide, T::remainder, T::add, T::subtract, T::leftShift, T::signedRightShift, T::unsignedRightShift, T::lessThan, T::equal, T::sameValue, T::sameValueZero, T::bitwiseAND, T::bitwiseXOR, T::bitwiseOR, and T::toString. A numeric type need not define every operation; one it does not define is not applicable to its values, and an operator that would use it is a type error. The definitions are given by 1.8 and 1.12.

    Note

    Reusing this table rather than introducing a parallel one is what lets the existing operator semantics carry over unchanged. The specification generalized these operations from Number to two types when BigInt was added, and the shape it arrived at, an operator dispatching on the numeric type of its operands with no implicit conversion between types, is the shape this proposal needs. What this clause adds is more numeric types, not a new mechanism.

    The table was written for types that are closed under their operations and totally ordered, and not every type here is either. A complex type has no lessThan, since the complex numbers are not ordered. A vector type's comparisons return a vector of lane results rather than a Boolean, which is why the SIMD extension gives them their own return type rather than reusing lessThan. A rational type's operations can fail where a float's would round. These are the cases 1.8.3 settles, and they are the reason the table above is a list of what may be defined rather than what must be.

    1.4.17 Type Names

    Each type of this clause is denoted by a name. The generic names int, uint, rational, complex, and vector denote a type when applied to arguments. The remaining names in Table 5 are shorthands, each denoting the same Type Record as its expansion and therefore, after interning, the same Type Object.

    Note 1

    The shorthands are aliases, not distinct types, so int8 === int.<8> is true and a value of one is a value of the other. complex expands through number rather than float64, so complex and complex128 are distinct types, as number and float64 are.

     Note 2

    How these names are bound is not settled by the design and is not settled here. Every other part of this proposal is gated by syntax that is an error today, which is the argument that no existing program changes meaning. Type names are not: they are ordinary identifiers, and number, string, object, boolean, symbol, any, and never are among the most common identifiers in existing code. The available answers each cost something, and this specification decides for the one that costs existing programs nothing: the built-in type names resolve only in type positions, every one of which is new syntax, through the ordinary scope chain first and through the built-in table only where no user binding of the name exists. Expression-position access is the type operator, const T = type uint8;, which exists for exactly this. Nothing binds in expression scope, so no existing use, assignment, shadowing, or typeof probe of these names changes meaning, and there is nothing to reassign because there is no binding to assign to; a scope that shadows a name in type position loses the short spelling there and writes the operator form. The committee can trade convenience back in later; compatibility cannot be traded back.

    Binding them as properties of the global object is what the language does for every constructor it has added, and it keeps var string = "x" working, since a var at global scope reuses an existing property. But the design also asks that these names cannot be reassigned, and a non-writable global property would make that same assignment throw in strict mode and fail silently outside it, so the two requirements cannot both hold. Binding them as immutable lexical declarations of the global scope satisfies the design but makes var string at the top level of an existing script a SyntaxError, which is not acceptable. Requiring them to be imported, which earlier drafts of the design assumed, has no compatibility cost at all and gives up the ambient spelling the rest of the document uses. Resolving them in type position only, as a language with separate type and value namespaces would, is compatible but contradicts types being values, since the same name must denote the type in expression position for reflection to work.

    This is the one place where the proposal's compatibility argument does not reach, and it deserves a decision and a web-compatibility analysis before Stage 2.

    1.5 Conversions

    This clause defines when a value of one type becomes a value of another. The governing rule is that it does not: a value of one value type never implicitly becomes a value of another, and every conversion between two distinct value types is written in the source text. This clause states that rule, defines the four things that remain implicit and are not conversions between two typed values, and specifies the explicit conversion that a program writes when it wants one.

    Note

    The rule exists because no widening is lossless for every pair. int64 to float64 loses precision above 2**53, and a language that admits the easy cases has to enumerate the hard ones anyway. Refusing all of them is the rule Rust, Swift, and Go use. It is livable here for the same reason it is livable there: the initializers and arguments that would want a conversion are usually literals, and a literal never needs one (1.5.3).

    1.5.1 The Conversion Rule

    At an assignment, an argument, or a return, the source value's type must be assignable to the target type (1.2.3.2). Since a primitive type is a subtype only of itself and of the any type, no numeric value type is assignable to another, and a program that requires one to become another states the conversion.

    Four things remain implicit. None is a conversion between two typed values.

    1.5.2 Contextual Types

    The contextual type of an expression is the type its position requires, or none where its position requires nothing. It is determined by the position rather than by the expression, and it is what a literal consults.

    Note

    An argument of an overloaded call has no single contextual type until an overload is chosen, and the choice depends on the argument. The literal ranking of 1.5.6 is what resolves that circularity for a literal argument. 1.9.3 specifies the rest of overload resolution.

    1.5.3 Literal Propagation

    A numeric literal has no type of its own. In a position whose contextual type is a numeric value type, the literal takes that type, and a literal whose value that type cannot represent is a type error rather than a silent truncation. This refines the Static Type given for a numeric literal in Table 2, which is the type of a literal whose contextual type is none.

    1.5.3.1 LiteralValueInType ( literal, t )

    The abstract operation LiteralValueInType takes arguments literal (a Parse Node) and t (a Type Record) and returns either a value of the type t or unrepresentable. It returns the value of the type t that literal denotes, or unrepresentable if t cannot represent it. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. Let mv be the mathematical value denoted by literal, as defined by the numeric literal grammar, before any rounding.
    2. If t is an integer type, then
      1. If mv is not an integer, return unrepresentable.
      2. If t has a value representing mv (1.4.2), return that value.
      3. Return unrepresentable.
    3. If t is a binary floating-point type or the Number type, then
      1. Let v be the result of rounding mv to the nearest value of t, with ties to even.
      2. If v is an infinity and mv is finite, return unrepresentable.
      3. Return v.
    4. If t is a decimal floating-point type, then
      1. Let q be the number of digits literal writes after its decimal point, negated, adjusted by any exponent literal writes.
      2. Let c be the integer such that mv = c × 10**_q_.
      3. If c is outside the coefficient range of t, return unrepresentable.
      4. If q is outside the exponent range of t, return unrepresentable.
      5. Return the value of t with coefficient c and exponent q.
    5. If t is a rational type, then
      1. Let n be the numerator of mv expressed in lowest terms with a positive denominator.
      2. Let d be the denominator of mv expressed in lowest terms with a positive denominator.
      3. Let ct be the integer type of the components of t.
      4. If n is a value of ct and d is a value of ct, return the value of t with numerator n and denominator d.
      5. Return unrepresentable.
    6. Return unrepresentable.
     Note

    The mathematical value of a literal is exact. 0.1 denotes one tenth, not the Number nearest to one tenth, so 0.1 in a decimal64 position is the decimal one tenth and in a rational position is 1/10, while in a float32 position it rounds. This falls out of the specification already defining a literal's mathematical value and rounding it only when producing a Number.

    A decimal type reads its cohort member from the source text rather than from the mathematical value, since 1.0 and 1.00 have the same mathematical value and are different values of the type (1.4.4). A literal therefore carries its significance into the type, which is what makes a price written to two places a value with two places.

    Representability is exact for an integer, a decimal, and a rational, and is range membership for a float, which rounds. A float is the one family where a literal that is not representable exactly is still accepted, because requiring exactness would reject 0.1 in a float32 position and no useful program could be written.

    The Static Type of a numeric literal whose contextual type is a numeric value type t is t. It is a type error if LiteralValueInType(literal, t) is unrepresentable.

    Note 1

    The error is an Early Error (1.2.6), so f(256) where f takes a uint8 does not compile, and neither does let a: uint8 = 200; a + 300;, where the literal takes the type of the other operand. This is the difference between a literal and a cast: a cast is an instruction to discard information and truncates, while a literal that does not fit its type is a mistake.

     Note 2

    One case in the design does not follow this rule. A store to an element of a typed array, as in array[0] = 300 where array is a [].<uint8>, is specified to wrap to 44 rather than to be rejected, on the grounds that it is unchanged from what a Uint8Array does today. But [].<uint8> is new syntax, so no existing program writes that store, and the compatibility argument does not apply to it; meanwhile the literal rule says the same literal in a uint8 binding does not compile. The two cannot both be right for a literal element, and 1.13.2 decides for the literal rule: the store of an out-of-range literal is rejected like every other typed position, and the wrap of Table 12 continues to govern a stored value that is not a literal.

    1.5.4 Explicit Conversion

    An explicit conversion is written as a call on the type, uint8(v), or with the conversion operator after the value, v := uint8 (1.3.5). The two are the same operation. A conversion is an instruction to discard information: it truncates, wraps, or rounds as the target requires, and does not fail merely because information is lost.

    1.5.4.1 ConvertValue ( value, from, to )

    The abstract operation ConvertValue takes arguments value (an ECMAScript language value), from (a Type Record), and to (a Type Record) and returns either a normal completion containing an ECMAScript language value or a throw completion. It converts value, of the type from, to the type to. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If SameType(from, to) is true, return value.
    2. If from.[[Kind]] is any, return ? RequireType(value, to).
    3. If to.[[Kind]] is any, return value.
    4. If from is a numeric type and to is a numeric type, return ? NumericConvert(value, from, to).
    5. If to is string or boolean, return ? PrimitiveConvert(value, to).
    6. Let conversion be the user-defined conversion from from to to, if one is declared (1.5.5).
    7. If conversion is not empty, return ? the result of applying conversion to value.
    8. If IsAssignable(from, to) is true, return value.
    9. Throw a TypeError exception.
     Note

    The final step is reached only where the operand types are related by no rule above. Where those types are known statically the conversion is rejected as a type error before evaluation (1.2.6), so the exception is thrown only where a type reaches the conversion dynamically, as when a Type Object computed at run time is called as a function.

    1.5.4.2 NumericConvert ( value, from, to )

    The abstract operation NumericConvert takes arguments value (a value of a numeric type), from (a numeric Type Record), and to (a numeric Type Record) and returns either a normal completion containing a value of the type to or a throw completion. It converts value between two numeric types according to Table 9. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If Table 9 specifies a RangeError for value converted from from to to, throw a RangeError exception.
    2. Let result be the value given by Table 9 for the families of from and to.
    3. Assert: result is a value of the type to.
    4. Return result.

    1.5.4.3 PrimitiveConvert ( value, to )

    The abstract operation PrimitiveConvert takes arguments value (an ECMAScript language value) and to (a Type Record for string or boolean) and returns either a normal completion containing a value of the type to or a throw completion. It converts value to one of the ordinary primitive types, where a conversion for its source is defined. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If to is boolean, return ToBoolean(value).
    2. Assert: to is string.
    3. If Table 8 gives no conversion for value, throw a TypeError exception.
    4. Return ? ToString(value).
         Note 1

    The rule for the string type divides its sources by whether the source has a canonical text or only a diagnostic one, and not by whether it is a primitive. A number, a BigInt, and a Boolean each have exactly one text that denotes them, ToString of them is total, and nothing is lost: 5 is '5' and reads back as 5. That is worth having without ceremony, and it is why this rule is not the mirror image of the numeric one. The asymmetry is principled rather than convenient: ToString of a number is total and lossless, while ToNumber of a string is partial and lossy, so the direction that cannot fail is admitted and the direction that can is written as a parse (1.12.5).

    undefined, null, an object, and a Symbol are refused because their text is a diagnostic rather than a denotation. The values they would produce are the language's best-known silent failures: a 'undefined' reaching an interface that was meant to show a name, a '[object Object]' where a field was meant, and an array quietly becoming its comma-joined elements. A program that wants those writes String(_v_) and has then said so. A Symbol is refused for the reason it always was, which is that ToString of one throws.

    The boolean type takes every value, and that is deliberate rather than an omission. ToBoolean is total, every value has a defined truthiness, and if (_v_) is the language's own idiom for asking. There is no lost value to warn about and no second answer a program might have meant.

     Note 2

    The design leaves the conversions between the decimal types and the binary types open, noting only that they would need to be clear. They are the hard ones, and the difficulty is not arithmetic but which cohort member results. A float64 converted to a decimal128 has an exact decimal expansion, since every binary float is a terminating decimal, but that expansion for 0.1 is a 55-digit value that no decimal128 can hold, so the result must be rounded and its exponent chosen. Choosing the exponent that preserves the most significance, choosing the shortest expansion that round-trips, and choosing a fixed quantum are all defensible and produce different values. This clause specifies the direction that is exact and leaves the other open deliberately.

    Integer wrapping is the same operation the language already performs when storing to a Uint8Array, and the float to integer rule is the shape of the existing ToInt32 and ToUint8 operations: truncate, then reduce modulo the width, with a non-finite source becoming zero. Reusing that shape means a typed array store and an explicit cast agree.

    1.5.5 User-Defined Conversions

    A type may declare a conversion to or from another type, and a declared conversion applies at an assignment, an argument, and a return, because the type that declares it has opted in. Three forms declare one.

    When both a converting constructor and a declared conversion could apply to the same pair, the constructor is preferred. It is a type error if two conversions of the same form apply and neither is more specific.

    Note

    A conversion to a tuple type is what makes a class destructurable: a class declaring operator [number, number, string]() may be destructured into three bindings, because the destructuring position has a tuple contextual type and the conversion supplies it. The operator overloading extension specifies the declaration syntax; this clause specifies only when a declared conversion is consulted.

    1.5.6 Literal Overload Ranking

    A literal argument may satisfy more than one signature of an overloaded function, since it has no type of its own and several parameter types could represent it. The signatures are ranked by the parameter type in the order of Table 11, and the first that can represent the literal is chosen. This ranks literals. An argument that is a typed value is not ranked; it selects the signature its type is assignable to.

    Note

    The ranking is the only place the order of the numeric types matters, and it matters only for a literal. Given f(a: float32) and f(a: uint32), the call f(1) selects the float32 signature by the ranking, while f(uint32(1)) selects the uint32 one because its argument is typed.

    The ranking as the design gives it does not include number, and it should. number is the type a numeric literal takes with no contextual type, the type an untyped program computes with, and the type the design's own justification for ranking float64 first appeals to. Given f(a: number) and f(a: float32), the call f(1) has no answer above. The natural repair is to rank number first, ahead of float64, on the same reasoning that ranks float64 ahead of the rest. It is recorded here rather than assumed because it changes which function a call selects.

    The ranking also omits the rational types and the parameterized widths int.<N> and uint.<N> for an N that is not a named shorthand, both of which can represent a literal. 1.9.6 says where they fall.

    1.5.7 The Existing Abstract Conversion Operations

    The abstract operations of 7.1, including ToNumber, ToString, ToPrimitive, and ToBoolean, continue to apply to the values of the existing language types unchanged. A value of a type added by this proposal is not an Object, so ToPrimitive returns it unchanged, and ToBoolean of a numeric value is false exactly when the value is a zero or a NaN of its type.

    Note

    ToNumber is where the conversion rule and the existing semantics meet, and the meeting needs care. ToNumber of a uint8 would produce a Number, which is a conversion between two value types that the rule forbids, so an operator specified in terms of ToNumber cannot simply be applied to a typed operand. The resolution is that an operator on typed operands dispatches through the numeric type protocol (1.4.16) rather than through ToNumber, so +a on a uint8 is a uint8 and never a Number. ToNumber retains its meaning for the any type, where the operand's type is not known statically and the existing dynamic semantics apply. 1.8.1 specifies the dispatch; this note records why ToNumber is not the mechanism.

    1.6 Runtime Enforcement

    A type violation is reported where it is found: at compile time where the types are known, and at run time otherwise. This clause specifies the second half. It defines the runtime type of a value, the test that decides whether a value belongs to a type, the check that enforces a type at a boundary, the boundaries at which that check is inserted, and the circumstances under which it is not needed at all.

    Note

    The checks exist because of the any type. A value reaches a typed position with its type unknown only when it passes through any, which is the type of every binding an untyped program declares. Everything else is settled statically. This is what makes the system gradual rather than merely optional: the guarantee a typed program has is not that its author annotated carefully, but that the boundary with untyped code is checked.

    1.6.1 The Runtime Type of a Value

    1.6.1.1 RuntimeTypeOf ( value )

    The abstract operation RuntimeTypeOf takes argument value (an ECMAScript language value) and returns a Type Record. It returns the Type Record describing the type of which value is a value. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If value is a value of one of the types added by 1.4, return that type's Type Record.
    2. If value is undefined, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "undefined", [[Arguments]]: « » }.
    3. If value is null, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: primitive, [[Name]]: "null", [[Arguments]]: « » }.
    4. If value is a Boolean, a String, a Symbol, a Number, or a BigInt, return the Type Record of the corresponding type named in 1.4.15.
    5. If value is a Type Object, return the Type Record for type.
    6. Assert: value is an Object.
    7. If value has a [[RuntimeType]] internal slot, return value.[[RuntimeType]].
    8. If value is an instance of a class, return the nominal Type Record of that class.
    9. Return the object Type Record whose [[Properties]] describes the own properties of value and their declared types, where a property with no declared type has the any type.
     Note

    Every value has exactly one runtime type, because the values of distinct value types are distinct (1.4.1). The uint8 representing 0 and the uint16 representing 0 are different values, so a instanceof uint16 is false for a uint8 binding a even though typeof a reports "number" for both. typeof is unchanged by this proposal: it reports the underlying language type, and RuntimeTypeOf is what reports the type of this proposal. 1.11.1 exposes the latter as Reflect.typeOf.

    The [[RuntimeType]] slot is what a Proxy constructed with a type argument carries, so that it reports the type its trap checks enforce rather than the shape of its target. A Proxy constructed without one has no slot and is an ordinary Object.

    The types named "undefined" and "null" are the undefined and null types of 1.4.10, which the type universe names and which PredefinedType (1.3.2) admits in type position. The design writes undefined as a type, since the reject type of a typed promise is undefined by convention when the promise never rejects, and a value that may be absent has a type such as uint32 | undefined.

    1.6.2 Type Membership

    1.6.2.1 IsOfType ( value, t )

    The abstract operation IsOfType takes arguments value (an ECMAScript language value) and t (a Type Record) and returns either a normal completion containing a Boolean or a throw completion. It determines whether value is a value of the type t. It may call user code, since deciding a structural type reads the value's properties. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If t.[[Kind]] is any, return true.
    2. If t.[[Kind]] is void, return false.
    3. If t.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. For each element m of t.[[Members]], do
        1. If ? IsOfType(value, m) is true, return true.
      2. Return false.
    4. If t.[[Kind]] is intersection, then
      1. For each element m of t.[[Members]], do
        1. If ? IsOfType(value, m) is false, return false.
      2. Return true.
    5. If t.[[Kind]] is parameterized, then
      1. If IsSubtype(RuntimeTypeOf(value), t, « ») is true, return true.
      2. If ? IsOfType(value, t.[[Base]]) is false, return false.
      3. If the metadata's meta type defines a validation judgment (1.4.13), return ? the result of applying that judgment to value and t.[[Metadata]].
      4. Return false.
    6. If t.[[Kind]] is literal, return SameValue(value, t.[[Value]]).
    7. If t.[[Kind]] is object, then
      1. If value is not an Object, return false.
      2. For each Property Type Record p of t.[[Properties]], do
        1. Let present be ? HasProperty(value, p's key).
        2. If present is false, then
          1. If p is not optional, return false.
        3. Else,
          1. Let pv be ? Get(value, p's key).
          2. If ? IsOfType(pv, p's type) is false, return false.
      3. Return true.
    8. If t.[[Kind]] is array or t.[[Kind]] is tuple, then
      1. If value is not an Object, return false.
      2. Return ? the result of checking value's length and elements against t (1.13.1).
    9. If t.[[Kind]] is function, then
      1. If IsCallable(value) is false, return false.
      2. Return IsAssignable(RuntimeTypeOf(value), t).
    10. Return IsSubtype(RuntimeTypeOf(value), t, « »).
     Note 1

    Deciding a structural type reads the value's properties, so the check calls user code: a property backed by a getter runs it, which may throw, may have side effects, and may return a different value each time it is read. A check is therefore not a passive observation, and this is why the operation returns a completion. A getter that returns a value of the required type once and a different value afterwards defeats the check, which is the same aliasing exposure noted below. Whether a boundary check should read through accessors at all, or inspect only own data properties, is settled as reading them: a property's type is a claim about what the property yields, and an interface implemented by accessors, which is how interfaces are routinely implemented, would otherwise be unverifiable at exactly the boundary that exists to verify it.

    The final step is the general case: the value's runtime type is compared against the target with the subtype relation of 1.2.3.1, so the runtime test and the static one agree by construction. The earlier steps exist because a structural type cannot be decided by comparing two Type Records: an ordinary Object's runtime type is a description of the properties it happens to have, and the question is whether it has the ones the target requires.

     Note 2

    The function case is checked shallowly and is unsound, and this is the one place where the specification knowingly permits a type violation to go unreported. A function's signature cannot be verified without calling it, so the check confirms only that the value is callable and that the signature it declares is assignable to the target, which admits any. An untyped function reaching a (uint8) => uint8 position therefore passes, and if it returns a String the caller receives one where its static type promised a uint8.

    This is the higher-order problem every gradual type system meets, and the design does not address it. The available answers are the known ones. The value can be wrapped so that each call checks its arguments and its result, which is sound and is what a contract system does, at the cost of an allocation per boundary crossing, a change to object identity, and a blame mechanism to say which side broke the contract. The check can stay shallow and the unsoundness be accepted, as an optional type system does, at the cost of the guarantee. Or a check can be inserted at each call through a binding whose runtime signature has an any where its static type does not, which is a wrapper by another name but pays only where the imprecision is. The choice bears on the proposal's central claim, since a type the engine cannot rely on is a type the engine cannot optimize against. This specification accepts the third: the boundary checks the shape, and the hole through a higher-order crossing is documented rather than papered over. Wrapping breaks the identity interning makes load-bearing, f === f across a boundary, and taxes every crossing to police a rare one; blame is machinery without precedent in the language. A program that needs the guarantee declares its functions, and nothing this proposal promises elsewhere is withdrawn, since the layout premise never extended inside a function value.

    The object case is checked at the boundary but not afterwards, and has the same shape of problem. A plain Object's properties are untyped unless declared with the { (a: uint8): 1 } form of the object typing section, so an aliased write through an untyped reference can invalidate a check that has already passed. 1.10.1 settles whether a boundary imposes the target's property types on the object it accepts: it does not.

    1.6.3 The Boundary Check

    At a boundary where a value of the any type reaches a position of a known type, the value is checked and, if it is a numeric value that the target can represent exactly, converted. A value the target cannot represent raises an error.

    1.6.3.1 RequireType ( value, t )

    The abstract operation RequireType takes arguments value (an ECMAScript language value) and t (a Type Record) and returns either a normal completion containing an ECMAScript language value or a throw completion. It enforces the type t on value at a boundary, returning the value of the type t to be used. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If ? IsOfType(value, t) is true, return value.
    2. If t is a numeric type and RuntimeTypeOf(value) is a numeric type, then
      1. Let r be the result of converting value to t by Table 9, except that a conversion that would wrap, truncate toward zero, or round a finite value to an infinity instead yields unrepresentable.
      2. If r is unrepresentable, throw a RangeError exception.
      3. Return r.
    3. If t is string or boolean, return ? PrimitiveConvert(value, t).
    4. Throw a TypeError exception.
     Note

    The two failures are different and are reported differently. A numeric value at a numeric type has a conversion available and the only question is whether this value survives it, which is a question of range: hence the RangeError. A value of any other type has no numeric conversion to attempt, which is a question of type: hence the TypeError, and it is raised before any coercion is tried. Coercing first and then range-checking the result would make an annotation a coercion rather than a check: it would accept a string, a Boolean, null, and any object with a valueOf, and at a float type, where every value is in range, it could not fail at all, so a missing field would become a NaN that surfaces far from the annotation that admitted it.

    This is why a string is not a source here even when it reads as a number. '5' at a uint8 is refused and the program writes uint8.parse('5') or uint8.tryParse('5'), which is the same position 1.12.5 states, and which distinguishes a string that is not a literal of the type from one that is a literal out of range.

    The three ways a numeric value may fail to fit a type are distinguished, and each is treated according to what the program said.

    Note

    The boundary converts rather than merely checking, which is what makes untyped data usable: a Number parsed from JSON reaching a uint8 field is a Number, not a uint8, and a check that demanded it already be a uint8 would reject every value that ever crossed the boundary. Converting only where the target can represent the value exactly, and raising a RangeError otherwise, keeps the loss from being silent. This is the same distinction the checked arithmetic of Math.addChecked draws against the wrapping of +.

    The rule above permits a float target to round, since it treats only wrapping, truncation, and overflow to an infinity as unrepresentable, so a Number 0.1 reaching a float32 boundary becomes the nearest float32. The alternative, demanding exactness, would reject almost every float that crossed a boundary and is not usable. This matches the treatment of a literal, which also rounds into a float and errors only outside the range, and it is recorded here because it is a choice rather than a consequence.

    1.6.4 Where Checks Are Inserted

    A check is inserted where a value whose static type is any reaches a position whose type is not, and nowhere else. Table 13 lists the positions.

    Note

    The list is the same list of boundaries at which a user-defined conversion is consulted (1.5.5) and at which the metadata protocol of the primitive metadata extension applies, and at which a where clause that cannot be decided statically is evaluated. This is not a coincidence: a boundary is where a value acquires a type it did not have, and every mechanism that has something to say about that acquisition says it there.

    1.6.5 Elision of Checks

    A check is required only where the static types do not already establish the result. It is not inserted, and an implementation need not perform one, in the following circumstances.

    • The static type of the value is not any and is assignable to the target. The result is established statically, and any violation was an Early Error.
    • The target is the any type. Every value is a value of it.
    • The value's static type and the target are the same type.
    • The index of a read or write of a fixed-length [N].<T> is known to be below N, because N is a compile-time constant and the index is a value generic, a where-constrained parameter, or the counter of a for over a range with that bound. The bound is proven statically and no check is performed.
    Note

    In a fully typed program every check above is elided, and what remains at the boundaries of a partly typed one reduces to a tag test on a sealed typed class. This is the property the proposal's premise rests on: a check the engine can discharge statically is not a cost, and the type is available to the engine as a fact rather than a hint. A program that is entirely untyped inserts no checks either, since it has no typed positions. The cost falls where the boundary is, in proportion to how mixed the program is.

    1.6.6 Typed Storage

    A class in which at least one public or private field is typed is automatically sealed, as if PreventExtensions had been performed on each of its instances, and its prototype is frozen. A field may be written, since a field's type is what constrains it, but a property may not be added or removed, and the prototype may not be modified.

    A store to a typed field or a typed element checks the value against the field's or element's declared type, by Table 13. Deleting a typed field, a typed element, or a member required by an implemented interface throws a TypeError exception.

    A class whose fields are typed may opt out with the dynamic modifier, which leaves it unsealed. Such a class cannot be used for sequential data and cannot be a value type in a typed array.

    Note

    Sealing is what makes a typed field's type a fact about the layout rather than a claim about the initializer. Without it a typed class would be a class whose fields happen to have been assigned values of the right type so far, which is not something an engine can lay out contiguously or an offset can be computed from. The memory layout extension depends on this, and it is the reason sealing is automatic rather than requested: a field's type and a reallocatable object are not compatible, so the type implies the seal.

    1.6.7 The instanceof Operator

    A Type Object has a %Symbol.hasInstance% method. When called with argument v, it returns ? IsOfType(v, the Type Object's [[TypeRecord]]).

    Note

    instanceof therefore extends to every type of this proposal through the protocol the language already has, and needs no new operator. a instanceof uint8 is true for a uint8 binding and a instanceof uint16 is false, a fixed-length array is an instance of the corresponding dynamic-length array type, and a class is unaffected, since a class's type object is its constructor and the prototype chain semantics apply as they do today. A successful check narrows the static type in the branch it guards, which 1.7.5 specifies.

    1.6.8 Typed catch Clauses

    A catch clause may name the type it handles, and a try may then carry more than one. The productions below restate their ECMAScript definitions (1.1).

    TryStatement[Yield, Await, Return] : try Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Catch[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] try Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] CatchClauses[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] try Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Finally[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] try Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Catch[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Finally[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] try Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] CatchClauses[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Finally[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] CatchClauses[Yield, Await, Return] : Catch[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] CatchClauses[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Catch[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] Catch[Yield, Await, Return] : catch ( CatchParameter[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt ) Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return] catch Block[?Yield, ?Await, ?Return]

    It is a type error if a Catch other than the last of its CatchClauses has no TypeAnnotation. When a value v is thrown, the clauses are tried in order, and the first runs whose declared type v satisfies, in the sense in which RequireType (1.6.3.1) would accept v at that type; a Catch with no annotation satisfies every value. Within a clause the binding's Static Type is the declared type, the narrowing of 1.7.5 applied to the catch binding. A value no clause accepts propagates as if the try had no catch: typed clauses filter, they do not swallow, so the completion is rethrown after Finally, when present, runs as it does today.

    Note

    This is the design's error handling document made normative: ordered dispatch, first match, an untyped tail for the rest. A rejected promise reaches a typed clause through await unchanged, since await rethrows the rejection reason and the dispatch above neither knows nor cares that the throw crossed an async boundary. The errors this proposal itself raises, RangeError at a checked numeric conversion and at an out-of-bounds access, TypeError at a dynamic boundary, are ordinary values here, so catch (e: RangeError) handles every range failure in one clause (design, errorhandling.md).

    1.7 Declarations

    This clause gives meaning to the annotations the grammar attached to bindings in 1.3.4. It specifies what a typed binding holds before it is assigned, what a typed initializer infers, what an assignment to a typed binding does, the declaration that names a type, and the narrowing by which a test refines the type of a binding in the branch it guards.

    1.7.1 Type Alias Declarations

    A type alias declaration names a type. It is added to the declaration forms.

    Declaration[Yield, Await] : HoistableDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await, ~Default] ClassDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await, ~Default] LexicalDeclaration[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] TypeAliasDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await] TypeAliasDeclaration[Yield, Await] : type [no LineTerminator here] BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt = Type[?Yield, ?Await] WhereClauses[?Yield, ?Await]opt ;

    A type alias declaration with no TypeParameters creates an immutable binding for its BindingIdentifier, initialized to GetTypeObject of the Type Record its Type denotes. The name is therefore usable in expression position, where it evaluates to that Type Object, and in type position, where it denotes the type. A declaration with TypeParameters creates no binding and is usable only in type position, since a generic alias denotes a type for each list of arguments rather than one type.

    Note

    type Point = { x: float32, y: float32 }; is therefore close to a const whose value is a type object, and the two spellings agree: Point === type { x: float32, y: float32 } is true, because Type Objects are interned (1.2.2.6). What the declaration adds over the const is that the name is also usable where a type is expected. An alias is transparent: it introduces no new type, so Point and its expansion are the same type and are mutually assignable, and a nominal type is created only by a class, an interface, or an enum.

    An alias may refer to itself, directly or through other aliases, provided every cycle passes through a position that holds a reference rather than an inline layout: a member written T | null, the element of a dynamic array, or a field of a sealed class. It is a type error if a cycle never does, since the type would demand an infinite inline layout, which is the same rule 1.10.2 applies to a value type class containing itself. The fixpoint of 1.2.5.3 validates a computed cycle by this rule.

    The [no LineTerminator here] restriction is what keeps type a contextual keyword. type followed by a line terminator and then an identifier is two statements today, an expression statement naming type and another naming the identifier, and it continues to be. This is the same device the design relies on throughout, and it is why type X = 1; on one line is unambiguous: it is a syntax error today.

    Whether a generic alias may be exported, and how export type Point = ... interacts with the module's binding namespace, is left to the modules clause. A generic alias creating no binding is a decision worth revisiting: it means Grown in type Grown<T> = [...Row, T]; cannot be reflected on, while a non-generic alias can be.

    1.7.2 Typed Bindings

    var, let, and const accept the same annotations. A binding declared with a TypeAnnotation has the annotated type; a binding declared without one has the any type. The type of a binding is fixed at its declaration and does not change with what is assigned to it.

    A binding declared with a type and an Initializer is initialized to the value of the initializer, checked against the declared type by the rules of 1.5: the check is static where the initializer's type is known, and is RequireType where the initializer has the any type. A binding declared with a type and no Initializer is initialized to the default value of that type (1.7.3) rather than to undefined.

    A const declaration without an Initializer remains a Syntax Error, whether or not the binding carries a TypeAnnotation.

    Assignment to a binding of declared type t checks the assigned value against t by the same rules. The temporal dead zone is unchanged: a let or const binding is uninitialized until its declaration is evaluated, and accessing it before then is a ReferenceError. The default value applies at the declaration, not before it.

    Note

    That a typed binding takes its type's default rather than undefined is the point at which a type stops being a claim about a program and becomes a fact about storage. let a: uint32; holds a uint32, so every read of it yields a uint32 and the engine may lay it out as four bytes; were it undefined until assigned, its type would be uint32 | undefined in all but name, and the layout would have to carry a bit saying which. This is the same reason a typed field takes its type's default, and the two rules are one rule.

    1.7.3 Default Values

    1.7.3.1 DefaultValueOf ( t )

    The abstract operation DefaultValueOf takes argument t (a Type Record) and returns either a value of the type t or none. It returns the value a binding or field of the type t holds before it is assigned, or none if t has no default. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If t.[[Kind]] is any, return undefined.
    2. If t is a numeric type, return the value of t representing 0.
    3. If t is the String type, return the empty String.
    4. If t is the Boolean type, return false.
    5. If t is the type named "undefined", return undefined.
    6. If t is the type named "null", return null.
    7. If t.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. If t.[[Members]] contains the type named "null", return null.
      2. If t.[[Members]] contains the type named "undefined", return undefined.
      3. Return none.
    8. If t.[[Kind]] is array, then
      1. If t.[[Extent]] is dynamic, return a new empty array of the type t.
      2. Let d be DefaultValueOf(t.[[Element]]).
      3. If d is none, return none.
      4. Return a new array of the type t whose t.[[Extent]] elements are each d.
    9. If t.[[Kind]] is tuple, then
      1. For each element e of t.[[Elements]], do
        1. If e.[[Rest]] is false and e.[[Initial]] is none and DefaultValueOf(e.[[Type]]) is none, return none.
      2. Return a new tuple of the type t whose elements are, for each element of t.[[Elements]] whose [[Rest]] is false and in order, its [[Initial]] where that is not none and the default value of its [[Type]] otherwise.
    10. If t.[[Kind]] is parameterized, then
      1. Let d be DefaultValueOf(t.[[Base]]).
      2. If d is none, return none.
      3. If the metadata's meta type defines a validation judgment and that judgment does not hold of d and t.[[Metadata]], return none.
      4. Return d.
    11. If t.[[Kind]] is nominal and t denotes a value type class, return the instance of t each of whose fields holds the default of the field's type, or none if any field's type has no default.
    12. Return none.

    It is a type error to declare a binding or a field with a type t and no initializer when DefaultValueOf(t) is none.

    Every default above is a zero: the numeric zero, the empty String, false, null, and an array or an aggregate whose storage is zero-filled. Zero-filling is part of the semantics and not an optimization a program may opt out of, and no form of this proposal allocates without it. What is filled for an aggregate is its layout (9), padding included, so two instances of one value type class that are === field by field are also equal byte for byte.

    Note

    The reason is security rather than convenience. An allocation that exposed the bytes of a previously freed one would leak whatever had been there: another script's data, a former secret, or a heap pointer that defeats address space layout randomization. A fresh page from the operating system is already zero, so the fill costs nothing on a large new allocation, and reused memory must be cleared regardless, so there is nothing to gain by exposing it. The one pattern an uninitialized form would serve, allocate and then fill from input, is served by placement new over an existing buffer and by array views.

    A type with no default is one for which no zero is meaningful, and the operation reports that rather than inventing one. A union of two non-nullable types is the ordinary case: let x: uint8 | string; has two candidate zeros and no reason to prefer either, so it requires an initializer. symbol, object, a function type, and a class whose construction must run are the others.

    The design states the defaults for the numeric, SIMD, String, Boolean, nullable, and array types and does not reach the rest, so the boundaries above are supplied here and need review. Three deserve attention. A bigint is given the zero 0n on the same reasoning as the other numeric types. A tuple defaults element-wise, which follows from a tuple of value types being laid out contiguously, but a tuple with a defaulted trailing position already has an initializer for that position, which 1.13.2 reconciles. A value type class defaults field-wise, which is what makes let d: [10].<A>; hold ten zero-filled instances as the memory layout extension requires, but it means a class can be brought into existence without its constructor running, which 1.10.2 endorses.

    1.7.4 Typed Initializers

    A binding declared with a TypedInitializer takes the type of its initializer rather than the any type. Its declared type is the Static Type of the AssignmentExpression, and it is initialized to that expression's value.

    Note

    let a := new MyType(); declares a of type MyType, where let a = new MyType(); would declare it of the any type. The two spellings differ in what may be assigned afterwards, not in what is stored: the first rejects a = 5, the second permits it. := therefore removes the redundancy of let a: MyType = new MyType(); without introducing inference anywhere the program did not ask for it, which is why it is a distinct operator rather than a rule that an initialized binding infers its type. An unannotated binding remaining any is what keeps an untyped program untyped.

    It is a type error if the Static Type of the initializer is the any type, since let a := f(); where f is untyped would declare a binding of the any type through a syntax that asks for a type.

    1.7.5 Narrowing

    A test that establishes something about a value's type refines the Static Type of that value in the branch the test guards. The Static Type of a reference to a binding at a given point is its declared type refined by the narrowing facts that hold there.

    v above is a reference to a binding or a chain of property accesses over one, a path, and s is its Static Type where the test occurs. A narrowing fact holds in any position the test governs, not only in the body of an if: the two branches of a conditional expression are narrowed by its condition, the right operand of && is narrowed by the truth of its left operand, and the right operand of || by the falsity of its left. A fact about a path holds until the path or a prefix of it is assigned a value whose type is not within the narrowed type. == and != narrow as === and !== do wherever the Static Types of both operands make the two agree, which a union of literal types over one base always does, so the design's this.country == 'US' narrows and a comparison that could coerce does not.

    Note 1

    Facts reach paths because the types this proposal narrows are about fields as much as bindings, and if (addr.country == 'US') is the design's own example. Assignment to a prefix discards a fact about everything under it, which is the design's account of a mode reassigned after a name was narrowed by it. What does not discard a fact is an intervening call that could reach the path, and that is the same shallow reading of a function value that 1.6.2.1 takes and the same hole, admitted in the same place: the boundary check is the backstop, since a value that has drifted out of its type is caught where it next crosses (Table 13).

    1.7.5.1 The Canonical Total Order

    The total order over canonical Type Records that CanonicalizeType invokes is fixed as follows, so that the member order a program observes through reflection is the same on every host. Records of different [[Kind]] order by the row order of Table 1. Records of one kind order by their fields in that table's order, compared as: a Type Record recursively by this order; a List element-wise and then shorter first; a String by code units; a Number by numeric value with -0𝔽 before +0𝔽 and NaN greatest; a BigInt by value; a Boolean with false before true; a Symbol by the order in which each is first interned into a Type Record within the surrounding Agent; a function, as an application's [[Builder]], by the source position of its declaration, module before offset; and a metadata value by the structural comparison of 1.2.5.2 with its leaves ordered by these same rules. Where one field holds values of two different ECMAScript language types, as a literal's [[Value]] does, they order by type, in the order String, Number, BigInt, Boolean, Symbol.

    Note

    The order is arbitrary and load-bearing at once: nothing about it matters except that every host computes the same one, because canonical order is observable, a union's arms reflect in it and a builder that turns a union into a tuple inherits it, and the determinism invariant of 1.2.5.4 therefore reaches it. A builder wanting a different order sorts. The order among Symbols is Agent-local, because a Symbol is, and it is stable within a program because the same source interns the same symbols in the same sequence; a Symbol never reaches a comparison in another Agent, since a type carrying one cannot be shared.

    1.7.5.2 NarrowTo ( s, t )

    The abstract operation NarrowTo takes arguments s (a Type Record) and t (a Type Record) and returns either a Type Record or empty. It returns the type of a value known to be of the type s and also of the type t, or empty if no value can be of both. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If s.[[Kind]] is any, return t.
    2. If s.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. Let kept be a new empty List.
      2. For each element m of s.[[Members]], do
        1. If IsSubtype(m, t, « ») is true, append m to kept.
        2. Else if IsSubtype(t, m, « ») is true, append t to kept.
      3. If kept is empty, return empty.
      4. Return CanonicalizeType(the Type Record { [[Kind]]: union, [[Members]]: kept }).
    3. If IsSubtype(s, t, « ») is true, return s.
    4. If IsSubtype(t, s, « ») is true, return t.
    5. If s and t have no common values, return empty.
    6. Return CanonicalizeType(the Type Record { [[Kind]]: intersection, [[Members]]: « s, t » }).
     Note

    Two distinct primitive types have no common values, since each value belongs to exactly one of them (1.4.1), and neither does a primitive type share values with an object or function type. Two object types, or two nominal types that are interfaces, may have common values, since a class may satisfy both, so an intersection of those is inhabited and is returned. This is the step that yields empty for d ?? 5 where d is a uint8: the narrowed type would be the intersection of uint8 and null | undefined, which has no values.
