Where a row's result is exact, an implementation must produce it exactly at every width; an integer row computed through a binary64 intermediate that returns a wrong integer carrying the type does not conform. Where a row is implementation-approximated, the approximation is computed as if at the argument type's format or better and rounded once to that format. Math.sqrt is correctly rounded at every binary float and decimal width, as IEEE 754-2019 requires of the operation; the other approximated rows carry the latitude the Math functions have always had.

    NaN and the infinities are values of every binary float type and of every decimal type, and a NaN or infinite result at those types is a value of T, not an error: Math.log((-1 := float32)) is the float32 NaN. The integer and rational families have no such values, which is why their domain cases above throw instead. The existing conventions of the functions, among them the NaN and signed-zero handling of min and max and Math.round rounding half up, are unchanged and are evaluated at T.

    A family has a row for a function exactly where the operation has an answer in that family's own mathematics, with the return type leaving nothing to decide about the answer's representation. The integer roots are such answers: for an integer T, Math.sqrt and Math.cbrt return the exact root truncated toward zero as a value of T, the direction integer division truncates (1.8.4) and the semantics the BigInt Math proposal gives BigInt.sqrt and BigInt.cbrt. The transcendental functions have no integer counterpart, so they have no integer rows, and an integer-typed argument to one fails resolution; the program writes the conversion it means.

    The listing declares one signature per parameter list: no function of the numeric library is overloaded on its return type alone, so a bare call is never ambiguous (1.9.5 remains available to operators and to user code, where a call without a contextual type is legitimately an error). A contextual type participates in resolution as it does anywhere. Where a call's numeric arguments are untyped literals, the contextual type selects a typed signature and the literals take its parameter type, so the call is a computation at T rather than a conversion of a Number result: const a: uint8 = Math.pow(2, 10); resolves at uint8, computes the exact 1024, and throws a RangeError exception at the return, while uint8(Math.pow(2, 10)) is the untyped call followed by a wrapping cast and is 0, the distinction of Table 12 visible at one call site. Where an argument is typed and the contextual type is of another family, no signature is viable and it is a type error; the cast the program then writes selects the computation by its placement, float64(Math.sqrt(10n)) being the integer root converted, exactly 3, and Math.sqrt(float64(10n)) the real root of the converted argument. A cast constrains nothing here: a Type Object call accepts any numeric source, so it supplies no contextual type and a call within it resolves bare.

    The complex numbers extension declares the rows of its family beside its arithmetic: Math.abs of a complex.<T> is the real magnitude, a value of T; the transcendental functions the extension names return values of its types; and Math.conj and Math.arg are its additions. Math.random takes no numeric argument, and its typed form Math.random.<T>() belongs to the random extension. The value properties of the Math object, Math.PI among them, remain values of the Number type.

    The Static Type of a member access reading the length property of an array is uint32 (1.2.4). The value read is unchanged, a Number, and no conversion is applied at run time.

    Note 1

    This is what makes the conversion rule livable rather than merely strict. Math.clz32 of a uint32 is a uint32, Math.min of two float32 values is a float32, and a uint32 loop counter compares against array.length without a cast, because the library meets the program where it is. A library that returned a Number from every function would force a conversion at every call and would make typed code read worse than untyped code, which is the failure mode the design is avoiding.

    The existing signatures are unchanged, so Math.min of two Numbers is a Number and every existing program means what it meant. The overloads are additions, and a call with untyped literal arguments and no contextual type selects among them by Table 11, which ranks number first and so keeps an untyped call on the signature it selects today.

     Note 2

    The bigint rows are those of the BigInt Math proposal: abs, sign, min, max, pow, sqrt, and cbrt, the roots truncated toward zero, sqrt of a negative throwing, and pow refusing a negative exponent. That proposal places the operations on the BigInt constructor rather than on Math, because in the base language a Math.sqrt whose result type depends on a runtime argument is a hazard every caller inherits. The objection does not transfer: here a signature is selected statically and the untyped surface is untouched, so the listing stays on Math, agreeing with the proposal value for value, and if the proposal advances, BigInt.sqrt(x) and Math.sqrt of a bigint are one mathematics with two spellings.

    The integer roots follow the shape integer division set. The result type leaves nothing to decide, so the type decides, and toward zero is the direction this specification's integer arithmetic already rounds: Math.sqrt((17 := uint8)) is (4 := uint8), and Math.cbrt of a negative integer value is defined, the cube root of -9 truncating to -2. A program that wants the real root of an integer writes the conversion, one cast that states the meaning.

     Note 3

    Math.pow and ** deliberately part ways at an integer type. (2 := uint8) ** (10 := uint8) wraps to 0, because the operators wrap (1.8.4); Math.pow((2 := uint8), (10 := uint8)) throws a RangeError exception, because a declared return is a checked boundary. This is the a + 1 against Math.addChecked(a, 1) distinction of 1.12.2 again, stated here so it reads as the design it is rather than as a disagreement: the operator is the cheap wrapping form and the function the checked one. What ** itself does with a negative typed exponent is a question for 1.8.4; this clause specifies the functions only.

    1.12.2 Checked and Saturating Arithmetic

    The operators wrap on overflow (1.8.4). Where wrapping is not wanted, the named forms of Table 21 are used. Each is overloaded for every integer type and returns a value of its operands' type.

    Each form performs the operation its corresponding operator performs, and differs from it only in what happens when the type cannot represent the answer. "The exact result" in Table 21 therefore means the exact result of THAT operation, not of an operation in the rationals: the division forms divide as / divides, truncating toward zero (1.8.4), so Math.divChecked(_a_, 2) on an int32 holding 7 is 3 and not an error. A division is the only one of the four whose result can fail to be representable for a reason other than magnitude, and it does so in exactly one case, the most negative value of a signed type divided by -1.

    A zero divisor produces no result at all, so neither treatment applies to it: Math.divChecked and Math.divSaturating both throw a RangeError exception when the divisor is zero. Saturating is a rule about a result the type cannot hold, and there is no result here to be nearest to.

    Note

    Silent wrapping is the wrong answer when the value is a count rather than a bit pattern, and the right answer when it is a bit pattern rather than a count. Neither is right always, so the operator takes the case that has to be cheap and the named forms take the rest: a + 1 on a uint8 holding 255 is 0, Math.addChecked(a, 1) throws a RangeError exception, and Math.addSaturating(a, 1) is 255.

    These forms exist for the integer types because only there is wrapping the default. A float already saturates, to an infinity, and a decimal already raises a RangeError, since a decimal's range is a property of the type rather than of the format (Table 9). A rational already raises a RangeError on an unrepresentable result. Each family's overflow behaviour is therefore the one its values make available, and the named forms give the integer types the two the others have built in.

    That a saturating form can still raise is worth stating plainly, because the name suggests otherwise. It raises for one reason only, and it is not an overflow: a division by zero has no result, and saturating chooses among the results a type can hold rather than inventing one where none exists. Every case that IS an overflow saturates, including the signed division that overflows, so Math.divSaturating of the most negative int8 by -1 is 127.

    1.12.3 Floored Division

    Math.divFloor(_a_, _b_) returns the exact quotient of a and b rounded toward negative infinity. Math.mod(_a_, _b_) returns the remainder whose sign follows the divisor. Both are overloaded for every integer type, and both also have a signature over the Number type.

    A zero divisor follows the family. At an integer type both throw a RangeError exception, since an integer type has no infinity and no NaN and so has nothing to return. Over the Number type both answer as the operators do: Math.mod(_a_, 0) is NaN, as _a_ % 0 is, and Math.divFloor(_a_, 0) is an infinity, as Math.floor(_a_ / 0) is.

    Note

    These are the other half of the pair 1.8.4 describes: Math.divFloor(_a_, _b_) * _b_ + Math.mod(_a_, _b_) = _a_ holds for the same reason the operators' identity holds, because the quotient and the remainder round the same way. They are named rather than assumed so that a program picks the pair it wants instead of receiving one by default.

    Unlike the checked and saturating forms, this pair is not restricted to the integer types. Those exist only there because only there is wrapping the default, and a checked form over the Number type would have nothing to check. This pair is not about overflow but about rounding direction, which is meaningful for any real, and its own motivating use is written in untyped code: array[Math.mod(_i_, _array_.length)].

    Math.mod is the % of Python, the mod of Kotlin, and the mod of Haskell. For a positive divisor, which is every case in which an index is being wrapped, its result equals the Euclidean remainder and is never negative, so array[Math.mod(i, array.length)] is always in range. Where the divisor may be negative and a non-negative result is still wanted, Math.mod(_a_, Math.abs(_b_)) gives it, which is why no separate Euclidean pair is provided.

    1.12.4 Counting Leading Zeros

    Math.clz is overloaded for the sized integer types, and for them alone. For a value of an integer type T of width N, it returns N less the bit length of the argument's mathematical value taken modulo 2**_N_, as a value of _T_, checked at the return as 1.12.1 checks every declared return. The operation is a question about an N-bit two's-complement encoding, so the families without one have no signatures: bigint has no width to count from the top of, and the float, decimal, and rational types have no integer encoding to count in. There is no untyped signature; Math.clz32 is already the 32-bit count.

    Note

    This is Math.clz32 freed from its width. The two agree at 32 bits, answering alike for every int32 and uint32, and differ below it: Math.clz((1 := uint8)) is 7 where Math.clz32((1 := uint8)) is 31, because clz32 counts in a 32-bit field whatever its argument's width and clz counts in the argument's own. Typed code that means the argument's width, which is most of it, writes clz; clz32 remains for the 32-bit field itself and for untyped callers.

    The count of a value of T always fits an unsigned T, and fits a signed T at every width past int.<2>; at the two narrowest signed widths a zero's count exceeds the type's greatest value and the checked return throws a RangeError exception, the same edge the clz32 row of Table 20 notes. bigint is excluded because the operation is width-relative and a bigint has no width; the width-free counterpart is a bit length, which is a different function and is not added here.

    A call whose argument is an untyped literal resolves like any other, by Table 11, which selects the widest width that can represent it. That is well defined and almost never meant, since the width is the semantics here, so a program writes the width it means, by a typed argument, by an annotation on the destination, or by a cast: const n: uint8 = Math.clz(1); is 7.

    1.12.5 Parsing

    Each numeric type has a parse function. For an integer type and for bigint its signature is parse(_string_, _radix_ = 10); for the binary floating-point, decimal, rational, and complex types it is parse(_string_). It returns a value of the type the function belongs to.

    The accepted input is the grammar of a literal of that type, with optional leading and trailing white space and an optional sign. Numeric separators are accepted, and the form taking a radix accepts the prefix matching that radix. The entire string must be a literal of the type: no trailing text is consumed.

    parse throws a SyntaxError when the string is not a literal of the type, and a RangeError when it is a literal whose value the type cannot represent.

    Each type also has a tryParse function with the same parameters, returning a value of the type where parse would return one and null where parse would fail to parse its argument. Its return type is the union of the type and the null type. A call that is not a parse at all, because the receiver is not a numeric type, throws as parse does rather than answering null: that is a mistake in the program, and reporting it as a failed parse would name the wrong thing.

    Note

    parseInt and parseFloat are unchanged and continue to consume a prefix and to return NaN. The new functions do neither, because neither is available to them: an integer type cannot represent NaN at all, so a failed parse has nothing to return and must throw. Doing the same for the float types is a choice rather than a necessity, and it is the right one, since a failed parse that becomes a NaN surfaces far from its cause. Refusing trailing text follows: a function that cannot signal failure with a value has no reason to be lenient about what it accepts.

    Distinguishing the two errors is what makes the failure legible. uint8.parse('12abc') throws a SyntaxError exception, since the string is not a uint8 literal, while uint8.parse('256') throws a RangeError exception, since it is a literal and the value is out of range. This is the same distinction Table 12 draws between a value of the wrong type and a value of the right type out of range.

    tryParse returning a nullable union rather than a sentinel is what lets the failure be handled by narrowing (1.7.5): the result is a uint8 in the branch that tests it against null and needs no cast there. A string is deliberately not a conversion source for a numeric type, so let c: uint8 = '1'; is a type error and the parse is always written; 1.6.3.1 is where that is enforced, and it is enforced at the explicit conversion too, so '1' := uint8 is a type error for the same reason. The pair is what makes the strictness livable: a program that knows its input writes parse and lets the failure throw, and one that does not writes tryParse and handles the null.

    1.12.6 The Numeric Predicates

    The global functions isFinite and isNaN, and the functions Number.isFinite, Number.isNaN, Number.isInteger, and Number.isSafeInteger, are overloaded for the numeric types by 1.9.3. Each overload takes one value of a numeric type and returns a boolean, answering as Table 22 gives for the value's family. The existing signatures are unchanged: an untyped call coerces, or declines to, exactly as it does today.

    The bigint family is the one exception to the table's uniformity, and it is a compatibility rule rather than a semantic one: an overload taking a bigint is declared only for a function that has no defined answer for a BigInt today. isNaN and isFinite perform ToNumber on their argument, which throws a TypeError exception for a BigInt, so each declares one. The four Number statics already answer false for a BigInt, because a BigInt is not a Number, so none of them declares one and a BigInt passed to a Number static reaches that static's existing signature and is answered exactly as it is today.

    The complex numbers extension declares its family's rows for all four predicates. The expected shape is the componentwise one: a complex value is NaN where either component is, and finite where both are.

    Note

    The integer and rational columns are constants, and that is the point of them: a sized integer is a type whose isFinite has one answer, and the predicate saying so at the type is what lets a reader delete the check. The decimal columns are value tests even though the arithmetic of this specification never produces a decimal NaN or infinity, overflow raising a RangeError exception instead: the values exist in the type (1.4.4), so the predicate tests the value rather than asserting a constant the type system does not guarantee.

    Every test is of the mathematical or format value, never of a representation. At a width past 2*53, `Number.isSafeInteger` of an `int64` holding 260 is *false because the value is out of safe range, not because a payload lost precision.

    A plain BigInt value is a value of the bigint type, and it raises the one compatibility question in this table that the type system does not settle by itself. Every other row answers about a value that no existing program can write: a float32 NaN and an int32 need syntax this proposal adds, so giving them answers displaces nothing. A BigInt needs no new syntax at all. Number.isInteger(5n) is false in code running today, and making it true under the flag would change the meaning of a program that opted into nothing, which nothing else in this proposal does. The rows are therefore declared where they displace an error and declined where they displace an answer: isNaN(1n) and isFinite(1n) throw today, so the flag defines them, and the Number statics answer today, so the flag leaves them alone.

    The visible cost is that isFinite(1n) is true while Number.isFinite(1n) is false. The two functions differ in kind already, one coercing its argument and the other testing it, and this proposal closes only the case where the coercion threw; it does not make them agree, and it makes them disagree about no value that either answered before. Number.isInteger and Number.isSafeInteger have no global counterpart, so no surface takes a BigInt for those two questions at all. Their answers at the bigint type, were a surface for them to be declared on to exist, are true and true where the magnitude is at most 2**53 - 1; they are recorded here rather than in the table because a table of what the predicates answer should not carry an answer no call can obtain.

    The other rows repair a quieter hazard: without them, Number.isNaN of a float32 NaN is false and Number.isInteger of an int32 is false, each predicate silently testing representation where its name promises a value test.

    1.13 Arrays and Tuples

    This clause completes the array and tuple kinds: the membership test 1.6.2.1 defers, the default value of a tuple, and the checking of an element store.

    A Tuple Element Record describes one position of a tuple type. It has a [[Type]] (a Type Record), a [[Rest]] field (a Boolean, true for a ... element), and an [[Initial]] field (an ECMAScript language value or none, the position's declared default).

    1.13.1 Membership

    For an array Type Record t, a value v is of the type t when v is an Object, v's length is t.[[Extent]] where that is not dynamic, and every element of v is of the type t.[[Element]].

    For a tuple Type Record t, a value v is of the type t when v is an Object, v's length is at least the number of elements of t.[[Elements]] whose [[Rest]] is false and [[Initial]] is none and that no [[Rest]] element precedes, at most the element count where no element's [[Rest]] is true and unbounded otherwise, and every element of v is of the [[Type]] of the element of t.[[Elements]] that receives it, a [[Rest]] element receiving every remainder.

    Note

    A tuple's length is a range rather than a number because a trailing position may carry a default and a position may spread an array type. [uint8, uint32 = 10] accepts a one-element and a two-element array, which is what lets a shorter array satisfy a longer tuple return, and [uint32, ...[].<float32>] accepts any length from one upward.

    Checking every element is linear in the array's length, which is the cost the any boundary has for an array and does not have for a uint8. It falls only where an array of unknown type reaches a typed position; an array whose static type is already the target is not checked at all (1.6.5), and a [].<uint8> passed to a [].<uint8> parameter costs nothing. Whether an implementation may instead check an element on each read, deferring the cost and spreading it, is the same question the object and function kinds raise, and it has the same answer here as there: the check as specified is eager, and a lazier one would need the wrapper the higher-order case would need.

    1.13.2 Defaults and Stores

    The default value of a tuple type is the array whose elements are, for each element of [[Elements]] whose [[Rest]] is false, its [[Initial]] where that is not none and the default value of its [[Type]] otherwise; a [[Rest]] element contributes no elements. It is none where an element has an [[Initial]] of none and a [[Type]] with no default.

    A store to an element of an array of element type t checks the value against t by Table 13. length is a uint32. Deleting an element of a typed array throws a TypeError exception.

    Note

    This reconciles the two rules 1.7.3 leaves in tension. A tuple defaults element-wise, but an element that carries a default already has a value for its position, so the initializer is used where there is one and the type's default only where there is not. [uint8, uint32 = 10] therefore defaults to [0, 10] rather than being rejected because the second position was already spoken for.

    The literal element store remains the open case 1.5.3 records: the design specifies array[0] = 300 on a [].<uint8> as wrapping to 44 on the grounds of matching Uint8Array, while the literal rule would reject it, and the compatibility argument does not reach new syntax. This clause resolves it against the design's example: a literal element store follows the literal rule and is rejected, because the compatibility argument does not reach new syntax, [].<uint8> exists nowhere today and Uint8Array is untouched, and a literal that does not fit its destination is the mistake every other literal rule here treats it as. A typed value stores by Table 12 as before, so the runtime wrap survives where it belongs.

    1.14 Extensions

    The design is organized as a core and a set of extensions, each of which adds types, operations, or syntax to the core rather than revising it. This clause states what an extension may do and records the hooks the preceding clauses have already named.

    1.14.1 The Extension Mechanism

    An extension may add types to 1.4, provided each is described by a Type Record of one of the kinds of Table 1. It may define the operations of 1.4.16 for a type it adds, and may declare operators for a type that does not define them (1.8.7). It may add syntax, subject to the constraint the whole proposal is under: the syntax must be an error in the current language. It may add library functions, overloaded by 1.9.3.

    An extension may not revise the type-checking framework of 1.2. In particular it may not add a kind to Table 1, may not change SameType or IsSubtype for a kind it did not introduce, and may not introduce an implicit conversion beyond those of Table 6.

    Note

    The last of these is the constraint worth stating explicitly, because it is the one an extension is most likely to want to break. A units extension wants Meter to become float32, a decimal extension wants a Number to become a decimal128, and a SIMD extension wants a scalar to broadcast into a vector. Each of these is available without an implicit conversion: the first is a metadata parameterization, which is a subtype of its base and so is subsumed rather than converted; the second is written; and the third is an operator the extension declares. The framework holds because every extension so far has been expressible within it, which is evidence that the four implicit cases are the right four rather than merely the ones that were listed first.

    1.14.2 The Hooks

    Table 23 lists the extensions the preceding clauses name and what each is relied on for. It is a map of the obligations this specification has incurred outward, not a specification of the extensions themselves.

    Note

    The extensions not listed, including serialization, structure of arrays, the regular expression types, Temporal, and the standard library, are specified in terms of the core and are relied on by nothing here. That is a claim about direction and not about importance: serialization is where a typed program meets a network, and it is built from the layout, the metadata hooks, and the where clauses this specification already gives, adding no rule any of them must accommodate. The one place the direction reverses is threading, whose heap sharing decides what a Type Object's identity means (19), which is why it alone among the outer extensions has a clause here.

    The dependency now runs in one direction without exception. The metadata parameterization that 1.2.3.1 once forward-referenced is core (1.4.13), as the refinement relation it shares with an enum's underlying type and a class's base always suggested it should be. What remains with the primitive metadata extension is the protocol's hook surface: the judgments beyond the branding default, and the conversion machinery.

    1.14.3 Coverage of the Design Documents

    Every document of the design has a disposition here: it is specified by clauses of this document, it is deferred to its own future clauses with a hook already named, or it is deferred outright, in which case whatever it adds must fit the mechanism above. The examples directory is a corpus this specification is checked against rather than a source of requirements.

    2 References and Borrowing

    A reference is a borrow: a handle to a storage location, a variable, an object property, or an array element, that reads and writes through to the original rather than to a copy. It is what lets a function mutate its caller's value and a loop write into an array in place. This clause specifies the borrowing forms of the design's references extension (Table 23): the ref argument and ref return, the ref parameter, the ref lexical binding and its rebinding, and the reference for-of loop. The ref type is already in the grammar; this clause adds the runtime that the type describes.

    2.1 Reference Values and the Absence of Identity

    A Reference Value is a value produced by a ref expression that holds a storage location: a Reference Record (in the sense of the base specification) whose base is an Environment Record for a variable or an Object for a property or an array element. A Reference Value is a primitive value in the sense that it is not an Object; nothing is allocated when one is created.

    A Reference Value has no observable identity. Every operation that reads a binding holding one dereferences to the value at the location, and a Reference Value that reaches any position consuming a value decays to the value at its location first. In consequence typeof, Reflect.typeOf, ===, and instanceof see the referenced value and never the reference, and a Reference Value cannot be stored in a binding that outlives its location, a property, an array, or a collection: the only bindings that hold one are the ref parameter and ref lexical binding below, which alias the location rather than store the reference. There is therefore no way to distinguish a reference from the value it refers to, which is what keeps it a location and an index rather than an object.

    Note

    Decay is where the absence of identity is realized. A reference reaches a value position at an ordinary call boundary, an operand of an operator, an argument to a non-ref parameter, or a conversion, and at each the value at the location is taken. The reference is observable only through the aliasing behaviour of the binding that holds it, not as a value in its own right, so no reflected type and no equality can name it.

    2.2 Reference Parameters and Arguments

    A ref argument f(ref e) evaluates e to a storage location and passes a Reference Value holding it. It is a TypeError exception if e does not denote a location: a value, a private member, a super property, and a property of a primitive have no location a borrow could alias. A ref parameter binds its name to the location the argument carries, so a read in the callee reads through to the referent and a write in the callee writes through to it. It is a TypeError exception if the argument to a ref parameter is not a Reference Value, since a plain value has no location to borrow, and a Reference Value passed to a parameter that is not ref decays to its referent.

    A type annotation on a ref parameter is checked against the referent and never converts it. The referent is of the annotated type or a TypeError exception is thrown; the value at the caller's location is not rewritten, because a borrow that converted its referent would silently change storage it does not own. A ref parameter's binding is not re-enforced by the parameter type pass of 1.6 for the same reason.

    A function may return a reference with return ref e, evaluated as a ref argument is. At an ordinary call the returned reference decays to its referent, so a caller that consumes the call as a value observes the referent. Consuming a returned reference as a location, so that first(a)++ writes through to the element, requires the assignment-target rules the design defers, and is not specified here (Table 23).

    2.3 Reference Bindings and Rebinding

    A ref lexical binding aliases a storage location for its scope. let ref b = e and const ref b = e evaluate e to a location, by the same rule as a ref argument, and bind b to it; it is a TypeError exception if e does not denote a location, and it is a Syntax Error for a ref binding to omit its initializer. A read of b reads through to the location. A let ref binding may be written through, so b = v writes v to the location, and may be rebound; a const ref binding may not be written through, so b = v is the ordinary assignment-to-constant TypeError exception, while a write to a member of the referent is permitted as for any const. An annotation on a ref binding is checked against the referent without conversion, as for a ref parameter.

    Reassigning a let ref binding with ref b = e rebinds it: b is redirected to the location e denotes, and the location it previously aliased is not written. It is a TypeError exception to apply the rebinding form to a name that is not a rebindable ref binding.

    2.4 Reference Iteration

    A for ( LetOrConst ref p of e ) loop binds p to a reference to each element of the array e in turn, so the body writes into the array in place. The iteration is index-based: it reads the length of e once, evaluates the body once for each index below that length with p bound to a reference to the element at that index, and does not go through %Symbol.iterator%, so patching the iterator protocol does not affect it and no result object is allocated. A let ref element binding may be written through and rebound and a const ref element binding may not, as for a ref lexical binding. It is a TypeError exception if e is not an array.

    The length read at the start of the loop is pinned for its duration. An operation that changes the length of the array while a reference into it is live, push, pop, shift, unshift, splice, or a store to length, is a TypeError exception, which a ref loop makes easy to reach since it holds a reference across the whole iteration. A user-defined iterator that yields references is not supported, and neither is a callback parameter declared ref; both compose from the forms above and belong to the design's extension (Table 23), as does the meaning of a reference into an SoA column set (9).

    Note

    The two liveness rules are the whole of what the borrow needs, in place of a borrow checker with lifetimes. A reference cannot dangle in the Rust sense because the array it points into is a garbage-collected object that stays alive as long as any reference to it exists; what remains to prevent is a reference into storage that moves, which is a length change while a reference is live, and a reference escaping its location, which the absence of identity (2.1) already forecloses. Both are local checks and neither needs a lifetime system. This specification checks the length rule at run time; the design also permits a static rejection wherever the array's type makes the offending operation visible, which an implementation may add as a diagnostic without changing the meaning of any program this clause accepts.

    3 Typed Regular Expressions

    A regular expression literal is a compile-time constant whose pattern an engine already parses before the program runs. This clause puts that parse into the type system: the Static Type (1.2.4) of a regular expression literal records the number and kind of its capture groups and the names of its named groups. This is the core of the design's regular expression types (Table 23); the fuller surface it enables, the typed match result and the group-name checking on it, the typed String methods, the flag-dependent result shapes, pattern composition, and string narrowing by pattern, is that extension's to complete and is specified in terms of this core.

    The Static Type of a regular expression literal is a nominal Type Record whose [[LibraryName]] is "RegExp" and whose [[Arguments]] are two Type Records, the Captures and the Groups, derived from the pattern:

    • Captures is a tuple Type Record with one element per capturing group, in the order the groups' opening parentheses appear, each element the type of that capture. Where the pattern has no capturing group, Captures is the empty array type rather than an empty tuple, so that it is the type a bare [] denotes (1.13).
    • Groups is an object Type Record with one property per named group, its key the group's name and its type the type of that capture. A name that occurs on more than one capturing group, which the pattern grammar admits only across alternatives, contributes one property.
    • The type of a capture is the "string" primitive where every path through the pattern that produces a match enters the group exactly once, and the union of "string" with the void type otherwise. A group is in the second case when it is quantified with a minimum of zero, when it lies within a disjunction of more than one alternative, or when it lies within a lookahead or lookbehind, and when any group that encloses it is.

    A non-capturing group, a lookaround, and a backreference add no capture and contribute nothing to Captures or Groups. Because the type is a parameterization of the "RegExp" library type, it is invariant in Captures and Groups (1.2.3.1): a RegExp.<Captures, Groups> annotation whose arguments differ from a literal's inferred shape is a type error. A bare RegExp, the raw library type, is the supertype of every such parameterization, so it holds a literal of any shape while a written parameterization does not hold a value of another.

    Note

    The analysis is syntactic and reads only the pattern, which is why it costs nothing an engine does not already spend: the same parse that reports an invalid pattern yields the capture count, the group names, and the nesting that decides optionality. The rule for optionality is the design's, that a group is required exactly when every matching path enters it once; a group behind ?, *, or {0,n}, a group in one arm of an alternation whose other arm a match may take instead, and a group inside a lookaround are the cases a match may complete without entering, and each widens the capture with the void type.

    The empty-capture case is written as the array type rather than an empty tuple only to agree with the type a bare [] denotes in this grammar, so that the common RegExp.<[], {}> annotation holds a no-capture literal. A pattern with captures is a tuple, so its arity is exact and a capture-count mismatch is caught. The flag-dependent result shapes, in particular the indices a d flag adds and the match overload a g flag selects, are part of the deferred match-result surface, so the inferred type carries the two arguments the annotation forms in common use write and not a third for the flags.

    4 Generics

    A generic declaration introduces parameters that a later application binds to types or values, and each distinct application is a distinct type with its own Type Object and its own specialized body. The core relies on this clause in three places already stated: the deferred applications of 1.2.5.2 carry the parameter Type Records this clause substitutes, the computed constraints of 1.3.3.1 bind parameters through the ordering this clause defines, and the invariance step of 1.2.3.1 is relaxed by the variance this clause declares. The full surface of the design's generics extension, specialized overloads, constraint families, variadic packs, and their inference, builds on the model here and is that extension's to complete (Table 23).

    4.1 Parameters as Types and as Values

    A generic parameter is a parameter Type Record whose [[Declaration]] is its TypeParameter. A type parameter, declared with extends or unbounded, denotes that Record in a type position and, in an expression position, the Type Object bound to it, exactly as a type name does. A value parameter, declared with : and a value type, is the value generic parameter of the design: its argument is a value of the named type rather than a type, and this specification binds it to the literal type of that value (1.4.12) with the value recoverable as that type's one value. The literal-type binding is primitive and the value is its view, the decision 1.3.3.1 records: a body that reads V where a value is required, as 2 ** V, reads the [[Value]] of the literal type, and a body that reads V where a type is required, as Reflect.typeOf(V), reads the type itself.

    Note

    Two applications name one specialization when their arguments are pairwise the same: a type argument under SameType, and a value argument under SameValue, which is the identity the literal type already has, so A.<0> and A.<-0> are distinct and A.<NaN> is usable, following from identity rather than as special cases. A reference value cannot be an argument, since specialization identity would then turn on object identity; the permitted value types are the primitive value types the design lists.

    4.2 SubstituteType ( t, bindings )

    The abstract operation SubstituteType takes arguments t (a Type Record) and bindings (a List of Records each with a [[Declaration]] and a [[Value]]) and returns a Type Record. It replaces the generic parameters bound by bindings throughout t and evaluates any deferred application thereby closed, the operation full specialization performs at each distinct application. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If t.[[Kind]] is parameter, then
      1. For each Record b of bindings, do
        1. If b.[[Declaration]] is t.[[Declaration]], return b.[[Value]].
      2. Return t.
    2. If t.[[Kind]] is application, then
      1. Let args be a new empty List.
      2. For each element a of t.[[Arguments]], do
        1. If a is a Type Record, append SubstituteType(a, bindings) to args.
        2. Else, append a to args.
      3. If some element of args is a Type Record with a parameter within it, return the Type Record { [[Kind]]: application, [[Builder]]: t.[[Builder]], [[Arguments]]: args }.
      4. Return the [[TypeRecord]] of the Type Object that results from evaluating the deferred application of t.[[Builder]] to args by EvaluateBuilderCall, which is a normal completion because args is now closed.
    3. Let u be a Type Record with the same fields as t, except that every Type Record reachable from t through its fields and the elements of any List or structured Record those fields hold, without descending into a nested application or parameter, is replaced by the Type Record that substituting bindings into it yields.
    4. Return CanonicalizeType(u).
     Note

    Substitution and evaluation are one pass: a deferred application becomes evaluable exactly when substitution has closed every parameter it read, at which point 1.2.5.3 runs under the budget and memo already specified, so a builder used at two instantiations evaluates twice under two distinct memo keys and interns each result. A value generic contributes its literal type where a type is substituted and that type's value where the body computes, which is the one mechanism seen from two sides. Where substitution leaves a parameter in place, in the body of a still-generic outer declaration, the result is itself generic and specializes when the outer one does.

    4.3 The where Clause

    A generic declaration may carry a where clause, written with the WhereClauses of 5.1 where 10 admits them: a compile-time-evaluable Boolean expression over its parameters, checked at each specialization once its parameters are bound. Where the expression is false for an application's bindings, that application is a type error, reported against the clause's source. A where over value generics, as where I.every(i => i < N) on a variadic pack, is the design's compile-time bound on the values a parameter admits; a where that inspects a runtime value rather than a generic parameter is the dependent record type extension's, checked at the boundaries Table 13 enumerates rather than at specialization; and a where naming return on a compile-time-evaluable function is a contract (10). One syntax carries all three because one rule does: a predicate is evaluated where its subject exists, which is at specialization for a parameter, at a boundary for a value, and at evaluation for a result.

    Note

    The clause is evaluated by 1.2.5 like any other compile-time expression, so it may call builders and read a value generic's value, and its determinism and budget are those already fixed. A where is a check, not an inference: it narrows which applications are legal, and never changes what a parameter is bound to.

    4.4 Declaration-Site Variance

    A type parameter may be declared covariant or contravariant. Where a parameter is covariant, one instantiation is a subtype of another along that position when the argument in the first is a subtype of the argument in the second; where contravariant, when the reverse holds; where neither is declared, the position is invariant, the default of 1.2.3.1. A covariant parameter is well-formed only where it appears in output positions of the declaration, a method return or a readonly field, and a contravariant parameter only in input positions, a method or constructor parameter; a parameter appearing in a position of the wrong polarity is an early error, since the variance would be unsound there for the reason the invariance note gives of a mutable container.

    Note

    This is the variance of C# and Kotlin, sound because the well-formedness rule confines a covariant parameter to positions a value is read from and a contravariant one to positions a value is written to. A Producer.<Dog> is usable as a Producer.<Animal> where Producer is declared covariant and only produces, which the container invariance of 1.2.3.1 forbids for a Map precisely because a Map also consumes. The relation composes with the others: an instantiation is a subtype along a covariant position by the ordinary subtyping of the arguments, so a covariant position holding another generic type carries the polarity inward, which the last two table rows state.

    5 Dependent Record Types

    A type alias declaration may carry where clauses, each a predicate over the value the type describes. What they state is what a property's own type cannot: that a credit card and a billing address are present together, or that a postal code's pattern follows from a country. The narrowing form v is T (Table 14) and the boundary checks of Table 13 are what this clause makes real.

    5.1 The where Clause

    The WhereClauses that 1.7.1 admits after a type alias's Type are given by:

    WhereClauses[Yield, Await] : WhereClause[?Yield, ?Await] WhereClauses[?Yield, ?Await] WhereClause[?Yield, ?Await] WhereClause[Yield, Await] : where RefinementPredicate[?Yield, ?Await] RefinementPredicate[Yield, Await] : AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] if ( AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ) { RefinementPredicate[?Yield, ?Await] } if ( AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ) { RefinementPredicate[?Yield, ?Await] } else { RefinementPredicate[?Yield, ?Await] }

    A RefinementPredicate is evaluated with this bound to the value under test, and it holds when ToBoolean of its result is true. The conditional form evaluates its AssignmentExpression and then the predicate of the branch taken, and holds vacuously where that expression is falsy and no else is present. It is an early error for a RefinementPredicate not to be compile-time evaluable (1.2.5.1) as a function of this, which is what admits the same predicate at compile time, where it decides a constant, and at run time, where it checks a value.

    type N = T where P ; binds N to the Type Record { [[Kind]]: parameterized, [[Base]]: the Type Record T denotes, [[Metadata]]: OrdinaryObjectCreate with a where property whose value is P as a function of this }. Each further clause refines the type the clauses before it produced, so _T_ where _A_ where _B_ is the refinement by B of the refinement by A of T, and conjunction, subtyping, and membership follow from that nesting rather than from a rule of their own.

    The where meta type (6) claims the where key. Its validate of a value against a constraint is the evaluation of the constraint's where with this bound to that value; its subtype holds where the required constraint is unconstrained, so a predicate is shed, or where both carry the same predicate; its default is the unconstrained constraint, and it defines no other hook. A dependent record type therefore admits a value of its base only where every predicate holds of it, is a subtype of its base and sheds its predicates upward freely, and is reached from its base only by a crossing that runs them.

    Note

    The refinement is 1.4.13's, not a kind of its own, which is why nothing here restates layout, defaults, or the relations: a predicate changes no representation, and the branding rule of that clause is already the rule this one wants, that a Payment is usable wherever its bare object type is and that going the other way is a check. The design writes a postal code's pattern as string.<{ pattern: … }> in the same document that writes a payment's cross-field rule as where, and the two are one mechanism over two bases.

    Because a predicate is a function, the structural equivalence of 1.2.5.2 compares it by SameValue, so a dependent record type's identity is its declaration's identity: two modules that write the same where text declare two types, where two that write stringPattern(/^[0-9]{5}$/) declare one. That asymmetry is deliberate and is not an accident of the encoding. A predicate is code and may close over its scope, so where this.total < LIMIT in two modules means two things whenever their LIMIT differs; comparing predicate source would make those one type and would be unsound. A regular expression is data and carries no scope, so comparing it structurally is safe. A program that wants one dependent record type across modules declares it once and imports it, as it would a class.

    Conjunction by nesting also gives the diagnostics: a value failing _T_ where _A_ where _B_ fails at the innermost refinement whose predicate is false, so the clause that rejected it is the clause named.

    5.2 The is Operator

    v is T is true when the value of v is of the type T denotes and false otherwise. It performs IsOfType (1.6.2.1) and never throws for a value the type rejects, which is the difference between it and the check a boundary performs: a boundary requires, and is asks. Its grammar is given with the conversion operator (1.3.5), whose precedence it shares.

    Note

    is is the branch a program takes instead of the throw: if (payment is Payment) tests what example(payment) would have required. Against a dependent record type it runs the predicates, so it is also the only way to ask whether a value the program has been mutating is valid again. Against any other type it is the same structural test every boundary makes, so v is uint8 and v is { x: float32 } are ordinary questions with ordinary answers, and the accessor and higher-order reading of 1.6.2.1 is the reading here too.

    5.3 Narrowing Through a Refinement

    Where the Static Type of v is a dependent record type whose predicate is the conditional form, and the narrowing facts in force at a position decide that predicate's AssignmentExpression, the assertions of the branch so taken contribute their narrowing facts at that position: an assertion this is T contributes what v is T contributes, and an assertion this.p is T contributes what v.p is T contributes (Table 14).

    An expression is decided by the narrowing facts when evaluating it is compile-time evaluable given them, reading this as v and reading a path whose narrowed type is a literal type as that type's [[Value]]. No other means of deciding it is specified: a predicate the facts leave open contributes nothing, and the boundary check stands.

    Note

    This is what lets if (addr.country == 'US') narrow addr.postalCode to USPostalCode without the program writing a discriminated union, which is the case the design contrasts its where against. The chain is short and each link is already specified: the literal-equality row of Table 14 gives addr.country the Static Type 'US', that fact makes this.country == 'US' a compile-time-evaluable expression over a known value, deciding it selects the then branch, and that branch's this is { postalCode: USPostalCode } narrows exactly as the is would if the program had written it.

    The rule is deliberately not a prover. Where a predicate is not decided, nothing is narrowed and the boundary check runs, which is the design's own account of the case where a 'strict' name is assigned a 'loose' mode: the checker cannot know that one pattern's language contains the other's, so the check at the next boundary is what catches it. Sharpening that particular case is the pattern subtype judgment's business (1.4.13), not narrowing's.

    6 The Metadata Protocol

    A metadata parameterization carries a value that 1.4.13 gives one default meaning: a brand, shed upward freely and crossed downward only at a construction boundary. This clause is what lets that value mean more. A meta type teaches the checker the semantics of the fields it claims, so that float32.<{ m: 1, s: -2 }> is an acceleration whose dimensions combine under multiplication, float32.<{ minimum: 0 }> is a bound that a comparison can narrow, and decimal128.<{ scale: 2 }> is a price that rounds where it is stored and not before.

    6.1 Meta Declarations

    The production below restates the Declaration of 1.7.1 and carries the declaration forms the extensions add (1.1).

    Declaration[Yield, Await] : HoistableDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await, ~Default] ClassDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await, ~Default] LexicalDeclaration[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] TypeAliasDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await] MetaDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await] PrimitiveOperatorDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await] InterfaceDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await] EnumDeclaration[?Yield, ?Await] MetaDeclaration[Yield, Await] : meta [no LineTerminator here] TypeName[?Yield, ?Await] { MetaHookList[?Yield, ?Await]opt } MetaHookList[Yield, Await] : MetaHook[?Yield, ?Await] MetaHookList[?Yield, ?Await] MetaHook[?Yield, ?Await] MetaHook[Yield, Await] : default = AssignmentExpression[+In, ?Yield, ?Await] ; MethodDefinition[?Yield, ?Await]

    A MetaDeclaration names an object type, the meta type's constraint shape, and supplies hooks for it. The MethodDefinitions it may contain are exactly those named in Table 26; a method of any other name is an early error, as is a second declaration for one type, a missing default or subtype, or a hook whose signature does not match the table.

    Every hook is required to be a pure function of its arguments, compile-time evaluable (1.2.5.1), and deterministic, the obligation 1.4.13 states of the judgments they compose. It is an early error for a hook not to be compile-time evaluable.

    Note

    The obligation is what makes one hook serve two phases. validate decides a literal at compile time and a value at run time; quantize folds a constant and rounds a computed result; subtype contributes only to the metadata subtype judgment: the checking pass (1.2.6) invokes it to decide a pair of portions, the decision is a pure function of its interned operands and is remembered, and the run-time operations that reach the judgment — a conversion between two parameterizations, an any-typed value arriving at a parameterized position — consult the remembered relation, invoking the hook only for a pair no check has yet decided. A hook that read the clock or a global would make a program's types depend on when it ran, which 1.2.5.4 already forbids of everything else in type position.

    meta is a contextual keyword under the restriction is takes (5.2) and for the same reason: meta T on one line is a Syntax Error today, and meta alone on a line is an expression statement that must stay one.

    6.2 Decomposition and the Judgments

    A metadata value is drawn from a closed language, given by Table 27. The top level is a flat record whose keys are claimed keys; the values under them may nest freely. Two metadata values are equivalent when they are equivalent by the comparison that table gives, and it is that comparison, and not object identity, that interning uses.

    Nothing else is a metadata value. A function, an object other than the forms above, and undefined are not: absence is the absence of a key rather than a key holding nothing.

    Note 1

    The language is closed because three things depend on it and each would break for a different reason. Interning compares metadata rather than identity, which is what makes brand(uint32, 'UserId') written in two modules one type with no registry, and a form with no structural comparison would have no answer there. An expansion artifact (18) is a serialization of the interning table, so a form that cannot be written down and read back could not appear in one. And a hook receives the metadata, so every form must be presentable to a program.

    The representation is this specification's business and the cost is an implementation's. Metadata is settled where a type is written, so an implementation is expected to compare these forms directly and to materialize an object only where a hook is actually called. Nothing here is intended to cost a running program anything.

    An unregistered Symbol is admitted deliberately, and it is the one form whose equivalence is not structural. That is what it is for: a module that mints a Symbol it neither exports nor registers has a metadata key no other module can write, so its parameterizations are its own, and two of them are one type exactly when they carry that Symbol. A registered Symbol behaves the other way, being the same Symbol in every agent that asks for the key, and is the form to reach for where two libraries must agree. A type whose metadata carries an unregistered Symbol cannot appear in an expansion artifact and loses nothing by it: an artifact carries the concrete types a module graph's public surface produces, and a Symbol deliberately not exported is not part of one. An artifact is an optimization and never a semantics, so a consumer evaluates such a type as it evaluates any other.

    A meta type claims the property keys of its constraint shape. Claiming is global and flat: it is an early error, reported at the second MetaDeclaration rather than at any use, for two meta types to claim one key. A metadata object whose own key no meta type claims is a type error at the parameterization that writes it.

    6.2.1 MetadataPortion ( m, M )

    The abstract operation MetadataPortion takes arguments m (an ECMAScript language value) and M (a meta type) and returns an Object. It returns the part of the metadata m that M governs. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. Let p be a copy of M's default.
    2. If m is an Object, then
      1. For each own property key k of m that M claims, set p's k to m's k.
    3. Return p.

    The metadata subtype judgment holds of two metadata values s and t when, for every meta type M declared in the surrounding realm, subtype of M holds of MetadataPortion(s, M) and MetadataPortion(t, M). The meta types declared in the surrounding realm are those declared as of the judgment's application: when the checking pass applies it, that is after the pass has processed the source text's own meta declarations (1.2.6) and after the evaluation of every source text evaluated before it.

    The validation judgment holds of a value v and a metadata value m when, for every meta type M whose MetadataPortion(m, M) is not M's default, M defines validate and it holds of v and that portion. A meta type that constrains and defines no validate therefore admits no bare value of the base at all, which is what makes a brand a brand and what makes const v: Velocity = 10; a type error until 6.5 gives number a way in.

    Note 2

    Decomposition is why one flat object serves every meta type at once: float32.<{ m: 1, s: -2, minimum: 0 }> is read by the dimensions meta type as an acceleration and by the bounds meta type as a non-negative number, each seeing its own keys and the defaults of the rest, and neither seeing the other's. It also fixes what an omitted key means, since a portion is completed from default before any hook sees it: float32.<{ m: 1 }> is a metre because the remaining exponents default to zero, and a plain float32 is every meta type's default at once, which is what makes it dimensionless and unbounded rather than untyped.

    Claiming is global so that a key means one thing wherever it is written, and the diagnostic is at the declaration because that is where the collision is, not at the unlucky program that parameterized on m last. The cost is that a meta type cannot be extended by a third party: an extension ships as its own meta type with its own keys, and the Symbol keys of 1.4.12 are what keep two libraries from colliding at all.

    The where of a dependent record type (5), the brand, and the string pattern are meta types of this protocol declared by this specification rather than by a program, which is why nothing about them is special-cased above: a brand's subtype is the identity of its tag and it defines no validate, a pattern's validate is a full match and its subtype is the judgment 1.4.13 sanctions, and a where's validate runs its predicate while its subtype holds only where the required side is unconstrained or carries the same predicate.

    6.3 Conversion at a Boundary

    Two parameterizations of one base that differ in their metadata convert by this clause, which is the fourth case of Table 6 and applies at every boundary of Table 13, an operator's argument among them.

    6.3.1 ConvertParameterization ( value, from, to )

    The abstract operation ConvertParameterization takes arguments value (an ECMAScript language value), from (an ECMAScript language value), and to (an ECMAScript language value) and returns either a normal completion containing an ECMAScript language value or a throw completion. It converts value, whose metadata is from, to the metadata to. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. Let v be value.
    2. For each meta type M declared in the surrounding realm, do
      1. Let fp be MetadataPortion(from, M).
      2. Let tp be MetadataPortion(to, M).
      3. If subtype of M holds of fp and tp, then
        1. NOTE: M admits the crossing, and requires nothing of the value.
      4. Else if fp is M's default and an implicit cast operator into M is declared on the base (6.5), then
        1. Set v to the result of applying that operator to v.
        2. If M defines validate and it does not hold of v and tp, throw a TypeError exception whose message names M and, where M defines describe, its description of tp.
      5. Else,
        1. Throw a TypeError exception whose message names M and, where M defines describe, its descriptions of fp and tp.
    3. Let f be 1.
    4. For each meta type M declared in the surrounding realm that defines conversionFactor, set f to f × the result of conversionFactor of M applied to MetadataPortion(from, M) and MetadataPortion(to, M).
    5. If f is not 1, set v to v × f.
    6. For each meta type M declared in the surrounding realm that defines quantize, set v to the result of quantize of M applied to v and MetadataPortion(to, M).
    7. Return v.

    Where the required metadata leaves a meta type unpinned, as an operator parameter typed float32.<D> leaves every meta type but the dimensions of its receiver, that meta type's portion of from flows to the position rather than the target's: scaled, it is the result of rescale of M applied to MetadataPortion(from, M) and f where M defines rescale, and M's default where it does not.

    Each meta type gates the crossing independently, and there are exactly two ways through: subtype admits it, or the value carries nothing of that meta type and a cast supplies what it lacks. Meter reaches Kilometer by the first, since the exponents agree and only the ratio differs; a bare number reaches Velocity only by the second, which is why the design says that without a cast on number the assignment is a type error, and why a brand, for which no cast is declared, is reachable only by construction. validate is what the second way costs, and it is where a bound is enforced: subtype cannot prove an unconstrained value non-negative, so the cast into the bounds meta type checks it.

    The rest runs in the order above: the factor once, as the product over every meta type that contributes one; rescale on the portions that flow, so a bound stated in metres arrives stated in kilometres; and quantize after the scaling, so a value rounds onto the representation it will be stored in rather than one it passes through. Where every operand is a compile-time constant the whole of it folds and no check survives to run time (1.6.5).

    Note

    Kilometer(5) + Meter(300) is the worked case. The parameter is the receiver's dimensions, so the argument's metre constraint must pass subtype, which it does since the exponents agree; conversionFactor yields 1/1000, so the value becomes 0.3; the argument's bounds are not pinned by the parameter and so flow through rescale with that factor, arriving in kilometre space; and the operator body adds 5 and 0.3 with both operands in one unit system, which is the point of doing all of this before the body runs. Had the bound not been rescaled, a sum of bounds would have added a metre-space number to a kilometre-space one and produced a bound that means nothing.

    Taking the factor as a product over the meta types that contribute one, rather than applying each in turn, is a decision this specification makes where the design leaves an order: sequential application would make the result depend on the order meta types were declared in whenever a rescale is not linear, and an order-dependent type is the one thing the determinism invariant cannot have. Only the dimensions meta type contributes a factor in the design's own examples, so the two readings agree there and differ only where a second one appears.

    6.4 Narrowing a Constraint

    A comparison against a compile-time constant narrows the metadata of a parameterized value, as Table 14 records. The metadata it narrows to is NarrowMetadata(m, op, c), where m is the value's metadata, op the comparison, and c the constant: the metadata whose portion for each meta type M defining narrow is the result of narrow of M applied to MetadataPortion(m, M), the String naming op, and c, and whose portion for each other meta type is unchanged. The false branch narrows by the negation of op, pairing >= with <, > with <=, and == with !=, in both directions.

    Note

    This is what lets if (v >= 0) give v the type float32.<{ …, minimum: 0 }> inside the branch, and a further if (v <= 343) give it a maximum too, so that a return v into a type requiring both bounds passes subtype and needs no check at all. It is a narrowing and not a proof: a meta type that defines no narrow learns nothing from a comparison and keeps the constraint it had, which costs a check at the next boundary and nothing else.

    6.5 Operators on a Parameterized Primitive

    A declaration of the form primitive T P { … }, where T names a primitive type and P is an optional TypeParameters constrained by a meta type, declares operators on T for each parameterization its parameters admit, and is the form that 1.8.7 allows an extension to supply. Its grammar, and that of the operator definitions within it, is 12's, which declares the same definitions on a class. What this clause fixes is what those definitions mean for metadata, which is this specification's to say because the metadata is.

    An operator body evaluates on raw values: no operator declared by any block is re-entered within one, so return this + rhs; inside operator+ is the primitive addition rather than itself. The metadata of a result comes from the return type annotations alone. For one operator invocation: at most one definition with a body may match, and two that do is an early error; any number of definitions without a body may match, each contributing its own meta type's portion of the result through its return type; where no definition with a body matches, the primitive operation runs; and the portions the matching return types evaluate to are merged into one flat metadata object, each meta type contributing its default where no matching definition mentions it.

    Such a declaration may also define an implicit cast operator, written as an operator whose name is a parameterization of the primitive, which supplies the conversion of Table 6 from the bare primitive into a parameterization. At a boundary, one is invoked for each meta type the required metadata constrains and the supplied value's does not, and its absence is why such a boundary is otherwise a type error: const v: Velocity = 10; compiles exactly where number declares a cast into the dimensions meta type, and a bare number reaches a bounded type only through a cast whose boundary runs validate.

    Note

    The raw-body rule is what makes the whole protocol terminate: an operator body is the definition of the operation, so an expression inside it cannot mean the thing being defined. It is also why the boundary conversion of 6.3 happens before the body and not within it: a body sees operands already converted, quantized, and in one unit system, and needs to know nothing about metadata to be correct.

    Splitting a value definition from metadata-only definitions is what lets two meta types share an operator without either knowing of the other. The dimensions block computes the sum and says the exponents are unchanged; the bounds block says the sum's bounds are the sum of the bounds and computes nothing; the result carries both. A meta type that has nothing to say about an operator says nothing, and its portion of that result is its default, which is the correct answer for a constraint that the operation does not preserve.

    7 Declaration Reflection and Decorators

    The reflection of 1.11.3 reads a type. This clause reads a declaration: the fields a class declares, the parameters a function takes, the enumerators an enum lists, and what a decorator has recorded about any of them. The two are one facility under one name, selected by a first type argument, and they answer different questions: a type is shared by everything of that shape, and a declaration is the one place a program wrote it down.

    7.1 Reflection Contexts

    A reflection context is a type in the Reflect namespace naming a kind of declaration or a part of one. Reflect.getReflection.<C, T>(…) reflects the part of T that the context C names, and is resolved by the specialized overloads of 4: the first argument selects among the signatures and the second is the declaration reflected, which is why one name serves every context with no any in it.

    A context that names a set of members has two signatures: one taking no name, returning an object keyed by member name, and one taking a name, returning that member alone. Where a position rather than a name is wanted, as for a parameter list, Reflect.getReflectionByIndex returns the same reflections as a tuple in declaration order. Reflection of a class includes inherited members; passing { own: *true* } restricts it to the members the class declares itself.

    Note

    The contexts are the reason the facility needs no separate function per kind of declaration: getReflection.<Reflect.ClassField, Point>('x') and getReflection.<Reflect.Type>(Point) are the same call resolved by its selector, and the selector is an ordinary type. It is also the design's own demonstration of specialized overloads, one signature per context, which is why 4 records that form as the thing this extension needs from it.

    7.2 The Metadata Channel

    Every reflection context has a metadata property holding the metadata object of what it names. A metadata object is an instance of the intrinsic class corresponding to its context, %ClassMetadata% for Reflect.Class, %ClassFieldMetadata% for Reflect.ClassField, and so on for each row above. A program adds to one by declaring a partial class over it whose fields are typed and Symbol-keyed, and the fields it adds are the only ones there are: the intrinsic classes declare none.

    Reflect.getMetadata.<C, T>(…) returns the metadata object that Reflect.getReflection of the same context and arguments would report, and is the reading half of what a decorator writes.

    A metadata object is inherited through the prototype chain. Where a derived class does not redeclare an inherited member, the member's metadata object is the base's, so a Symbol lookup finds what the base's decorators wrote. Where it redeclares the member and decorates it, the member's metadata object is a new one whose prototype is the base's, so what the derived class's decorators write shadows the base's without mutating it.

    Note

    Typed Symbol-keyed fields on a partial class are what make this a channel rather than a property bag: a key cannot collide, because a Symbol cannot; a value cannot be of the wrong type, because the field is declared; and nothing is reachable that was not declared, so a reader of %ClassFieldMetadata% sees exactly the vocabulary the program's decorators established. This is the same discipline the metadata protocol takes for a meta type's keys (6.2), reached from the other direction.

    Prototypal inheritance of the metadata object is what makes @tag('base') on a field of A visible on B's inherited copy and invisible once B redeclares and retags it, without either class knowing of the other. It also means metadata is read the way a property is read, so a key absent from a derived member's own object is not absent but inherited, which is usually what a decorator's reader wants and is worth knowing when it is not.

    7.3 Applying a Decorator

    A decorator is an ordinary function whose last parameter is annotated with a reflection context. Which function a decoration applies is decided by the overload resolution of 1.9.3.1 over the arguments written and the context of the position decorated, so @f, @f(0), and @f('a') may name three declarations of f and select among them the way any call does. @f and @f() are one form: both resolve with no explicit argument, and a signature taking the context alone is preferred over one whose remaining parameters are satisfied by defaults. Where no such signature exists and more than one is satisfied by defaults alone, the decoration is ambiguous and a TypeError is thrown where the decorated declaration is evaluated.

    A decorator runs when the declaration it decorates is evaluated, and what it may do there is write to the metadata of its context, register an initializer, and return a replacement for what it decorated. The replacement a context admits, the initializer registration, and the order in which decorators of one declaration run are the decorators extension's (Table 23).

    Note

    Types are what remove the (value, context) return from a decorator that takes arguments: an erased system cannot tell @f from @f(0) without one, since it cannot see which f is meant, and this proposal resolves it by the annotation on the last parameter. So a decorator looks the same whether or not it takes arguments, and giving one an argument is editing its parameter list rather than rewriting it into a factory.

    8 Declarative Checker Facts

    A signature carries two facts that no rule of the language computes and that a program states instead: the this a function expects, and what a call of it establishes about its arguments. They are data the checker reads, never code it runs, which is what distinguishes them from the meta protocol's hooks (6) and keeps them outside the boundary 1.2.5 draws: nothing evaluates, and each is consumed by machinery that already exists.

    8.1 The Expected this Type

    A signature's [[ThisType]] is the type its this is required to have. It is contravariant, as a parameter is, so a signature with a [[ThisType]] is usable where one requiring a wider this is required and never the reverse, and a signature with none is usable nowhere a this is required at all (1.9.2.2). A method extracted from its class and called free of it is the case this decides: its this is not of the type its body assumes, and the extraction is a type error at the boundary that took it rather than a TypeError inside it.

    Where a non-arrow function literal's contextual type (Table 7) is a function type whose applicable signature has a [[ThisType]], the literal adopts it: this within the body has that type, and the literal's own signature has that [[ThisType]]. An arrow adopts nothing, since it has no this of its own to give a type to, and the this it closes over is already typed where it was written.

    Note

    This is the whole of what an erased system's ThisType<T> marker does, said as data rather than as a marker the checker recognizes by name. It is also why the design's options(Data, Methods) builder is three lines: it constructs an intersection of the data and the methods, and calls withThisType to put it on every method's signature, at which point a method written in the object literal has this typed as the finished object. Nothing about that reaches into inference; the type is built first and the literal is checked against it, which is the order 1.2.5 requires of everything.

    8.2 Declared Narrowing

    A Narrowing Record has a [[Target]], a String naming a parameter of the signature or "this", and a [[Type]], a Type Record. A signature's [[Narrows]] is a List of them, and it says what a call establishes: that the argument passed at [[Target]] is of [[Type]]. Two forms follow from the signature's [[Return]], and no third is defined.

    Where the [[Return]] is boolean, the call is a test and its narrowings apply where its result is true, exactly as v is T applies (Table 14), so if (isFish(pet)) narrows pet in the branch it guards and narrows it away in the other. Where the [[Return]] is void, the call establishes its narrowings by returning at all, since there is no result to condition on, so they apply in every position the call dominates. Where the [[Return]] is any other type, a non-empty [[Narrows]] is a type error: nothing would consume it.

    A narrowing declared by a signature is checked against nothing. It is a claim the declaration makes and the body is not required to honour, in the way that v is T is a question the language answers. This is the same reading a declared type of an unchecked boundary gets, and the same one an erased system's type predicate gets; what makes it sound here is that a value crossing a boundary is checked against its declared type regardless (Table 13), so a narrowing that lied costs a check that fails rather than a value that was never what it claimed.

    Note

    The two forms are the type predicate and the assertion function of an erased system, and they are one field here rather than two spellings, because the difference between them is not in the fact declared but in what the declaration returns. A function that answers a question returns the answer, and its narrowing applies where the answer was yes; a function that establishes a fact returns nothing, and its narrowing applies once it has returned. Deriving the two from [[Return]] is why no asserts operator appears here.

    Neither [[ThisType]] nor [[Narrows]] has a source spelling. A signature acquires them by construction (1.11.4), which is how the design's withThisType writes one, and a program uses the result by annotating with it: a binding declared of a constructed guard type narrows at every call through it. They join the index signature and the symbol literal type as types this specification admits and no declaration writes (1.11.4), and giving them one is a question for the syntax the design has not yet posed, not a gap in what they mean.

    9 Memory Layout

    A value type has a defined layout in memory, and this clause defines it. It is what makes a [N].<T> a contiguous buffer rather than a list of references, what gives 1.7.3's zero-fill something to fill, and what a serializer, a placement construction over existing bytes, or a vertex descriptor reads. The default is the rule C, C++, and Rust follow, so a value type class is laid out as the same declaration is in those languages, and the controls below are their repr attributes together with the field offsets those languages keep to themselves.

    9.1 Layout Properties

    A Type Object whose type has a layout has three own properties. bitLength is the size in bits, which is what an arbitrary-width integer needs in order to describe itself; byteLength is the laid-out size in bytes, the bits rounded up to a byte and then to the type's alignment, so that every element of an array of the type is aligned; and alignment is the byte alignment.
