Each has the attributes { [[Writable]]: false, [[Enumerable]]: false, [[Configurable]]: false }, and each is compile-time evaluable (1.2.5), so [Vertex.byteLength * 1024].<uint8> is an array extent and T.byteLength inside a generic is a constant once T is specialized.

    A Type Object whose type has no layout has none of the three properties, so reading one is undefined and calling it a size is a TypeError rather than a number that hides the mistake.

    Note

    These are properties of an interned Type Object rather than a sizeof operator, which is why they need no grammar and work wherever a type does, including on the result of Reflect.typeOf. The layout they report is the declared one, so the controls of 9.3 are accounted for in it: byteLength is what the program asked for, not what an implementation would have chosen.

    The alignment of a named numeric type is its byteLength. For an int.<N> or uint.<N> of a width no named type has, the alignment is the smallest power of two that is at least the bits rounded up to a byte, and at most 8; the design fixes only uint.<4>, whose byteLength is 1, and this rule agrees with it and with every named width. A width above 64 therefore aligns to 8 and pads to a multiple of it, which is the only reading on which an array of them stays aligned.

    9.2 Natural Alignment and Padding

    The layout of a value type class places its fields in declaration order. Let a bit cursor begin at 0, at the end of the layout of the class it extends where it extends one and at 0 otherwise. For each field in order:

    • Where the field's type has a bitLength under 8, it is a bit-field: it is placed at the cursor, or at the bit its offsetBit names, and the cursor advances by its bitLength.
    • Otherwise the cursor is first rounded up to a byte. The field is placed at the byte its offset names, where it has one; at the cursor, where the class is packed; and at the next multiple of its alignment otherwise, that alignment being the one its align names where it has one and its type's otherwise. The cursor advances to the end of the field.

    The class's alignment is the one its alignAll names, or 1 where it is packed, or the largest alignment among its fields otherwise. Its bitLength is the cursor's furthest extent, and its byteLength is the size its size names, or that extent rounded up to a byte and then to the class's alignment. It is a type error for a field to be placed outside the size a size fixes, or for an inherited field to be redeclared, since the redeclaration would have no defined offset.

    A field's offset is read through the reflection of its declaration rather than from the type, because it belongs to the field: Reflect.getReflection.<Reflect.ClassField, T>(name) reports an offset and a byteLength, compile-time evaluable for the reason the layout is (7). This is the offsetof a serializer, a placement construction, or a vertex attribute descriptor needs.

    Note

    Rounding a class's byteLength up to its alignment is what makes an array of it an array: without the trailing pad, the second element would be misaligned. class A { a: uint8; b: uint16; } is therefore 4 bytes and not 3, and [10].<A> is 40.

    Because layout follows declaration order and this specification never reorders, field order is a performance decision the program makes: { a: uint8, b: float64, c: uint8 } occupies 24 bytes where { b: float64, a: uint8, c: uint8 } occupies 16. Reordering would be sound for a program that only reads its own fields and wrong for every one that does not, and views, serialization, and interop are exactly the cases that do not. Since byteLength is a constant, a program that cares can assert the size it meant to have.

    Inheritance appends, so a base's fields keep their offsets and a subclass's follow under the same rule, and class B extends A is laid out as the flattening of both. That is what lets a B be passed where an A is expected without copying: the prefix is an A already.

    9.3 Explicit Control

    Seven reserved names control the layout above. Each is a property-descriptor key and each has a decorator of the same name that sets it, so a program may write either and a tool that generates classes need not write decorators.

    These apply only where every field of the class and of its whole prototype chain is typed, since a class with an untyped field has no fixed layout to place a field within. A dynamic class may gain a typed field later through Object.defineProperty with a type key, and that field is part of no layout; on any other class such a call is a TypeError, because the layout closed at the declaration.

    Note

    An align replaces its field's alignment rather than strengthening it, which is the design's own reading: a float32x4 at @align(4) follows a float32 at byte 2 at byte 8, not at byte 16. That is what makes the control useful in both directions, and it is the program's to get right, since an implementation may hold a field's natural alignment to be a precondition of the instruction it would otherwise use.

    Two fields given one offset occupy one memory, which is the C union, and a negative offset overlaps a base's. That is deliberate reinterpretation, for reading a float32's bits as a uint32, and it is not how a program should write a tagged union: a sealed abstract class hierarchy is that, and the language checks it for exhaustiveness (1.10.5).

    Reading or writing a bit-field is a shift and a mask, and taking a reference to one is a type error, since it has no byte address to refer to. A bit-field is also not a SIMD lane mask: boolean8 and its family are one bit per lane and belong to the vector types, where a bit-field is a sub-byte integer member of a class.

    Automatic packing reaches only a field under 8 bits, so a uint.<12> occupies 2 bytes unless an offsetBit places it, which is worth knowing for a wire format whose fields are 12 bits wide. The design states the rule for the sub-byte case and leaves the wider one to the explicit control, and whether automatic packing should follow bitLength rather than a byte is a question it should answer.

    9.4 Finiteness

    The layout of a value type class is defined exactly where the rule of 1.10.2 holds, that a value type class may not contain itself, directly or through a cycle of value type fields. The two are one condition. Computing a layout recurses from a class into the type of each of its fields; the recursion terminates exactly where that field relation is acyclic; and every field contributes a size of at least zero and at least one field of a cycle contributes more, so a cycle admits no finite fixpoint and every acyclic graph reaches one in as many steps as it has edges. The rule is therefore not a restriction placed on top of the layout but a statement of when the layout exists, and the diagnostic a program gets names the cycle rather than a size that could not be computed.

    A field written T | null closes a cycle because it is a reference, which Table 29 gives no layout and whose width is the implementation's; the recursion stops there rather than descending, which is why a linked list is expressible and a class containing itself by value is not.

    10 Checked Contracts

    A function declaration may carry where clauses, the WhereClauses of 5.1, between its return annotation and its body. On an ordinary function they are the compile-time bound over its generic parameters that 4.3 describes. On a compile-time-evaluable function they are more, because such a function returns a type and a predicate over that type is a checked contract: a fact about the type the builder returns, stated at the declaration and checked at every evaluation.

    The production below restates the one 1.3.4 gives, with WhereClauses added (1.1).

    FunctionDeclaration[Yield, Await, Default] : function BindingIdentifier[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FormalParameters[~Yield, ~Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt WhereClauses[?Yield, ?Await]opt { FunctionBody[~Yield, ~Await] } [+Default] function TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FormalParameters[~Yield, ~Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt WhereClauses[?Yield, ?Await]opt { FunctionBody[~Yield, ~Await] }

    Within a WhereClause of a function declaration, return is a PrimaryExpression denoting the value the function returns. It is a Syntax Error for return to occur as an expression anywhere else, which costs nothing, since the token is ungrammatical in expression position today and no program can be using it.

    A contract is read two ways, and both are load-bearing. It is verified: at every concrete evaluation of the builder, once 1.2.5.3 has a result, each clause is evaluated with return bound to it, and a clause that is falsy is a type error naming the builder, the arguments it was given, and the clause. A contract is never trusted. It is assumed: before specialization, where the application is deferred and no result exists, the checker takes each clause as a known fact about the application Type Record. The second is sound because of the first: any specialization that would falsify an assumption is stopped at the builder, before the code that relied on it runs.

    A generic declaration may carry an exemplars decorator naming types, which forces specialization of the declaration at those arguments during compile-time evaluation, so that a builder's contracts are evaluated where the declaration is rather than only where a caller reaches it.

    Note

    This is the trade C++ concepts make, recovered inside a specialization model: the body of a generic is checked against declared bounds at its declaration, and per-instantiation verification is the backstop that makes the bounds safe to believe. function sanitize<T>(value: T): omit(T, ['password']) checks at its declaration because omit's contract says Reflect.isAssignable(T, return), and { ...value } is a T.

    Direction is the thing to get right and is easy to invert. An upper bound, that the result is assignable to something, serves a consumer holding the result. Checking a body that produces the result needs a lower bound, and for omit the true one is that T is assignable to the result, since removing properties yields a supertype. A contract stating the reverse would be both true of nothing and useless to the body.

    Two limits are worth stating rather than discovering. A contract is a proposition about one evaluation, so universally quantified knowledge, the variance of omit(T, K) in T or any parametricity fact, is not expressible as one and stays unavailable before specialization; after specialization the types are simply evaluated and compared, so nothing is lost where a program actually needs it. And a contract does not check the builder's body ahead of any evaluation, which is what the exemplars decorator is for: it turns testing a library's generics from advice into an annotation the checker reads. Beneath contracts sits the cast, which is legal with a deferred application as its target and resolves at specialization into the ordinary checked conversion of Table 13; that is the honest division, with contracts stating what an author can promise and a checked cast bounding what an author merely asserts.

    11 Inference Through Computed Positions

    Inference reads plain parameter positions (1.3.3.1): a parameter under a deferred application is opaque, because binding it from the application's result would require running the builder backwards, and a builder is an opaque function. Where a computed type sits in a return position nothing is needed, since the argument infers plainly and the result is a function of it. This clause is the remainder, for a parameter that occurs only in computed positions, and it adds two rules that propose a binding and verify it forward. Neither runs a builder backwards and neither admits anything forward checking would not.

    11.1 Literal Freshness

    An object literal checked against an expected object type is checked freshly: an own property the expected type neither declares nor admits through an index signature is a type error, reported against the property. Freshness is a property of the literal and not of its type, so it is lost the moment the value is bound to a name and read back: f({ a: 1, b: 2 }) is checked freshly against f's parameter, and f(o) is not.

    Note

    Without this an all-optional shape is a supertype of nearly everything, and width subtyping admits any literal against it, which is correct for a value that reached the position through a binding and useless for one written at the position. A value type class already imposes the rule, since a literal converted to one fills a layout that has no room for a property it did not declare (9); freshness is that rule stated for the structural case, and it is what makes the trials below discriminate at all.

    11.2 Trial Specialization

    A generic parameter that ordinary inference leaves unbound and whose constraint denotes a closed candidate set is inferred by trial. A constraint denotes one when it is a union of types, an enum of types, or a sealed class, whose candidates are the members in declaration order, the enumerators in declaration order, and the class with its direct subclasses transitively, the set that is fixed once the declaring module finishes evaluating (1.10.5). Any other constraint denotes none, and a parameter so constrained does not trial.

    A parameter is trialed against exactly the arguments whose declared types mention it and no other unbound parameter; an argument whose type mentions two or more is not separable and takes part in no trial. For each candidate in order, that candidate is substituted for the parameter throughout those arguments' declared types (4.2), which evaluates each deferred application under the memo already specified, and each argument is checked against the result, freshly where it is a literal. A candidate every argument satisfies survives. Exactly one survivor binds the parameter; zero or more than one is a type error asking for explicit arguments and naming the constraint.

    Trials are counted against the ceiling of 1.2.5.4, and the count is a sum over the parameters trialed rather than a product across them, since each is trialed alone. Exceeding it is a type error asking for explicit arguments, and because the ceiling is fixed rather than host-tunable, whether a program's inference succeeds is a fact about the program.

    Note

    This is overload resolution's logic pointed at specialization: enumerate, evaluate forward, keep what checks. It terminates in as many memoized evaluations as the constraint has members and is sound because nothing is accepted that forward checking did not accept. apply({ kind: 'circle', radius: 3 }) against apply<T: Shape>(patch: partial(T)) trials Circle, whose partial the literal satisfies, and Rect, whose kind it contradicts, and infers Circle because exactly one survived.

    Separability is a determinism rule before it is a cost rule. merge<A: Shape, B: Shape>(a: partial(A), b: partial(B)) runs the members of Shape for A and again for B, never their product, and a site whose argument mentions both, as pair(A, B) does, asks for explicit arguments rather than searching a space whose size is the product of two constraints.

    11.3 Declared Inverses

    A compile-time-evaluable function may carry an inverse decorator naming another, which the checker consults for a parameter that ordinary inference and trial specialization both leave unbound. The declared inverse is applied once per site, memoized and counted against the budget, to the type of the argument whose computed type mentions the parameter, and what it returns is a proposal: for a builder with one inferable slot, a type; for one with several, a record keyed by parameter name, whose proposals are verified together.

    A proposal binds nothing until it is verified. It is substituted for the parameter, the deferred applications are evaluated forward, and the arguments are checked against the results, which is the check an explicitly specialized call would face and is performed by the same operations. Where it holds, the proposal binds. Where it does not, the parameter stays unbound and the site is a type error asking for explicit arguments. A declared inverse that does not invert can therefore only make inference fail, never make it succeed wrongly.

    Note

    The association lives on the declaration because that is the only place with one possible author and no timeline: an imperative registration would make inference depend on module evaluation order and let unrelated modules contend for one builder's meaning, and a decorator has neither failure mode and is read where the checker is already reading. The proposal is what user code contributes, and it contributes it as an oracle whose answer is checked, which is why letting user code propose here does not reopen the boundary 1.2.5 draws.

    This rung is last on purpose. The first two need nothing from an author: a builder in a return position infers plainly, and a closed constraint trials. An inverse is for the remainder, and it should be adopted when porting demonstrates the need.

    12 Operator Declarations

    An operator is declared in one of three places, and they differ only in what the receiver is: on a class, where the receiver is the left operand; on a class reopened elsewhere, by the extension form, which appends operators to a type declared in another module; and in a primitive block (6.5), where the receiver is the primitive. This clause gives the form; 1.8.7 gives what an operator declaration means for the operators of the language, and 6.5 what it means for metadata.

    PrimitiveOperatorDeclaration[Yield, Await] : primitive [no LineTerminator here] TypeName[?Yield, ?Await] TypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt { OperatorDefinitionList[?Yield, ?Await]opt } OperatorDefinitionList[Yield, Await] : OperatorDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] OperatorDefinitionList[?Yield, ?Await] OperatorDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] OperatorDefinition[Yield, Await] : staticopt operator OperatorName OperatorTypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FormalParameters[~Yield, ~Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt { FunctionBody[~Yield, ~Await] } staticopt operator OperatorName OperatorTypeParameters[?Yield, ?Await]opt ( FormalParameters[~Yield, ~Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt ; operator Type[?Yield, ?Await] ( ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt { FunctionBody[~Yield, ~Await] } * operator ... ( ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt { GeneratorBody } * operator ... ( ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt ; OperatorTypeParameters[Yield, Await] : .< TypeParameterList[?Yield, ?Await] > OperatorName :: one of + - * / % ** == < > <= >= & | ^ ~ << >> >>>

    A PrimitiveOperatorDeclaration is a Declaration; 6.1 restates that production with it added. An OperatorDefinition is a ClassElement, restated below, and an interface member, so a class declares operators among its methods and an interface declares the operators a type must have, which is what makes an operator expressible as a constraint. The form without a body is the declaration-only one an interface and a metadata-only definition take. The cast production is the implicit cast operator of 6.5.

    The ... productions are the typed iteration protocol: a generator operator whose declared return type types the elements it yields, and whose declaration defines the class's Symbol.iterator method. Like any operator it may be overloaded on its return type, and untyped iteration uses the first declared overload. It yields values rather than references.

    ClassElement[Yield, Await] : MethodDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] static MethodDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] FieldDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] ; static FieldDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] ; ClassStaticBlock ; OperatorDefinition[?Yield, ?Await] abstract ClassElementName[?Yield, ?Await] ( UniqueFormalParameters[?Yield, ?Await] ) TypeAnnotation[?Yield, ?Await]opt ;

    The abstract alternative is the abstract method of 1.10.3, restated here because a later restatement carries a production's complete form (1.1).

    An operator's own type parameters are written .<…> rather than <…> because the operator name may itself end in < or >: operator<.<D2: Dimensions> lexes where operator< <D2: Dimensions> would collide with the << token. This is the one place a declaration site uses the application spelling, and the reason is lexical rather than principled.

    Resolution reads the left operand's type alone. For a * b, the operators of a's type are searched for a signature whose parameter accepts b, and the operands are never reversed to find a match. Return type overloading applies as it does to any function (1.9.5), and two applicable definitions with the same parameter types and no expected return type is an ambiguity and a type error. A primitive block adds to a global operator table; two definitions for one pair of types is a type error at the second declaration. A static operator defines an operator on the class object rather than on its instances, so that A += 5 may mean something, and takes no part in any of the above.

    Note

    Never reversing the operands is the rule that makes Matrix4 * Vector4 and Vector4 * Matrix4 different operations, which in a column-major library they are: one transforms a column vector and the other a row vector. A language that reverses operands to find any match makes that bug unfindable. It is also why 2 * v needs a primitive block rather than falling back to v's operators: the left operand is a number, and the declaration belongs where the library author can write it.

    The block is made generic by an interface constraint rather than written once per pair, so primitive float32 { operator*.<T extends ScalarMultiply.<float32, T>>(rhs: T): T } covers every type that defines scalar multiplication, including ones declared later. 2 * v reaches it because viability filtering precedes ranking (1.9.3.1): a Vector4 satisfies ScalarMultiply.<float32, Vector4> and not the number instantiation, so the number candidate is eliminated before the number-first order could prefer it. Division does not commute, so no such block exists for it and 2 / v stays a type error, which is correct, since it is not defined.

    A global table is why an operator-bearing type is a nominal class and not an alias. Were Vector4 an alias for float32x4, its operators would land on float32x4 itself and any other library aliasing that lane type would collide on every shared pair. Two libraries with two nominal Vector4 classes coexist, because the parameter types differ. The division the design draws is to alias for storage and accessors and to declare a class where the type carries operators.

    13 Vector Comparisons

    A comparison between two vectors of one shape yields one lane per input lane, and what it yields for each is decided by the expected type, since the comparison is overloaded on its return type (1.9.5). Three results are defined: the wide mask, a vector of the boolean type of the same width as the compared element, so a float32x4 comparison yields a boolean32x4 of lanes all-set or all-clear; the compact mask, a bit vector of one bit per lane; and the compared vector type itself, its matching lanes all-ones. Left with no expected type the expression is ambiguous among them and is a type error, so the result's type is written.

    The lane counts the shorthand names cover are the register widths, which is why float32x4 has a name and a three-lane float vector does not. A vector of any other lane count is written vector.<T, N>, which is what a narrowing accessor produces, and a library wanting a name for one declares it.

    Note

    The three forms are three instructions, which is the point of the overloading rather than a consequence of it: the wide mask is what a comparison natively produces on SSE and NEON, the compact mask is an AVX-512 mask register, and the all-ones vector is what a program blends with. Choosing among them by the annotation lets one spelling reach each without an intrinsic per form, and makes the ambiguity rule load-bearing rather than pedantic: an unannotated a < b has no defensible default among three instructions, and a coin flip here is a performance cliff a program cannot see.

    The bit vectors are the one place a lane and a bit coincide: lane i of a boolean8 is bit i counting from the least significant, which is how movmskps and vpmovmskb number their results, and the conversion to and from an integer is that one rule read in each direction. They are not the bit-fields of 9, which are sub-byte integer members of a class.

    14 Complex Numbers

    complex.<T> is a value type holding a real and an imaginary component of the real numeric type T. The width-named shorthands count total bits, following the convention of the numeric libraries the design cites, so a complex64 holds two float32 components; the bare name complex is complex.<number>.

    An imaginary literal (1.3.1.3) has the type complex, with the literal's value as its imaginary component and zero as its real one, so 4i is complex(0, 4). Literal propagation applies as it does to any numeric literal (1.4.12), so an imaginary literal in a complex64 position is a complex64. complex64 and complex128 convert to and from complex explicitly and not implicitly, exactly as float32 and float64 convert to and from number, and the treatment of a value outside the component type's range is Table 12's as it is for that component.

    Note

    Bare complex has number components for the reason the language keeps number and float64 distinct: number is what an untyped literal and every Math function produce, so a complex built from Math.PI composes without a cast, and the fixed widths exist for an interleaved buffer and for interop. Being a value type, a complex copies on assignment and lies inline, so a [].<complex128> is the interleaved buffer an FFT expects and 9 gives it the layout to be one.

    15 Ranges

    A range is a value type class over an ordered element type and an interval, Range.<T, I>, whose four intervals are the closed, half-open, and open forms an omitted or included endpoint gives. It is a value, so it allocates nothing and copies; and it is an expression, so it appears wherever one does, including a case label and an argument list, without further rule.

    15.1 Range Literals

    A range is written with the .. operator. The two literal forms a..b and a..=b produce the half-open and the inclusive interval, and the open-ended forms a.., ..b, ..=b, and .. omit an endpoint. The operator collides with the numeric literal grammar, since 1. is already a complete numeric literal, and the collision is resolved by one lexical rule: a . is not the decimal point of a numeric literal when the character after it is also a .. So 1..6 is the three tokens 1, .., 6 rather than 1. then .6, while 1., 1.5, and the spread ... are unchanged. The one construction this rule changes is an integer literal followed by .. and a member name, the 1..toString() idiom, which becomes a range whose end is the result of a call rather than a member access on a number, and which fails loudly rather than silently.

    A range binds tighter than assignment and looser than || and ??, so its operands are ShortCircuitExpressions, and it is non-associative: a..b..c is a Syntax Error rather than a nesting. Member access binds tighter than the range, so 0..10.length is 0..(10.length) and a range reaches its own members through parentheses, as in (0..10).length. Because a range is an expression it appears in a case label, an object literal, and an argument list with no further rule, and a statement that begins with .. continues the preceding expression, joining the other leading-operator forms on the list of automatic-semicolon hazards.

    The value carries its start, its end, and its interval. Its start and end are the endpoints, absent for an omitted one; contains tests a value against the interval; length is the count of the members of a bounded integer range and isEmpty is whether it has none, with a descending range empty rather than reversed. A range iterates through its members by the rule of 15: over a bounded integer interval the step is 1 and implicit, and step supplies a step where the interval is not one an implicit step fits or widens the stride where it is. A range with no start is not iterable, and a range whose endpoints are not a whole integer interval is a TypeError to iterate without an explicit step, rather than a silent choice of 1.

    Note

    The lexical rule is the whole of what the operator costs the grammar, and it is the trade Rust made in requiring 1.0 rather than 1. so that .. could be a range. The alternatives were weighed and set aside: ... reads as spread to every reader and Ruby's ../... pairing is a known hazard, ..< cannot be used because .< already opens a type argument list, and a contextual keyword reads poorly in an index position. That the half-open form is the default is what makes for (const i of 0..array.length) the loop written without thinking rather than one short.

    A case whose label is a range matches where the range contains the discriminant, which is what a switch over a float discriminant needs, since a float has no enumerable cases to list. Matching is by containment rather than by ===, and the cases of such a switch are tested in source order, so overlapping ranges resolve to the first that contains the value rather than being an error.

    A range iterates where it has a step, which over an integer element type is 1 and implicit. The counter of a for over a range has the range's element type refined by the range's interval (6), so for (const i of 0..N) binds i at a type bounded below by 0 and above by N minus 1. Where N is a compile-time constant that refinement is one too, and an index of a [N].<T> by that counter needs no check, which is the case 1.6.5 names.

    Note

    The bound is the range's own interval, so nothing about the elision is special to a for: the counter's type carries the fact, and the ordinary subtyping of a bounded parameterization discharges the check the way it discharges any other. That the half-open form is the default is what makes for (const i of 0..array.length) right rather than off by one, and it is why the interval is part of the type rather than a convention a reader has to remember.

    The nth value of a stepped range is the start plus n times the step rather than the previous value plus the step, and iteration stops against the value rather than a computed count. Both halves matter over a float element type, where a computed count can round up and yield one value too many and repeated addition accumulates error; specifying the arithmetic rather than the loop is what makes a float range give the same values on every implementation.

    16 Structural Matching

    Reflect.inferSlot(name) returns a fresh slot, a Type Object standing for a position a match is to bind. Reflect.matchType(pattern, subject) performs one-sided structural unification of pattern against subject: where every slot in pattern can be bound consistently so that the pattern becomes subject, it returns an object mapping each slot's name to the Type Object bound there, and otherwise it returns null. A slot occurring twice binds once and must bind alike. A constraint on a binding is an ordinary check of the result afterwards, so Reflect.isAssignable supplies what an infer S extends string says.

    A pattern is structural: it is a node, and its slots stand where a Type Object would. A slot inside an application Type Record is a type error at the match rather than null, because evaluating a builder against an unbound slot is exactly the inversion no operation of this specification performs (11). A pattern for the result of a builder is therefore written against the shape the builder produces, an optional property or a wrapped element, and never against the application.

    Note

    This closes the question of whether anything an erased system's infer does is out of reach, and the answer is that it is not. It is deliberately no more than that: the design's own catalog needed this operation exactly zero times, which is the measurement that makes it optional rather than load-bearing, and a library author who wants an explicit inverse now has one that is ordinary code (11.3 is where the checker will consult it).

    17 Provenance

    A Property Type Record and a Type Record may carry an [[Origin]], a List of references to the declaration sites the record came from. It is not part of any type's identity: SameType does not read it, IsSubtype does not read it, and CanonicalizeType neither reads it nor writes it into the comparison that decides whether two Records are one. What canonicalization does with it is union it: where two Records that differ only in [[Origin]] are merged by interning, the merged Record's [[Origin]] is the union of theirs, ordered by 1.7.5.1. It is a HOST-FACING channel: an implementation makes it available to a tool, and no program reads it. That is a deliberate restriction rather than an omission. Because canonicalization unions origins, a reflected origin would change what a program observes about its own type when an unrelated module declared a structurally identical shape, which is a surprise with no upside for the program and none for the tool, since a tool already has the whole program in view. A builder that spreads a property record therefore spreads no origin, and one that mints a record has none to spread.

    A record a builder mints carries no [[Origin]], which is the correct answer rather than a missing one: nothing declared it. An implementation is likewise free to hold an origin as an opaque handle, a source and a position, rather than as a reference to the declaration itself, and is encouraged to: retaining the declaration would keep a parse tree alive for as long as the type is interned, which in an agent that does not exit is a leak and not a cost. Documentation is not carried here and is not carried in the node model at all. It stays with the source, and a tool resolves it from an origin at the moment it displays something, falling back for a minted record on the application that produced it, which is the expression a diagnostic already names.

    Note

    The reason to want this is that partial(User) should not cost an editor its memory of where name came from. The reason not to put a documentation string in the node instead is worth stating, because it is the same argument this specification makes twice already: anything inside a node rides the round trip through 1.11.4, so a doc string either joins the canonical form, and two structurally identical types with different comments become different types, or interning crowns one winner and the rest is lost silently and by load order. Provenance escapes both because it is a reference rather than a payload and because it unions rather than wins.

    That union is also why [[Origin]] does not reopen what 1.9.1 forecloses. The rule there is not that no second non-canonical field may exist; it is that no field may depend on the order modules loaded. A [[Name]] is first-won and so does depend on it, which is tolerated because a parameter has one name and there is nothing else to do. An [[Origin]] accumulates every site and is ordered canonically, so the same program produces the same origins in the same order however its modules were reached, and the accident the rule exists to prevent does not arise.

    18 The Expansion Artifact

    Because evaluation is deterministic and its results are interned canonical forms, they may be computed once and shipped. An expansion artifact is a serialization of the interning table for the concrete types a module graph's public surface produces, every closed application among them pre-evaluated, keyed by a hash of the module graph that produced it. A consumer verifies the hash and reads the types out of it rather than evaluating them; on a mismatch it evaluates, and determinism is what makes the two agree.

    An artifact contains no open application. partial(T) inside an exported generic evaluates against the consumer's T, at the consumer, so the evaluator is not removed by the artifact and cannot be: what the artifact removes is the common path, a dependency's concrete surface, which is the part that would otherwise be recomputed by every tool on every build. An artifact is an optimization and never a semantics: a consumer that ignores every artifact computes the same types, and one that reads a stale artifact detects it by the hash rather than by disagreeing.

    Note

    This is what makes the standalone conformance of A pay. A tool that reads an artifact is trusting that whoever produced it computed what this specification says, and the conformance suite is what makes that claim checkable rather than hopeful; the hash is what makes the trust revocable. The artifact also carries the extracted documentation for the origins of 17, since a dependency's sources are exactly what a consumer does not have on disk, which is the one thing provenance alone cannot supply.

    The format is not fixed here. What is fixed is the contract: the key is a hash of the module graph, the contents are canonical forms, a mismatch re-derives, and nothing in it can change what a program means.

    19 Types Across Agents

    The threading extension gives a program more than one executing thread over one heap. There is no copying step between them: a value is shared by being referenced, and no structured clone, no message, and no byte buffer stands in between. The threads of such a program are agents of one agent cluster that share a heap rather than only a block of memory, and the intern table of 1.2.2.6 is a property of that heap. They therefore share it. A type constructed on one thread is the same Type Object on every other, === answers on any thread what it answers on the thread that built it, and nothing about interning, canonicalization, or the total order is per-thread. This is what that clause's note defers to, and the answer is that the extension does not carry a Type Object across a boundary; it removes the boundary.

    An agent that does not share a heap is the other case, and a Type Object does not reach it. A value that crosses to one crosses as data, and the receiving agent types that data by its own declarations and interns the result in its own table. Two structurally identical declarations in two such agents are two Type Objects and are not ===, which is correct rather than a defect: identity is what interning within one heap establishes, and there is no third table above them in which to establish more. A program that needs the same type on both sides declares it on both sides, and every structural rule of this specification then agrees about it, because structure is what those rules read.

    Two consequences follow for the types whose identity rests on something an agent holds. A symbol literal type (1.4.12) over a registered Symbol has a counterpart in another agent, because the registry is shared and the key is what identifies it; over an unregistered Symbol it has none, and the type does not travel, because the Symbol does not. The canonical order of symbol values is by first interning within an agent (1.7.5.1), which is a per-heap fact for the same reason the table is, and two agents may order the same two symbols differently without either being wrong.

    A value type is copied by assignment (1.10.2), and that copy is not atomic. A value wider than a single primitive, or an update spanning several fields, may be observed torn or half-applied by a thread that races it. Atomicity is obtained only through Atomics operations, whose compareExchange compares with SameValueZero so that a loop cycling through NaN terminates; there is no rule making some assignments atomic and others not, because leaving that set undefined would make a racing program unspecifiable and would tax every shared write. The shared modifier marks the state a program expects to cross threads, which is what lets the boundary be reasoned about statically rather than discovered.

    Note

    The proposal makes no claim to prove races away. A race here is a stale value and never a corrupted heap, which is the position Java and Go take rather than Rust's; what the types offer in place of a proof is the discipline that shared marks what crosses and that mutable shared state is reached through an atomic or a lock. Retrofitting a Send/Sync proof onto a shared mutable heap without a borrow checker would make this a different language, and the memory safety the core guarantees is unconditional either way.

    That tearing is possible is worth stating next to the value type rules rather than only here, since it is the one place the core's "a value type copies" meets a second thread. It is also why the layout extension's guarantees are the ones a concurrent program leans on: a shared SoA.<T> allocates each field its own column, so two threads writing two fields of one element never contend for a cache line, which no interleaved layout can promise.

    20 Typed JSON Parsing

    JSON.parse gains a generic overload, written JSON.parse.<_T_>(_text_), that parses and validates in one pass. It parses text as JSON, which is a SyntaxError on malformed input exactly as the untyped JSON.parse is, and then converts the resulting value against T by 20.1. Well-formed JSON that does not satisfy T is a TypeError naming the path at which the mismatch occurred, so a typed catch (_e_: SyntaxError) and catch (_e_: TypeError) separate a malformed document from a well-formed one of the wrong shape.

    Validation is layered as any other boundary in this specification: a leaf is checked against its type as it is reached, a parameterized type consults its metadata's validation judgment (1.6.2.1), and a class or record where clause runs once the target is filled, as the construction boundary check. Unlike the general boundary of 1.6.3.1, which distinguishes a RangeError for a numeric value the target cannot represent, every failure here is a TypeError: in a parsed document the type is the schema, so a value outside a field's range violates the schema rather than overflowing an arithmetic. The overload takes no reviver, since a reviver observes the intermediate untyped graph this feature exists not to build.

    20.1 CoerceJSONValue ( value, type, path )

    The abstract operation CoerceJSONValue takes arguments value (an ECMAScript language value), type (a Type Record), and path (a String) and returns either a normal completion containing an ECMAScript language value or a throw completion. It converts the JSON value into type, or returns a throw completion whose value is a TypeError describing path. It may call user code, since converting a structural type reads and writes properties. It performs the following steps when called:

    1. If type.[[Kind]] is any, return value.
    2. If type.[[Kind]] is reference, return ? CoerceJSONValue(value, type.[[Target]], path).
    3. If type.[[Kind]] is primitive, then
      1. If type is a numeric type, then
        1. If value is not a Number, throw a TypeError exception.
        2. If type is a sized integer type and the mathematical value of value is not an integer in the range of type, throw a TypeError exception.
        3. If type is the number type, return value.
        4. Return value converted to type by Table 9.
      2. If type is the string type, then
        1. If value is not a String, throw a TypeError exception.
        2. Return value.
      3. If type is the boolean type, then
        1. If value is not a Boolean, throw a TypeError exception.
        2. Return value.
      4. Throw a TypeError exception.
    4. If type.[[Kind]] is literal, then
      1. If SameValue(value, type.[[Value]]) is true, return value.
      2. Throw a TypeError exception.
    5. If type.[[Kind]] is union, then
      1. For each element m of type.[[Members]], do
        1. Let r be Completion(CoerceJSONValue(value, m, path)).
        2. If r is a normal completion, return r.[[Value]].
      2. Throw a TypeError exception.
    6. If type.[[Kind]] is array, then
      1. If value is not an Array, throw a TypeError exception.
      2. Let len be ? LengthOfArrayLike(value).
      3. If type.[[Extent]] is not dynamic and type.[[Extent]] ≠ len, throw a TypeError exception.
      4. Let elements be a new empty List.
      5. Let i be 0.
      6. Repeat, while i < len,
        1. Let element be the element of value at index i.
        2. Let elementPath be the string-concatenation of path, the code unit 0x005B (LEFT SQUARE BRACKET), the decimal representation of i as a String, and the code unit 0x005D (RIGHT SQUARE BRACKET).
        3. Let converted be ? CoerceJSONValue(element, type.[[Element]], elementPath).
        4. Append converted to elements.
        5. Set i to i + 1.
      7. Return CreateArrayFromList(elements).
    7. If type.[[Kind]] is object, then
      1. If value is not an Object or value is an Array, throw a TypeError exception.
      2. Let result be OrdinaryObjectCreate(%Object.prototype%).
      3. Let keys be ? value.[[OwnPropertyKeys]]().
      4. For each element k of keys such that k is a String and no Property Type Record of type.[[Properties]] has key k, do
        1. Let accepted be false.
        2. For each index signature s of type, do
          1. If accepted is false and s accepts k, then
            1. Let kv be ? Get(value, k).
            2. Let keyPath be the string-concatenation of path, the code unit 0x002E (FULL STOP), and k.
            3. Let converted be ? CoerceJSONValue(kv, s's value type, keyPath).
            4. Perform ! CreateDataPropertyOrThrow(result, k, converted).
            5. Set accepted to true.
        3. If accepted is false, throw a TypeError exception.
      5. For each Property Type Record p of type.[[Properties]], do
        1. Let present be ? HasProperty(value, p's key).
        2. If present is true, then
          1. Let pv be ? Get(value, p's key).
          2. Let fieldPath be the string-concatenation of path, the code unit 0x002E (FULL STOP), and p's key.
          3. Let converted be ? CoerceJSONValue(pv, p's type, fieldPath).
          4. Perform ! CreateDataPropertyOrThrow(result, p's key, converted).
        3. Else if p is optional, then
          1. Let d be the default value of p's type, if one is defined; otherwise empty.
          2. If d is not empty, perform ! CreateDataPropertyOrThrow(result, p's key, d).
        4. Else,
          1. Throw a TypeError exception.
      6. Return result.
    8. If type.[[Kind]] is parameterized, then
      1. Let base be ? CoerceJSONValue(value, type.[[Base]], path).
      2. If ? IsOfType(base, type) is false, throw a TypeError exception.
      3. Return base.
    9. If type.[[Kind]] is nominal, then
      1. If type is an enum type, then
        1. If value equals the value of an enumerator of type, return that enumerator.
        2. Throw a TypeError exception.
      2. If type has a structural form, return ? CoerceJSONValue(value, that form, path).
    10. Throw a TypeError exception.
         Note

    The physical form the extension describes, writing straight into contiguous typed storage with no intermediate graph, is an implementation strategy and not observable, so an implementation that parses to the untyped JSON value and then converts it conforms.

    The conversions this operation does not perform are the deferred part of the extension: a class target that fills a sealed layout without running its constructor, the exact wide numeric types (the 64-bit integers, decimal128, and bigint) whose digits must convert without first rounding through a Number, and the enforcement of the built-in refinement judgments, which parameterized validation consults but which are themselves specified with the primitive metadata they belong to.

    Annex A (normative) The Compile-Time Evaluable Fragment

    This annex fixes the compile-time-evaluable subset of the language as an implementation target in its own right, so that a tool that is not an ECMAScript engine can evaluate types and prove that it agrees with one. The determinism this specification already requires, one result or failure and never a different result (1.2.5.4), is what makes that agreement a checkable claim.

    A.1 The Grammar of the Fragment

    The fragment is the ECMAScript grammar less the following, together with the reading discipline of 1.2.5.1, which is what excludes input, output, and ambient state, since code in the fragment cannot name them.

    • AwaitExpression and YieldExpression, and every async and generator form of function, method, and arrow.
    • ClassDeclaration and ClassExpression: a builder transforms and constructs types but does not mint a nominal one (1.4.13).
    • A direct or indirect eval, the Function constructor, the construction of a Proxy, and a dynamic import(), which the library rule below also excludes.
    • The debugger statement.

    Everything else is within the fragment: functions and closures over evaluable bindings, local bindings and their mutation, the ordinary operators and control forms including try, subject to 1.2.5.4's carve-out, template literals, destructuring, spread, and regular expression literals.

    A.2 The Library Surface

    A built-in is within the fragment when its result is determined by its arguments, it performs no observable mutation outside values the call created, and it depends on no host or ambient state. The floor the fragment guarantees: the type operations of 1.11, Type Object identity and toString, regular expression construction and matching, Map and Set, whose keys Type Objects serve as by interned identity, Symbol and its registry, and the pure methods and functions of String, Number, BigInt, Math, Array, Object, and JSON. Symbol is within the fragment although a fresh symbol is not determined by its argument: a fresh symbol is a value the call creates, as a fresh array or Map is, and the determinism the fragment requires is of the types a program computes and the order it observes them in, both of which the same source fixes by creating the same symbols in the same sequence. Symbol.for is within it by the ordinary rule, its result determined by its key. Excluded by the same rule: Math.random, Date, every locale-sensitive method, Atomics and shared memory, and weak references and finalization. The conformance suite fixes the exact enumeration.

    A.3 Semantics and Conformance

    The fragment's semantics are this specification's own, restricted to the fragment; no second evaluator is defined. A standalone implementation of the fragment conforms when, for every program of the fragment, it produces, position for position, the same interned types this specification produces, or fails where this specification fails, under the budget invariant of 1.2.5.4 and with the memoization of 1.2.5.3 or anything observably equivalent to it.

    Note

    The subset is deliberately small as a language: a conforming standalone evaluator is a small tree-walking interpreter, not an embedded engine, and because evaluation is memoized, an interactive tool evaluates each distinct application once. Regular expressions are the one sizeable obligation, and the pattern metadata of 1.4.13 requires them anyway.

    A.4 The Conformance Suite and the Acceptance Corpus

    A conformance suite accompanies this annex, and conformance to the fragment is conformance to it together with this annex's text. Its seed is the design's acceptance corpus: one hundred and ninety programs, the whole of the type-challenges repository, the warm-up, thirteen easy, one hundred and four medium, fifty-five hard, and seventeen extreme, each pairing the erased-language solution with a builder solution whose assertions must hold under this specification's semantics. The assertions include type identity written ===, which interning supplies and which the erased corpus can only imitate through unspecified checker behaviour.

    Note

    The corpus is maintained with the design, and the symbol literal types its eleventh challenge exercises are now those of 1.4.12, so no challenge is gated on an unadopted extension. The questions and divergences the corpus surfaced are recorded in its own notes and tracked with the design documents.

    Annex B (informative) The Standard Builder Kit

    The primitives are normative and live in the core: construction, relations, never, keyof, and the evaluation semantics. The kit is the roughly two hundred lines of ordinary evaluable code written over them, and it ships as source under one standard module, spelled std:types here and reconciled with the standard library extension by observing that the two do not overlap: that extension gives typed signatures to methods that already exist and introduces no module, where the kit is new code and needs one. The kit is therefore the single new specifier, and a signature listing is never one until its final home is reconciled with the standard library extension. Shipping it as source is the proof that no construct in it is engine magic, gives the ecosystem one interned vocabulary, so that partial(User) in two packages is one type, and makes the definitions themselves the reference documentation. A codebase that cannot assume the module can polyfill it verbatim.

    The surface, by family: property mapping and the object utilities (mapProperties, mapPropertyTypes, partial, required, readonly, mutable, pick, omit, pickByValue, record, merge, renameProperties, propertyType, prop, objectOf); keys and indexing (keysOf, indexed, paths); unions (union, arms, exclude, extract, nonNullable, mapUnion, discriminants, byKind, handlers, removeKind); functions (fn, parameters, firstParameter, returnType, constructorParameters, thisParameterType, omitThisParameter, withThisType, instanceType); tuples and arrays (tupleOf, tupleElements, elementTypes, arrayOf, tail, reverse, zip, toArrayAll, toArrayEach, flatten); literals and strings (literal, literalValues, mapLiterals, uppercase, lowercase, capitalized, uncapitalized, suffixed, routeParams); recursion and composition (deepPartial, traverse, compose, awaited); and the additions of the design's maximal set (noInfer, brand, stringPattern, options).

    The style is visible in the definitions. The four property mappers are one line each, the union filters two, and the brand three:

    export function partial(T: type): type { return mapProperties(T, p => ({ ...p, optional: true })); }
export function required(T: type): type { return mapProperties(T, p => ({ ...p, optional: false })); }
export function readonly(T: type): type { return mapProperties(T, p => ({ ...p, readonly: true })); }
export function mutable(T: type): type { return mapProperties(T, p => ({ ...p, readonly: false })); }

export function exclude(T: type, U: type): type {
  return union(arms(T).filter(arm => !Reflect.isAssignable(arm, U)));
}
export function extract(T: type, U: type): type {
  return union(arms(T).filter(arm => Reflect.isAssignable(arm, U)));
}

export function brand(T: type, tag: string | symbol): type {
  return Reflect.makeType({ kind: 'parameterized', base: T, metadata: { brand: tag } });
}
    Note

    Where the kit and the core describe one operation, they must agree, and the corpus checks that they do: keysOf computes the function 1.3.2.3.1 specifies for keyof, and the corpus's library blocks assert each shipped helper against the exercise it solves. The kit's normative status is this annex's status: informative, with the primitives it is written over carrying the normativity.
